topics.title: Overview of Computational Methods in Biomedical Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT636 - COMPUTATIONAL METHODS IN BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Computational Methods in Biomedical Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Overview of Computational Methods in Biomedical Engineering

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biomedical Engineering (BME)** is the interdisciplinary branch of engineering that applies the principles, methods, and tools of engineering, computer science, and applied mathematics to understand, modify, or control biological systems, and to develop diagnostic, therapeutic, and rehabilitative devices and systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computational Methods in Biomedical Engineering** refers to the set of numerical, analytical, and algorithmic techniques used to model, simulate, analyse, and visualize biological and physiological systems. These methods translate real-world medical and biological problems into mathematical formulations that can be solved using computers.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, the course **PEEBT636** specifically equips electrical/electronics-oriented engineering students with the mathematical and computational foundation required to solve problems in:

- Bioelectric signal processing (ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG)
- Biomechanical modelling
- Medical image reconstruction
- Pharmacokinetic modelling
- Physiological system simulation

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 establishes the *taxonomy* of computational methods. Every subsequent module (Modules 2–5) zooms into one specific branch of this taxonomy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **civil engineer designing a bridge**. Before pouring a single bag of concrete, you create a **mathematical model** on a computer — you calculate load, stress, strain, and material fatigue using differential equations and numerical solvers. The bridge exists only as equations first, concrete later.

**Biomedical engineers do exactly the same thing** — but the "bridge" is a *human organ or biological system*, and the "concrete" is a *clinical decision or medical device*.

For example:
- A cardiologist cannot physically "test" 10,000 drug dosages on a patient's heart. Instead, a **computational model** of the cardiac electrical system is built, and simulations predict the optimal dosage.
- An MRI scanner does not deliver a photograph of the body. It delivers **raw k-space data** that must be computationally reconstructed (using the **Fourier Transform**) into a usable image.

> [!NOTE]
> The computational method is therefore the **translator** between the *physical biological reality* and the *digital mathematical universe*.

## 1.3 Why Computation is Indispensable in BME

Three fundamental reasons make computation non-negotiable:

1. **Biological systems are multi-scale and non-linear.** Heart electrical activity spans from ion channels (nanometres, microseconds) to whole-organ contraction (centimetres, seconds). Computation is the only way to bridge these scales.
2. **Direct experimentation is unethical or impossible.** You cannot slice open a living human brain 100 times to study epilepsy — but a **finite element model (FEM)** of brain tissue can.
3. **Real-time clinical decisions demand speed.** A defibrillator must detect a lethal arrhythmia (ventricular fibrillation) and deliver a shock within seconds. Embedded computational algorithms make this possible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard performance metrics in computational BME: accuracy (often expressed as **>95% sensitivity/specificity** for diagnostic algorithms), execution time (typically **<100 ms** for real-time systems), and reproducibility.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Computational Methods in BME

Computational methods in biomedical engineering can be classified into the following structured branches:

### A. Signal Processing Methods
- **Filtering** (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass) — to remove noise from ECG/EEG signals.
- **Fourier Transform** — to convert time-domain bio-signals into frequency-domain spectra.
- **Wavelet Transform** — for non-stationary signals like ECG QRS complexes.
- **Auto-correlation & Cross-correlation** — to detect repeating patterns (e.g., heart rate variability).

### B. Modelling & Simulation Methods
- **Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs)** — for compartmental models (e.g., pharmacokinetics).
- **Partial Differential Equations (PDEs)** — for heat transfer in tissue, wave propagation in arteries.
- **Finite Element Method (FEM)** — for biomechanical stress analysis of bone, implants.
- **Monte Carlo Simulation** — for radiation dosimetry, blood flow particle tracking.

### C. Statistical & Machine Learning Methods
- **Regression analysis** — for dose-response curves.
- **Classification algorithms** (SVM, Neural Networks) — for cancer detection from images.
- **Clustering** — for patient stratification.

### D. Image Processing & Reconstruction Methods
- **Computed Tomography (CT)** — uses Radon Transform + inverse algorithms.
- **MRI** — uses Fourier Transform of k-space data.
- **Ultrasound beamforming** — uses delay-and-sum algorithms.

### E. Optimization Methods
- **Linear/Non-linear programming** — for treatment planning in radiotherapy.
- **Genetic Algorithms** — for protein folding and drug design.

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundation — Why It Works

The entire computational pipeline rests on the assumption that **biological phenomena can be approximated by mathematical functions**. The general workflow is:

$$ \text{Biological Reality} \;\xrightarrow{\text{modelling}}\; \text{Mathematical Equation} \;\xrightarrow{\text{discretisation}}\; \text{Numerical Algorithm} \;\xrightarrow{\text{computer}}\; \text{Prediction / Image / Diagnosis} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why discretisation?** A computer cannot solve a continuous equation directly. It must convert derivatives into finite differences and integrals into sums. This is the root of all numerical methods (Euler's method, Runge-Kutta, Newton-Raphson, etc.).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Method / Concept | General Equation / Form | Typical Application in BME | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Continuous-time bio-signal | $x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t + \phi)$ | ECG, EEG waveform modelling | mV, $\mu$V |
| 2 | Sampling Theorem | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | Digitising ECG at 500 Hz (Nyquist) | Hz |
| 3 | Fourier Transform | $X(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{-j 2\pi f t}\, dt$ | EEG spectral analysis | V/Hz |
| 4 | Discrete Fourier Transform | $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$ | Digital ECG filtering | — |
| 5 | Convolution (filtering) | $y[n] = \sum_{k} h[k]\, x[n-k]$ | Removing 50 Hz powerline noise | — |
| 6 | ODE — 1-compartment drug model | $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -k\, C(t)$ | Drug concentration in blood | mg/L |
| 7 | Solution to 1st-order ODE | $C(t) = C_0\, e^{-k t}$ | Pharmacokinetic decay | mg/L |
| 8 | Heat equation in tissue | $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha\, \nabla^{2} T$ | Hyperthermia cancer treatment | °C |
| 9 | Finite Difference (1-D derivative) | $\dfrac{dy}{dx} \approx \dfrac{y_{i+1} - y_{i-1}}{2h}$ | Numerical solution of bio-ODEs | — |
| 10 | SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio) | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | Quality of bioelectric recordings | dB |
| 11 | Mean Squared Error | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Model validation | (unit)² |
| 12 | Beamforming delay | $\tau_i = \dfrac{d_i \sin\theta}{c}$ | Ultrasound imaging | s |

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise equations **1, 3, 4, 6, 7, 10, 11** — these are the most frequently tested formulae across all 5 modules of PEEBT636.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

| Field | Computational Method Used | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| Cardiology | ODE modelling of action potential | Pacemaker design |
| Radiology | Iterative reconstruction (inverse Radon) | Low-dose CT scans |
| Neurology | Wavelet + Neural Network | Epileptic seizure prediction |
| Orthopaedics | Finite Element Analysis | Customised bone implants |
| Pharmacology | Multi-compartment ODE | Optimal drug dosing |
| Rehabilitation | EMG signal classification | Smart prosthetic control |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Since Module 1 is an *overview* topic, we demonstrate the **mathematical workflow** that underpins all later modules by deriving the canonical **1-compartment pharmacokinetic model**. This single derivation establishes how a biological phenomenon is converted into a solvable computational problem.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Single-Compartment Drug Elimination Model

### Step 1 — State the Biological Problem
A patient receives an intravenous drug bolus of initial concentration $C_0$ (in mg/L). The body eliminates the drug at a rate proportional to its current concentration. Find the concentration $C(t)$ at any time $t$.

### Step 2 — Translate to a Mathematical Statement
"Eliminates at a rate proportional to concentration" means:

$$ \frac{dC}{dt} = -k\, C(t) $$

where:
- $C(t)$ = drug concentration at time $t$
- $k$ = elimination rate constant (**units: $\text{h}^{-1}$**)
- The negative sign indicates concentration is decreasing.

### Step 3 — Apply Initial Condition
At $t = 0$, the concentration is $C_0$:

$$ C(0) = C_0 $$

### Step 4 — Solve the ODE Using Separation of Variables
Rearrange the equation:

$$ \frac{dC}{C} = -k\, dt $$

Integrate both sides:

$$ \int \frac{dC}{C} = \int -k\, dt $$

This yields:

$$ \ln \vert C \vert = -k t + K_1 $$

where $K_1$ is the constant of integration.

### Step 5 — Exponentiate to Recover $C(t)$

$$ \vert C \vert = e^{-k t + K_1} = e^{K_1}\, e^{-k t} $$

Drop the absolute value (concentration is non-negative):

$$ C(t) = C_0\, e^{-k t} $$

Here $C_0 = e^{K_1}$ is determined from the initial condition $C(0) = C_0$.

### Step 6 — Verify Using Initial Condition
Substitute $t = 0$:

$$ C(0) = C_0\, e^{0} = C_0 \;\checkmark $$

### Step 7 — Compute the Half-Life
Half-life $t_{1/2}$ is the time for $C$ to fall to half its initial value:

$$ \frac{C_0}{2} = C_0\, e^{-k t_{1/2}} $$

Divide both sides by $C_0$:

$$ \frac{1}{2} = e^{-k t_{1/2}} $$

Take natural log:

$$ \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -k\, t_{1/2} $$

$$ -\ln 2 = -k\, t_{1/2} $$

Therefore:

$$ \boxed{\, t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} \approx \frac{0.693}{k} \,} $$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Methods to Problems

| Biological Problem | Mathematical Type | Computational Technique | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| ECG noise removal | Linear filtering | FIR / IIR digital filter | Clean ECG |
| Brain tumour from MRI | Image segmentation | Thresholding + Morphology | Tumour boundary |
| Drug in bloodstream | 1st-order ODE | Analytical / Euler's method | $C(t)$ curve |
| Bone stress under load | 2nd-order PDE | Finite Element Method | Stress map |
| Heart rhythm abnormality | Pattern recognition | SVM / Deep Learning | Normal vs Arrhythmic |
| Radiation dose planning | Linear programming | Simplex / Interior-point | Optimal beam angles |
| Cell population growth | Logistic ODE | Runge-Kutta 4th order | Growth curve |

> [!NOTE]
> Every method listed above reduces, ultimately, to **solving equations using discrete numerical steps on a digital computer**. This is the unifying principle of PEEBT636.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Example — Euler Discretisation of the Drug Model

To illustrate *how* a continuous ODE becomes a computer algorithm, apply Euler's method to $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -k C$:

$$ C_{n+1} = C_n + h \cdot (-k\, C_n) = C_n (1 - k h) $$

where $h$ is the time step. Starting from $C_0 = 100$ mg/L, $k = 0.1$ /h, $h = 0.5$ h:

| Step $n$ | $t_n$ (h) | $C_n$ (mg/L) | Exact $C(t_n)$ (mg/L) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0 | 100.00 | 100.00 |
| 1 | 0.5 | 95.00 | 95.12 |
| 2 | 1.0 | 90.25 | 90.48 |
| 3 | 1.5 | 85.74 | 86.07 |
| 4 | 2.0 | 81.45 | 81.87 |

This demonstrates that **discrete numerical computation approximates the analytical solution** with controlled error.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Computational Biomedical Engineering Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bio Signal or Imaging Data] --> B[Signal Acquisition]
    B --> C[Pre-processing and Filtering]
    C --> D[Mathematical Modelling]
    D --> E[Numerical Discretisation]
    E --> F[Algorithmic Computation]
    F --> G[Output Interpretation]
    G --> H[Clinical Decision or Device Control]
    H --> I[Feedback and Validation]
    I --> D
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Bio Signal / Imaging Data:** ECG, EEG, MRI raw k-space, CT projections, etc.
- **Acquisition:** Amplification, A/D conversion at sampling rate $f_s$.
- **Pre-processing:** Removal of baseline wander, 50/60 Hz line noise, motion artefacts.
- **Modelling:** Translating the biological phenomenon into differential equations or statistical models.
- **Discretisation:** Converting continuous equations into finite-difference / finite-element forms.
- **Computation:** Running the algorithm on CPU, GPU, DSP, or FPGA.
- **Interpretation:** Generating a diagnosis, image, or therapy parameter.
- **Feedback:** Validating output against ground truth (e.g., biopsy result).

## 4.2 Taxonomy of Computational Methods in BME

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ROOT[Computational Methods in BME]
    ROOT --> SP[Signal Processing]
    ROOT --> MS[Modelling and Simulation]
    ROOT --> ML[Statistical and ML Methods]
    ROOT --> IP[Image Processing]
    ROOT --> OPT[Optimization Techniques]

    SP --> SP1[Filtering]
    SP --> SP2[Fourier Transform]
    SP --> SP3[Wavelet Transform]

    MS --> MS1[ODE and PDE Solvers]
    MS --> MS2[Finite Element Method]
    MS --> MS3[Monte Carlo]

    ML --> ML1[Regression]
    ML --> ML2[Classification SVM NN]
    ML --> ML3[Clustering]

    IP --> IP1[CT Reconstruction]
    IP --> IP2[MRI Fourier]
    IP --> IP3[Ultrasound Beamforming]

    OPT --> OPT1[Linear Programming]
    OPT --> OPT2[Genetic Algorithms]
```

## 4.3 Real-Time Closed-Loop Medical Device Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Patient Physiological State] --> S2[Biosensor Electrode or Probe]
    S2 --> S3[Analog Front End Amplifier]
    S3 --> S4[ADC at fs greater than 2fmax]
    S4 --> S5[Digital Signal Processor DSP]
    S5 --> S6{Decision Algorithm Threshold Classifier}
    S6 -->|Abnormal| S7[Therapeutic Actuator Drug Shock Stimulator]
    S7 --> S1
    S6 -->|Normal| S8[Monitor and Log]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This closed-loop architecture is the foundation of **artificial pacemakers, insulin pumps, and seizure-prediction neurostimulators** — all real-world examples of computational biomedical engineering.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Computational Methods in Biomedical Engineering. List any four major categories.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Computational Methods in Biomedical Engineering refer to the numerical, analytical, and algorithmic techniques used to model, simulate, analyse, and visualise biological and physiological systems using digital computers.

*(1 mark for definition)*

Four major categories:
1. **Signal Processing Methods** — filtering, Fourier/wavelet transforms of bio-signals.
2. **Modelling and Simulation Methods** — ODEs, PDEs, Finite Element Method.
3. **Statistical and Machine Learning Methods** — regression, classification, clustering.
4. **Image Processing and Reconstruction Methods** — CT, MRI, ultrasound image formation.

*(1 mark per pair of categories, 2 marks total)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Nyquist Sampling Theorem. Why is it important when digitising an ECG signal?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** A band-limited continuous-time signal with maximum frequency $f_{\max}$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies:

$$ f_s \geq 2\, f_{\max} $$

*(1.5 marks for statement + equation)*

**Importance in ECG:** The ECG signal has dominant frequency components up to approximately **150 Hz** (QRS complex bandwidth). Hence the standard clinical sampling rate is **500 Hz**, which is well above the Nyquist rate of 300 Hz. This avoids *aliasing*, which would otherwise distort the morphology of the QRS complex and lead to misdiagnosis of arrhythmias.

*(1.5 marks for ECG-specific reasoning)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the general computational pipeline used to process a bioelectric signal (such as ECG or EEG) from acquisition to clinical interpretation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the mathematical expression for the concentration of a drug in a single-compartment pharmacokinetic model. From the result, derive an expression for the half-life of the drug. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 marks

The general computational pipeline for processing a bioelectric signal consists of the following sequential blocks:

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| 1. Biosensor / Electrode | Converts ionic bioelectric potential into electronic voltage. |
| 2. Amplifier (Analog Front End) | Boosts microvolt-level signals (ECG ~ 1 mV, EEG ~ 50 $\mu$V) to ADC range. |
| 3. Anti-aliasing Filter | Low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c \leq f_s / 2$ to satisfy Nyquist. |
| 4. ADC | Samples analog signal at $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$. Quantises to binary. |
| 5. Digital Pre-processing | Removes baseline wander, 50 Hz notch, motion artefacts. |
| 6. Feature Extraction | Computes statistical/spectral features (e.g., QRS width, RR interval). |
| 7. Decision Algorithm | Classifier (threshold, SVM, neural network) produces diagnosis. |
| 8. Output Display / Actuator | Monitor screen, alarm, or therapeutic device. |

*(5 marks for block-by-block explanation + 2 marks for diagram and one example — ECG or EEG)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 marks

The single-compartment model assumes uniform instantaneous distribution and first-order elimination.

**Step 1:** Differential equation (1 mark):

$$ \frac{dC}{dt} = -k\, C(t) $$

**Step 2:** Separation of variables (1 mark):

$$ \frac{dC}{C} = -k\, dt $$

**Step 3:** Integration (1 mark):

$$ \ln C = -k t + K_1 $$

**Step 4:** Exponentiation with initial condition $C(0) = C_0$ (1 mark):

$$ C(t) = C_0\, e^{-k t} $$

**Step 5:** Half-life derivation (3 marks):

Setting $C(t_{1/2}) = C_0 / 2$:

$$ \frac{C_0}{2} = C_0\, e^{-k t_{1/2}} $$

$$ \frac{1}{2} = e^{-k t_{1/2}} $$

$$ \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -k\, t_{1/2} $$

$$ \boxed{\, t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} \approx \frac{0.693}{k} \,} $$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the Fourier Transform and its role in analysing bioelectric signals. Write the equation for the Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT) and Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT), and state the relationship between them. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the Finite Element Method (FEM) as applied to biomechanical stress analysis of a bone implant. List any three advantages and two limitations. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 marks

The **Fourier Transform** decomposes a time-domain signal into its constituent frequency components. This is essential in BME because bio-signals (ECG, EEG, EMG) often have diagnostically meaningful frequency content hidden in the time waveform.

**CTFT equation** (2 marks):

$$ X(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{-j 2\pi f t}\, dt $$

**Inverse CTFT** (1 mark):

$$ x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(f)\, e^{+j 2\pi f t}\, df $$

**DFT equation** (2 marks) — required because computers work on discrete samples:

$$ X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}, \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1 $$

**Relationship** (2 marks): DFT is obtained from CTFT by (i) sampling $x(t)$ at $t = nT_s$ to obtain $x[n]$, and (ii) replacing the integral with a finite sum over $N$ samples. DFT is the discretised, computer-implementable form of CTFT. The Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) is an efficient $O(N \log N)$ algorithm to compute the DFT.

**Role in BME:**
- EEG spectral bands: Delta (0.5–4 Hz), Theta (4–8 Hz), Alpha (8–13 Hz), Beta (13–30 Hz) — extracted via FFT for sleep-stage and seizure analysis.
- Heart Rate Variability (HRV): LF/HF power ratio from FFT of RR-interval tachogram.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 marks

The **Finite Element Method (FEM)** is a numerical technique for solving PDEs over complex geometries by discretising the domain into small sub-regions (elements).

**Workflow applied to bone implant analysis** (4 marks):

1. **Geometry creation:** 3-D CAD model of the femur + titanium hip implant.
2. **Meshing:** Domain divided into thousands of tetrahedral elements.
3. **Material properties assigned:** Cortical bone (E = 17 GPa), trabecular bone (E = 0.5 GPa), titanium (E = 110 GPa).
4. **Boundary conditions:** Walking load (~ 3× body weight) applied on femoral head; distal end fixed.
5. **Solver computes:** Displacement field, stress and strain tensors at every node.
6. **Output:** Von Mises stress contour — identifies high-stress regions risking implant loosening or bone fracture.

**Three advantages** (1.5 marks):
- Handles irregular anatomical geometries.
- Allows heterogeneous material properties (cortical vs trabecular bone).
- Visualises stress/strain distributions that are impossible to measure in vivo.

**Two limitations** (1.5 marks):
- Computationally expensive for large models (requires hours of CPU time).
- Accuracy is highly dependent on mesh quality — poor meshing yields inaccurate results.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do not skip the initial condition** when solving ODEs in pharmacokinetic questions — losing this costs **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Do not confuse CTFT with DFT** — CTFT is continuous and integral-based; DFT is discrete and sum-based. Marks are awarded separately for each equation.
> 3. **Do not write $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table** in your answer sheet — use parentheses $(x)$ instead, otherwise the table will break during evaluation script processing.
> 4. **For FEM questions, always mention mesh + boundary conditions** — many students forget these and lose 2 marks.
> 5. **For Nyquist questions, explicitly mention "to avoid aliasing"** — examiners award this phrase as a key conceptual point.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **BME = Engineering + Biology + Computation.** Computation is the bridge.
- The computational pipeline is universal: **Acquire → Filter → Model → Discretise → Compute → Interpret → Validate**.
- Five major categories: **Signal Processing, Modelling/Simulation, Statistical/ML, Image Processing, Optimisation**.
- **Nyquist Sampling:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$. ECG sampled at 500 Hz.
- **Fourier Transform:** time ↔ frequency conversion. FFT is its fast algorithm.
- **1st-order ODE model:** $C(t) = C_0 e^{-k t}$, half-life $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k$.
- **Euler's method:** $C_{n+1} = C_n (1 - k h)$ — discrete numerical approximation.
- **FEM** solves PDEs over irregular biological geometries via meshing + boundary conditions.
- **SNR in dB** = $10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{signal}} / P_{\text{noise}})$.
- All real-time medical devices (pacemaker, insulin pump, defibrillator) follow a **closed-loop sensor → DSP → actuator** architecture.
- Every Module of PEEBT636 (2–5) drills deeper into one branch of the taxonomy introduced in Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Programming Skills (Python/MATLAB): Variables and Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT636 - COMPUTATIONAL METHODS IN BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Programming Skills (Python/MATLAB): Variables and Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Computational Methods in Biomedical Engineering**, a **variable** is a named symbolic reference to a memory location that stores a value of a specific data type. The value may change during program execution, but the name (identifier) remains constant. **Operations** are the symbolic actions (arithmetic, relational, logical, assignment) performed on variables and constants to manipulate biomedical data such as ECG amplitudes, blood pressure values, heart rates, and signal samples.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** A variable is the fundamental unit of data manipulation in any numerical computation. In biomedical engineering, variables represent physiological parameters (e.g., heart rate, blood glucose concentration, body temperature).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **hospital patient monitor**:
- The monitor has several **labeled compartments** (HR, SpO₂, BP, Temp).
- The **labels** are the *variable names*.
- The **numbers displayed** are the *variable values*.
- The labels never change (they remain "HR"), but the values update continuously as the patient's condition evolves.

This is exactly how a programming variable works — a **named container** that holds a value which can be updated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Variables vs. Constants:**
> - **Variable**: Value can change (e.g., `heart_rate = 72`, later `heart_rate = 85`)
> - **Constant**: Value is fixed (e.g., `PI = 3.14159` for blood vessel cross-sectional calculations)

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Biomedical Context

| Symbol | Description | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **π** | Mathematical constant Pi | **3.14159265** |
| **g** | Gravitational acceleration | **9.81 m/s²** |
| **c** | Speed of light (in imaging) | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** |
| **f₀** | Standard ECG sampling frequency | **500 Hz** |
| **ρ_blood** | Density of blood | **1060 kg/m³** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Variables (Data Types)

Variables in Python/MATLAB fall into these categories:

- **Numeric Types**
  - `int` (integer): Heartbeat count, patient age
  - `float` (floating-point): Blood pressure (120.5 mmHg), temperature (37.2 °C)
- **String Type**: Patient ID, name, diagnosis ("Normal Sinus Rhythm")
- **Boolean Type**: `True`/`False` — used in threshold alarms (e.g., `is_critical = True`)
- **Array/Vector Type**: ECG samples, EEG channels (1-D array), MRI slices (3-D array)

### 2.2 Operators — The Action Toolbox

Operators are symbols that perform specific operations on variables and values:

**A. Arithmetic Operators** (used in dose calculations, signal processing)

- **Addition** `+`: Sum of two physiological measurements
- **Subtraction** `-`: Difference between measured and reference value
- **Multiplication** `*`: Area = length × width (organ dimensions)
- **Division** `/`: Mean arterial pressure calculation
- **Modulus** `%`: Remainder (used in indexing signal arrays)
- **Exponentiation** `**` or `^`: Exponential decay of drug concentration

**B. Relational (Comparison) Operators**

- Equal to `==`
- Not equal to `!=`
- Greater than `>`
- Less than `<`
- Greater than or equal to `>=`
- Less than or equal to `<=`

Used in alarm conditions: `if heart_rate > 120: alarm()`

**C. Logical Operators**

- `and`: Both conditions must be True
- `or`: Either condition must be True
- `not`: Negation of a condition

**D. Assignment Operators**

- `=`, `+=`, `-=`, `*=`, `/=`, `**=`

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Operation | Python Syntax | MATLAB Syntax | Biomedical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Assignment | `x = 5` | `x = 5;` | Store heart rate |
| Addition | `a + b` | `a + b` | Sum of signal samples |
| Exponentiation | `a ** b` | `a^b` | Drug decay: $C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ |
| Modulus | `a % b` | `mod(a,b)` | Array indexing |
| Integer division | `a // b` | `idivide(a,b)` | Average beats per minute |
| Equal to | `a == b` | `a == b` | Threshold check |
| Logical AND | `a and b` | `a && b` | Multi-condition alarm |
| Type cast | `float(x)` | `double(x)` | Sensor data conversion |

### 2.4 Operator Precedence (Highest to Lowest)

$$\text{Parentheses} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Exponentiation} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Multiplication/Division} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Addition/Subtraction} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Comparison} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Logical}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always use parentheses** when in doubt. In MATLAB/Python, `( )` override default precedence.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Biomedical Engineering

- **Signal Processing**: Variables store ECG samples; operations filter noise.
- **Image Processing**: Pixel intensities stored as arrays; operations perform contrast adjustment.
- **Pharmacokinetics**: Variables track drug concentration over time.
- **Statistical Analysis**: Mean, variance, standard deviation of patient data.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Mathematical Operations on Biomedical Variables

**Example 1: Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)**

The Mean Arterial Pressure is clinically calculated as:

$$\text{MAP} = \text{DBP} + \frac{1}{3}(\text{SBP} - \text{DBP})$$

where SBP is systolic blood pressure and DBP is diastolic blood pressure.

**Step-by-step implementation logic:**

- **Step 1**: Assign SBP and DBP from patient data.

  $$\text{SBP} = 120 \;\; \text{mmHg}, \quad \text{DBP} = 80 \;\; \text{mmHg}$$

- **Step 2**: Compute the pulse pressure difference.

  $$\text{SBP} - \text{DBP} = 120 - 80 = 40 \;\; \text{mmHg}$$

- **Step 3**: Multiply by the constant $\frac{1}{3}$.

  $$\frac{1}{3} \times 40 = 13.33 \;\; \text{mmHg}$$

- **Step 4**: Add to DBP.

  $$\text{MAP} = 80 + 13.33 = 93.33 \;\; \text{mmHg}$$

**Python equivalent:**

```python
# Mean Arterial Pressure Calculation
SBP = 120       # Systolic Blood Pressure (mmHg)
DBP = 80        # Diastolic Blood Pressure (mmHg)

pulse_pressure = SBP - DBP
one_third = 1 / 3
MAP = DBP + one_third * pulse_pressure

print(f"Mean Arterial Pressure = {MAP:.2f} mmHg")
```

**MATLAB equivalent:**

```matlab
% Mean Arterial Pressure Calculation
SBP = 120;      % Systolic Blood Pressure (mmHg)
DBP = 80;       % Diastolic Blood Pressure (mmHg)

pulse_pressure = SBP - DBP;
MAP = DBP + (1/3) * pulse_pressure;

fprintf('Mean Arterial Pressure = %.2f mmHg\n', MAP);
```

---

**Example 2: Body Mass Index (BMI)**

The Body Mass Index formula is:

$$\text{BMI} = \frac{W}{H^2}$$

where $W$ is weight in kilograms and $H$ is height in meters.

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

- **Step 1**: Define weight and height variables.

  $$W = 70 \;\; \text{kg}, \quad H = 1.75 \;\; \text{m}$$

- **Step 2**: Compute the square of height (exponentiation operation).

  $$H^2 = (1.75)^2 = 3.0625 \;\; \text{m}^2$$

- **Step 3**: Divide weight by height squared.

  $$\text{BMI} = \frac{70}{3.0625} = 22.86 \;\; \text{kg/m}^2$$

**Python equivalent:**

```python
# Body Mass Index Calculation
weight = 70         # Weight in kg
height = 1.75       # Height in meters

BMI = weight / (height ** 2)
print(f"BMI = {BMI:.2f} kg/m^2")
```

---

**Example 3: Drug Concentration Decay (First-Order Kinetics)**

The plasma concentration of a drug follows exponential decay:

$$C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$$

Let $C_0 = 100$ mg/L (initial dose), $k = 0.1$ hr$^{-1}$ (elimination constant), $t = 5$ hr.

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

- **Step 1**: Identify the variables.

  $$C_0 = 100, \quad k = 0.1, \quad t = 5$$

- **Step 2**: Compute the exponent argument.

  $$-k \cdot t = -(0.1)(5) = -0.5$$

- **Step 3**: Apply the exponential function.

  $$e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065$$

- **Step 4**: Multiply by $C_0$.

  $$C(5) = 100 \times 0.6065 = 60.65 \;\; \text{mg/L}$$

**Python equivalent:**

```python
import math

# Drug Concentration Decay
C0 = 100       # Initial concentration (mg/L)
k = 0.1        # Elimination rate constant (per hour)
t = 5          # Time elapsed (hours)

C_t = C0 * math.exp(-k * t)
print(f"Drug concentration at t={t}h: {C_t:.2f} mg/L")
```

**MATLAB equivalent:**

```matlab
% Drug Concentration Decay
C0 = 100;      % Initial concentration (mg/L)
k = 0.1;       % Elimination rate constant (per hour)
t = 5;         % Time elapsed (hours)

C_t = C0 * exp(-k * t);
fprintf('Drug concentration at t=%d h: %.2f mg/L\n', t, C_t);
```

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common Biomedical Computations

| Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variable Roles | KTU Importance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cardiac Output | $\text{CO} = \text{HR} \times \text{SV}$ | HR, SV as float | High |
| Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | ρ, v, D, μ as float | Medium |
| Ohm's Law (Bioelectric) | $V = I \cdot R$ | V, I, R as float | High |
| Beer-Lambert Law | $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$ | ε, c, l as float | Medium |
| Respiratory Rate Tidal Volume | $V_T = V_{\text{insp}} - V_{\text{exp}}$ | Subtraction op | Medium |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Variable Lifecycle and Memory Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Start] --> B[Variable Declaration]
    B --> C[Memory Allocation]
    C --> D{Type Check}
    D -->|Integer| E1[Allocated as int]
    D -->|Float| E2[Allocated as float]
    D -->|String| E3[Allocated as str]
    D -->|Boolean| E4[Allocated as bool]
    E1 --> F[Value Stored at Address]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Operations Performed]
    G --> H{Value Changed?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Update Memory Location]
    H -->|No| J[Read Value]
    I --> G
    J --> K[Output / Use in Computation]
```

### 4.2 Operator Precedence Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Expression Entered] --> B{Parentheses?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Evaluate Innermost First]
    B -->|No| D{Exponentiation?}
    C --> D
    D -->|Yes| E[Apply Power]
    D -->|No| F{Multiplication / Division?}
    E --> F
    F -->|Yes| G[Apply * or /]
    F -->|No| H{Addition / Subtraction?}
    G --> H
    H -->|Yes| I[Apply + or -]
    H -->|No| J{Comparison?}
    I --> J
    J -->|Yes| K[Apply < > == !=]
    J -->|No| L{Logical?}
    K --> L
    L -->|Yes| M[Apply and or or]
    L -->|No| N[Final Result]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture for Biomedical Computation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Patient Data Input] --> A2[Variable Assignment]
    A2 --> A3[Operation Module]
    A3 --> A4[Arithmetic Engine]
    A3 --> A5[Relational Engine]
    A3 --> A6[Logical Engine]
    A4 --> A7[Computation Output]
    A5 --> A7
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8[Display / Alarm / Record]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** In a biomedical computation pipeline, raw patient data enters as variables; the operation module selects appropriate operators; results flow to output channels (monitor display, alarm system, or electronic health record).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]**

**Q1. Define a variable. List any four data types supported in Python/MATLAB with biomedical examples.**

**Model Answer:**
A variable is a named memory location that stores a value which may change during program execution.

Four data types with biomedical examples:
- `int` — Patient age (e.g., `age = 45`)
- `float` — Body temperature (e.g., `temp = 37.2`)
- `str` — Patient diagnosis (e.g., `diagnosis = "Hypertension"`)
- `bool` — Critical alert flag (e.g., `is_critical = True`)

**(3 Marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Q2]**

**Q2. Differentiate between `=` and `==` operators in Python with a biomedical example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `=` (Assignment) | `==` (Comparison) |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Assigns a value to a variable | Checks equality between two values |
| Returns | The assigned value | Boolean (`True`/`False`) |
| Example | `heart_rate = 72` | `if heart_rate == 72:` |

Biomedical example: `heart_rate = 72` assigns the value 72 to the variable `heart_rate`. The expression `heart_rate == 72` evaluates to `True` and may trigger a stable-condition alert.

**(3 Marks)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 Choice]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the different types of operators available in Python/MATLAB with suitable biomedical engineering examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Operators are symbols that perform operations on variables and values. The main categories are:

**1. Arithmetic Operators** — Used for mathematical calculations:

- **Addition `+`**: `total_dose = morning_dose + evening_dose`
- **Subtraction `-`**: `pulse_pressure = SBP - DBP`
- **Multiplication `*`**: `cardiac_output = heart_rate * stroke_volume`
- **Division `/`**: `bmi = weight / (height ** 2)`
- **Modulus `%`**: Used to determine odd/even index in signal arrays
- **Exponentiation `**` or `^`**: `drug_concentration = C0 * exp(-k * t)` uses `**` for power

**2. Relational (Comparison) Operators** — Return boolean values:

- `==`, `!=`, `>`, `<`, `>=`, `<=`
- Example: `if temperature >= 38.5: trigger_fever_alarm()`

**3. Logical Operators** — Combine multiple conditions:

- `and`, `or`, `not`
- Example: `if heart_rate > 100 and spo2 < 90: critical_alert()`

**4. Assignment Operators** — Combined assignment + operation:

- `+=`, `-=`, `*=`, `/=`, `**=`
- Example: `cumulative_dose += 50` adds 50 mg to the running total

**(7 Marks — 1 mark per category definition + 1 mark per biomedical example)**

---

**(b)** A patient weighing 75 kg and standing 1.68 m tall has systolic BP = 130 mmHg and diastolic BP = 85 mmHg. Write a Python program to compute the BMI and Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP). Display the results with two decimal places. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Formulas used:**

$$\text{BMI} = \frac{W}{H^2}$$

$$\text{MAP} = \text{DBP} + \frac{1}{3}(\text{SBP} - \text{DBP})$$

**Python Program:**

```python
# Patient Parameters
weight = 75.0          # Weight in kg
height = 1.68          # Height in meters
SBP = 130              # Systolic BP in mmHg
DBP = 85               # Diastolic BP in mmHg

# Compute BMI
BMI = weight / (height ** 2)

# Compute MAP
MAP = DBP + (1/3) * (SBP - DBP)

# Display Results
print(f"Body Mass Index = {BMI:.2f} kg/m^2")
print(f"Mean Arterial Pressure = {MAP:.2f} mmHg")
```

**Step-by-step trace:**

- BMI: $75.0 / (1.68)^2 = 75.0 / 2.8224 = 26.57 \;\text{kg/m}^2$
- Pulse Pressure: $130 - 85 = 45$ mmHg
- MAP: $85 + (1/3)(45) = 85 + 15 = 100$ mmHg

**Output:**
```
Body Mass Index = 26.57 kg/m^2
Mean Arterial Pressure = 100.00 mmHg
```

**(7 Marks — 2 for formulas, 3 for code, 2 for correct output)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Describe the rules for naming variables in Python/MATLAB. Provide five valid and three invalid variable names with reasons. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Naming Rules:**

1. Must start with a letter (a–z, A–Z) or underscore `_`.
2. Subsequent characters can be letters, digits (0–9), or underscores.
3. Cannot be a reserved keyword (e.g., `if`, `for`, `while`, `class`).
4. Case-sensitive in Python (`HeartRate` ≠ `heartrate`); MATLAB is also case-sensitive.
5. Should be descriptive and meaningful (good practice).
6. No spaces or special characters (`@`, `#`, `$`, `%`).

**Five Valid Names:**

- `heart_rate = 72` — uses lowercase with underscore (snake_case)
- `ECG_signal = [0.1, 0.2, 0.3]` — descriptive
- `patientAge = 45` — camelCase style
- `_temp_celsius = 37.2` — leading underscore allowed
- `BP1 = 120` — alphanumeric (starts with letter)

**Three Invalid Names:**

- `2nd_pressure = 90` — starts with a digit ❌
- `class = "ECG"` — `class` is a reserved keyword ❌
- `heart-rate = 72` — contains a hyphen ❌

**(7 Marks — 3 for rules, 2 for valid examples, 2 for invalid examples with reasoning)**

---

**(b)** Explain operator precedence in Python/MATLAB with a biomedical expression. Evaluate the following expression step by step:

$$y = 5 + 3 \times 2^2 - \frac{10}{2} + (8 > 5)$$ **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Precedence Order (highest → lowest):**

1. Parentheses `()`
2. Exponentiation `**` or `^`
3. Multiplication/Division `* /` (left-to-right)
4. Addition/Subtraction `+ -` (left-to-right)
5. Comparison `> < ==`
6. Logical `and or`

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

- **Step 1:** Evaluate parentheses: $(8 > 5) = \text{True} = 1$

$$y = 5 + 3 \times 2^2 - \frac{10}{2} + 1$$

- **Step 2:** Evaluate exponentiation: $2^2 = 4$

$$y = 5 + 3 \times 4 - 5 + 1$$

- **Step 3:** Evaluate multiplication and division (left-to-right):

$$3 \times 4 = 12, \quad \frac{10}{2} = 5$$

$$y = 5 + 12 - 5 + 1$$

- **Step 4:** Evaluate addition and subtraction (left-to-right):

$$5 + 12 = 17, \quad 17 - 5 = 12, \quad 12 + 1 = 13$$

- **Final Result:** $y = 13$

**Python Verification:**

```python
y = 5 + 3 * 2**2 - 10/2 + (8 > 5)
print(y)   # Output: 13.0
```

(Note: In Python, `10/2 = 5.0` (float), so result is `13.0`)

**(7 Marks — 2 for precedence list, 4 for stepwise evaluation, 1 for final answer)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - Confusing `=` (assignment) with `==` (comparison) — costs up to **2 marks**.
> - Using `^` for power in Python (it's XOR, not exponentiation; use `**`) — **1 mark** deducted.
> - Forgetting that `True` evaluates to `1` in arithmetic context — **2 marks** lost in output.
> - Writing `10/2` as `5.0` (float) instead of `5` (int) in Python — output mismatch.
> - Not showing trace/stepwise computation in mathematical problems — partial marking lost.
> - Using reserved keywords as variable names — **entire answer invalidated** in coding questions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **variable** is a named memory location; its value can change during execution.
- ✅ Python uses `**` for exponentiation; MATLAB uses `^` — do not interchange.
- ✅ The `=` operator is for **assignment**; `==` is for **comparison**.
- ✅ Python is **dynamically typed**; MATLAB variables are also dynamically typed (default `double`).
- ✅ Operator precedence: `()` → `**` → `*/` → `+-` → `comparison` → `logical`.
- ✅ Boolean `True` equals `1` and `False` equals `0` in arithmetic expressions.
- ✅ Variable names must start with a letter or underscore — never a digit.
- ✅ Reserved keywords (`if`, `for`, `while`, `class`, `return`, etc.) **cannot** be variable names.
- ✅ Biomedical applications: dose calculation, MAP/BMI, signal sample storage, threshold alarms.
- ✅ Always use **parentheses** to override default precedence and avoid ambiguity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conditional Statements (if, elif, else) and Loops (for, while)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT636 - COMPUTATIONAL METHODS IN BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Conditional Statements (if, elif, else) and Loops (for, while)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conditional Statements** are programming constructs that allow the execution of specific blocks of code based on whether a given Boolean expression evaluates to `True` or `False`. In Python, these are implemented using the `if`, `elif` (else-if), and `else` keywords.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Loops** are iterative control structures that allow a block of code to be executed repeatedly until a termination condition is met. Python natively supports two primary loop constructs: the **definite `for` loop** (used for iterating over sequences) and the **indefinite `while` loop** (used for repeated execution based on a Boolean condition).

Together, these constructs form the **backbone of algorithmic flow control** in any biomedical computation pipeline — from automated ECG R-peak detection to iterative solvers for bioheat transfer equations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **Conditional Statement** as a *clinical decision tree* in a diagnostic protocol:

- **If** the patient's body temperature is $> 38.5^{\circ}\text{C}$ → diagnose as **fever**.
- **Elif** the temperature is between $37.5^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $38.5^{\circ}\text{C}$ → diagnose as **low-grade fever**.
- **Else** → the patient is **afebrile** (normal).

Just as a physician evaluates conditions one after another, the program checks each condition sequentially.

Think of a **Loop** as a *pharmacokinetic sampling protocol*:

- A **for loop** is like a pre-scheduled sampling routine: *"Collect blood samples at t = 0, 1, 2, 4, 8, 12, 24 hours"* — the iterations are known and finite.
- A **while loop** is like titration: *"Keep adjusting the IV drip rate until the patient's blood pressure stabilizes within the normal range"* — the loop continues **as long as** the target condition is not met.

## 1.3 Core Syntax Standards (KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> Python uses **indentation** (4 spaces or 1 tab) instead of braces `{}` to define code blocks. The `:` at the end of `if`, `elif`, `else`, `for`, and `while` lines is **mandatory**.

### Canonical Skeletons

```python
# Conditional Statement Skeleton
if condition_1:
    # block executes if condition_1 is True
elif condition_2:
    # block executes if condition_1 is False AND condition_2 is True
else:
    # block executes if all above conditions are False
```

```python
# For Loop Skeleton
for variable in iterable:
    # block executes once per element in iterable
```

```python
# While Loop Skeleton
while condition:
    # block executes repeatedly as long as condition is True
    # MUST include an update statement to avoid infinite loops
```

## 1.4 Standard Boolean Comparison Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following are the standard comparison and logical operators that drive conditional expressions in Python biomedical algorithms:

| Operator Type | Symbols | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Relational | `==`, `!=`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=` | Compare two values |
| Logical | `and`, `or`, `not` | Combine Boolean expressions |
| Membership | `in`, `not in` | Test sequence membership |
| Identity | `is`, `is not` | Test object identity |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of `if-elif-else`

The `if-elif-else` construct is evaluated **top-down**. Python stops at the *first* condition that evaluates to `True` and skips the rest. This is critical in biomedical applications where **triage** (priority-based decision logic) is essential.

### Step-by-step Evaluation Logic

1. **Evaluate `condition_1`**:
   - If `True` → execute the `if` block, then **jump out** of the entire conditional chain.
   - If `False` → proceed to the next `elif` (if any).
2. **Evaluate each `elif` condition in order**:
   - If `True` → execute its block, then jump out.
   - If `False` → continue to the next.
3. **Execute `else` block** (optional, but acts as a catch-all):
   - Only reached if **all** preceding conditions were `False`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** A common mistake is using multiple independent `if` statements instead of `if-elif-else`. Multiple `if`s check *every* condition; `if-elif-else` executes *only one* branch.

## 2.2 Operational Logic of `for` Loop

The `for` loop performs **definite iteration** over an iterable object (list, tuple, string, range, NumPy array, etc.). The loop variable takes on the value of each element successively.

### Step-by-step Execution Flow

1. The iterable is converted to an iterator internally.
2. The loop variable is assigned the value of the **next** element.
3. The loop body executes.
4. Steps 2–3 repeat until the iterator is **exhausted** (raises `StopIteration` internally).

### The `range()` Function — A KTU Favorite

`range(start, stop, step)` generates a sequence of integers:

$$\text{range}(a, b, s) = \{a, \, a+s, \, a+2s, \ldots, \, a + ks\}$$

where the largest element satisfies $a + ks < b$ and $a + (k+1)s \geq b$.

> [!NOTE]
> In Python, `range(stop)` implies `start = 0` and `step = 1`. The `stop` value is **exclusive**.

## 2.3 Operational Logic of `while` Loop

The `while` loop performs **indefinite iteration** based on a Boolean condition.

### Step-by-step Execution Flow

1. Evaluate the **condition**.
2. If `True` → execute the body.
3. Re-evaluate the condition.
4. If `False` → terminate.
5. **Loop Control Statements**:
   - `break` → exit the loop immediately.
   - `continue` → skip the rest of the current iteration and jump to the next condition check.
   - `pass` → do nothing (placeholder).

> [!WARNING]
> A `while` loop with a condition that **never becomes `False`** is called an **infinite loop**. In KTU exams, always ensure the loop variable is **updated** inside the body.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Construct | Syntax | Termination Condition | Typical Biomedical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| `if` | `if cond:` | Evaluates once | Alarm threshold check |
| `if-else` | `if cond: ... else: ...` | One of two branches | Binary diagnostic (Normal/Abnormal) |
| `if-elif-else` | `if c1: ... elif c2: ... else:` | First matching branch | Multi-class disease classification |
| `for` | `for var in iter:` | Iterator exhausted | Iterating over patient records (list) |
| `range(n)` | `for i in range(n):` | $i \in \{0, 1, \ldots, n-1\}$ | Generating time indices for signal processing |
| `while` | `while cond:` | `cond` becomes `False` | Iterative numerical solvers (Newton-Raphson) |
| `break` | `break` | Inside loop | Early exit when signal exceeds threshold |
| `continue` | `continue` | Inside loop | Skip corrupted samples in a dataset |

## 2.5 Nested Control Structures

Conditional and loop constructs can be **nested** within each other. The total number of iterations of a nested loop is the **product** of iterations of each loop. For two loops with $m$ and $n$ iterations:

$$T_{\text{nested}} = m \times n$$

For biomedical signal processing, a typical pattern is:

```python
for channel in range(num_channels):       # outer: electrode channels
    for sample in range(num_samples):    # inner: time samples
        if signal[channel][sample] > threshold:
            # flag abnormal sample
```

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Construct Used | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| ECG R-peak detection | `if-elif-else` | Classify QRS complex morphology |
| DICOM image pixel iteration | `for` | Traverse 2D image matrix |
| Iterative MRI reconstruction | `while` | Continue until error $< 10^{-4}$ |
| Heart rate variability (HRV) classification | `nested for` | Compute features per time window |
| Real-time patient monitor | `while True:` with `break` | Continuous monitoring with alarm exit |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides exhaustive, step-by-step logical derivations and worked examples of common KTU examination problems on control flow. Every transition is explicitly shown.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: BMI Classification (Conditional Statement)

**Problem:** Write a Python program to read a patient's weight (in kg) and height (in m) and classify their Body Mass Index (BMI) according to WHO standards.

### Step 1: Define the BMI formula

The Body Mass Index is given by:

$$\text{BMI} = \frac{w}{h^{2}}$$

where $w$ is the weight in kilograms (kg) and $h$ is the height in meters (m). The standard unit of BMI is $\text{kg/m}^{2}$.

### Step 2: Define classification thresholds

According to WHO, the standard BMI categories are:

| BMI Range | Category |
|---|---|
| $\text{BMI} < 18.5$ | Underweight |
| $18.5 \leq \text{BMI} < 25$ | Normal |
| $25 \leq \text{BMI} < 30$ | Overweight |
| $\text{BMI} \geq 30$ | Obese |

### Step 3: Translate to Python

```python
# Step 3a: Read inputs
w = float(input("Enter weight in kg: "))
h = float(input("Enter height in m: "))

# Step 3b: Compute BMI
bmi = w / (h ** 2)

# Step 3c: Conditional classification
if bmi < 18.5:
    category = "Underweight"
elif bmi < 25:
    category = "Normal"
elif bmi < 30:
    category = "Overweight"
else:
    category = "Obese"

print(f"BMI = {bmi:.2f}, Category = {category}")
```

### Step 4: Dry Run for $w = 70$ kg, $h = 1.75$ m

$$\text{BMI} = \frac{70}{(1.75)^{2}} = \frac{70}{3.0625} \approx 22.86$$

Trace:

- `bmi < 18.5` → `22.86 < 18.5` → **False** → skip.
- `bmi < 25` → `22.86 < 25` → **True** → assign `"Normal"`, exit chain.
- Output: `BMI = 22.86, Category = Normal`.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Heart Rate Analyzer (for Loop)

**Problem:** A list `hr = [72, 110, 55, 88, 130, 60]` contains heart rate measurements in bpm. Write a program to count how many readings are **bradycardic** ($< 60$ bpm), **normal** ($60$–$100$ bpm), and **tachycardic** ($> 100$ bpm).

### Step 1: Initialize counters

```python
hr = [72, 110, 55, 88, 130, 60]
brady = 0
normal = 0
tachy = 0
```

### Step 2: Iterate using `for` loop

```python
for rate in hr:
    if rate < 60:
        brady = brady + 1
    elif rate <= 100:
        normal = normal + 1
    else:
        tachy = tachy + 1
```

### Step 3: Trace execution

| Iteration | `rate` | Condition Matched | `brady` | `normal` | `tachy` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Init | — | — | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| 1 | $72$ | `60 ≤ 72 ≤ 100` | $0$ | $1$ | $0$ |
| 2 | $110$ | `110 > 100` | $0$ | $1$ | $1$ |
| 3 | $55$ | `55 < 60` | $1$ | $1$ | $1$ |
| 4 | $88$ | `60 ≤ 88 ≤ 100` | $1$ | $2$ | $1$ |
| 5 | $130$ | `130 > 100` | $1$ | $2$ | $2$ |
| 6 | $60$ | `60 ≤ 60 ≤ 100` | $1$ | $3$ | $2$ |

### Step 4: Output

```python
print(f"Bradycardic: {brady}, Normal: {normal}, Tachycardic: {tachy}")
# Output: Bradycardic: 1, Normal: 3, Tachycardic: 2
```

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Drug Dosage Accumulation (while Loop)

**Problem:** A patient receives a drug dose of $D$ mg at the start of every hour. The drug is eliminated at a rate of $k$ per hour (first-order kinetics). The amount remaining after $t$ hours is:

$$A(t) = D \cdot e^{-kt}$$

Write a program to find the **first hour** at which the drug amount falls below $0.5D$ (half-life). Take $k = 0.1$, $D = 100$ mg.

### Step 1: Mathematical Setup

The threshold condition is:

$$A(t) = D \cdot e^{-kt} < 0.5D$$

Dividing both sides by $D$:

$$e^{-kt} < 0.5$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides (note that $\ln$ reverses the inequality since $e^{-kt} > 0$):

$$-kt < \ln(0.5)$$

Since $\ln(0.5) \approx -0.6931$:

$$-kt < -0.6931$$

Dividing by $-k$ (note that dividing by a negative **flips** the inequality):

$$t > \frac{0.6931}{k} = \frac{0.6931}{0.1} \approx 6.93 \text{ hours}$$

So the first integer hour at which $A(t) < 0.5D$ is $t = 7$ hours.

### Step 2: Python `while` Implementation

```python
import math

D = 100
k = 0.1
threshold = 0.5 * D
t = 0
amount = D

while amount >= threshold:
    t = t + 1
    amount = D * math.exp(-k * t)

print(f"Drug falls below half-life at t = {t} hours")
print(f"Amount at t={t}: {amount:.2f} mg")
```

### Step 3: Trace Execution

| `t` | $A(t) = 100 e^{-0.1t}$ | `amount >= 50`? | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $100.00$ | True | Enter loop |
| 1 | $90.48$ | True | Continue |
| 2 | $81.87$ | True | Continue |
| 3 | $74.08$ | True | Continue |
| 4 | $67.03$ | True | Continue |
| 5 | $60.65$ | True | Continue |
| 6 | $54.88$ | True | Continue |
| 7 | $49.66$ | **False** | Exit loop |

**Output:** `Drug falls below half-life at t = 7 hours`, `Amount at t=7: 49.66 mg`

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Nested Loops — Multiplication Table (Biomath)

**Problem:** Generate a $5 \times 5$ multiplication table representing dose multipliers (1× to 5×) across five patient weight bands.

### Step 1: Mathematical Representation

The table entry at row $i$ and column $j$ is:

$$T_{ij} = i \times j \quad \text{for } i, j \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$$

### Step 2: Python Code

```python
for i in range(1, 6):
    for j in range(1, 6):
        print(f"{i*j:4}", end=" ")
    print()
```

### Step 3: Output

```
   1    2    3    4    5 
   2    4    6    8   10 
   3    6    9   12   15 
   4    8   12   16   20 
   5   10   15   20   25 
```

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | `if-elif-else` | `for` Loop | `while` Loop |
|---|---|---|---|
| Iteration count | 0 or 1 | Known (over iterable) | Unknown (depends on condition) |
| Risk of infinite execution | None | None | **High** (if condition never false) |
| Requires initialization | No | No (Python handles iterator) | **Yes** (loop variable must be initialized) |
| Requires update statement | No | Automatic | **Yes** (inside body) |
| Time complexity (typical) | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(k)$ where $k$ depends on condition |
| Best suited for | Decision logic | Sequence traversal | Iterative numerical methods |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart: `if-elif-else` Conditional Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Start]) --> nodeB[Read input values]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Evaluate condition 1}
    nodeC -->|True| nodeD[Execute Block 1]
    nodeC -->|False| nodeE{Evaluate condition 2}
    nodeE -->|True| nodeF[Execute Block 2]
    nodeE -->|False| nodeG[Execute Else Block]
    nodeD --> nodeH([End])
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
```

**Interpretation:** The diamond nodes represent decision points (Boolean evaluations). Only **one** execution block is reached before ending. This is the classical **triage pattern** used in clinical decision support systems.

## 4.2 Flowchart: `for` Loop Iteration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Start]) --> nodeB[Initialize iterator over sequence]
    nodeB --> nodeC{More elements in iterator?}
    nodeC -->|Yes| nodeD[Assign next element to loop variable]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Execute loop body]
    nodeE --> nodeC
    nodeC -->|No| nodeF([End])
```

**Interpretation:** The iterator cycles through the sequence. The body executes once per element. This is the **definite iteration** model — used when the number of iterations is known in advance (e.g., processing a fixed-length ECG recording).

## 4.3 Flowchart: `while` Loop with Loop Control

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Start]) --> nodeB[Initialize loop variable]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Evaluate condition}
    nodeC -->|True| nodeD{Encounter break statement?}
    nodeD -->|Yes| nodeG([End])
    nodeD -->|No| nodeE{Encounter continue statement?}
    nodeE -->|Yes| nodeC
    nodeE -->|No| nodeF[Execute loop body and update variable]
    nodeF --> nodeC
    nodeC -->|False| nodeG
```

**Interpretation:** The condition is re-checked **before every iteration**. `break` forces immediate exit; `continue` skips the rest of the current iteration and re-evaluates the condition. This is the **indefinite iteration** model — used in iterative solvers and real-time monitoring.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Control Flow in a Biomedical Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Raw Biosignal Input] --> nodeB{Preprocessing Required?}
    nodeB -->|Yes| nodeC[Filter and Denoise]
    nodeB -->|No| nodeD[Direct Feature Extraction]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE{Iterative Refinement Needed?}
    nodeE -->|Yes| nodeF[While Loop Solver]
    nodeE -->|No| nodeG[For Loop over Channels]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH{Abnormality Detected?}
    nodeH -->|Yes| nodeI[Trigger Alert]
    nodeH -->|No| nodeJ[Log Normal Reading]
    nodeI --> nodeK([Output Report])
    nodeJ --> nodeK
```

**Interpretation:** This is a high-level **block diagram** of a typical biomedical signal processing pipeline. It shows how conditional and loop constructs interleave to handle raw biosignals (ECG, EEG, EMG) through preprocessing, feature extraction, iterative numerical refinement, multi-channel analysis, and final diagnostic decision-making.

## 4.5 Comparison Diagram: Definite vs Indefinite Iteration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Choose Loop Type]) --> nodeB{Know number of iterations in advance?}
    nodeB -->|Yes| nodeC[Use FOR loop with range or sequence]
    nodeB -->|No| nodeD[Use WHILE loop with condition]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Example: Iterate over 1000 samples]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Example: Iterate until convergence]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> Differentiate between a `for` loop and a `while` loop in Python. When would you prefer one over the other in a biomedical computation context?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `for` loop | `while` loop` |
|---|---|---|
| Iteration type | Definite (over an iterable) | Indefinite (condition-based) |
| Number of iterations | Known in advance | Determined by runtime condition |
| Initialization | Not required (handled by iterator) | Must be explicitly initialized |
| Update statement | Automatic | Must be coded inside the body |
| Risk of infinite loop | None (iterator exhausts) | High if update is missing |

**Biomedical preference:** Use a `for` loop when iterating over a **fixed-length** dataset such as samples in a digitized ECG signal. Use a `while` loop in **iterative numerical algorithms** (e.g., Newton-Raphson for solving the Nernst equation) where the termination depends on achieving a convergence criterion, not a fixed count.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> What is the role of `break` and `continue` statements inside loops? Give one biomedical example for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **`break`** — Terminates the loop immediately and transfers control to the statement following the loop. *Biomedical example:* Exit a real-time heart rate monitoring loop the moment an alarm threshold is exceeded.

- **`continue`** — Skips the remaining statements in the current iteration and proceeds to the next iteration. *Biomedical example:* Skip corrupted or noisy samples in an EEG dataset without terminating the entire processing pipeline.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse `break` with `continue`. `break` exits the **entire loop**; `continue` only skips the **current iteration**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Write a Python program that reads a list of $n$ patient temperatures (in °C) and uses a `for` loop with `if-elif-else` to classify each as **Hypothermia** ($< 35^{\circ}\text{C}$), **Normal** ($35$–$37.5^{\circ}\text{C}$), **Fever** ($37.5$–$39.5^{\circ}\text{C}$), or **Hyperthermia** ($\geq 39.5^{\circ}\text{C}$). Print the count of each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A patient's blood concentration of a drug follows first-order elimination:

$$C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$$

where $C_0 = 50$ mg/L, $k = 0.2$ per hour. Write a Python program using a `while` loop to find the **first integer hour** at which the concentration falls below the therapeutic threshold of $C_{\text{thresh}} = 10$ mg/L. Display the concentration at each hour. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (a)

```python
# Step 1: Read number of patients
n = int(input("Enter number of patients: "))
temps = []

# Step 2: Read temperatures into a list
for i in range(n):
    t = float(input(f"Enter temperature for patient {i+1}: "))
    temps.append(t)

# Step 3: Initialize counters
hypo = 0
normal = 0
fever = 0
hyper = 0

# Step 4: Classify each temperature
for temp in temps:
    if temp < 35:
        hypo = hypo + 1
    elif temp < 37.5:
        normal = normal + 1
    elif temp < 39.5:
        fever = fever + 1
    else:
        hyper = hyper + 1

# Step 5: Display results
print(f"Hypothermia: {hypo}")
print(f"Normal: {normal}")
print(f"Fever: {fever}")
print(f"Hyperthermia: {hyper}")
```

**Explanation of logic (for 7 marks):**
- `range(n)` generates indices $0, 1, \ldots, n-1$. **(1 mark)**
- `append()` builds the input list. **(1 mark)**
- The `for` loop iterates over every recorded temperature. **(1 mark)**
- The `if-elif-else` chain uses boundary checks in **ascending** order to avoid overlap. **(2 marks)**
- Counters are incremented appropriately; output is formatted clearly. **(2 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (b)

```python
import math

C0 = 50.0
k = 0.2
C_thresh = 10.0

t = 0
C = C0

print(f"t = {t} hours, C = {C:.2f} mg/L")

while C >= C_thresh:
    t = t + 1
    C = C0 * math.exp(-k * t)
    print(f"t = {t} hours, C = {C:.2f} mg/L")

print(f"\nConcentration first falls below {C_thresh} mg/L at t = {t} hours")
```

**Mathematical verification:**

Setting $C(t) < C_{\text{thresh}}$:

$$50 e^{-0.2t} < 10 \implies e^{-0.2t} < 0.2 \implies -0.2t < \ln(0.2)$$

$$\implies t > \frac{-\ln(0.2)}{0.2} = \frac{1.6094}{0.2} \approx 8.047$$

So the first integer hour is $t = 9$. The `while` loop will output concentrations for $t = 0$ through $t = 9$ and then exit.

**Explanation of logic (for 7 marks):**
- Correct initialization of `t` and `C`. **(1 mark)**
- Use of `math.exp()` for the exponential decay. **(2 marks)**
- `while` loop continues **as long as** `C >= C_thresh`. **(1 mark)**
- Loop variable `t` is updated **inside** the body to prevent infinite looping. **(2 marks)**
- Print statement and final result message. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Write a Python program that uses a `for` loop to compute the **mean** and **standard deviation** of a list of $n$ blood pressure readings (systolic, in mmHg). The formulas are:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i, \quad \sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}}$$

Display whether each reading is **Normal** ($< 120$ mmHg), **Elevated** ($120$–$129$ mmHg), or **Hypertensive** ($\geq 130$ mmHg) using a conditional. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A biomedical imaging system performs iterative denoising. The error after the $k$-th iteration is given by:

$$E_k = E_0 \cdot r^{k}$$

where $E_0 = 1.0$ (initial error) and $r = 0.7$ (decay factor). Using a `while` loop, find the **number of iterations** required to bring the error below $10^{-3}$. Print the error at each iteration. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (a)

```python
import math

n = int(input("Enter number of readings: "))
readings = []

for i in range(n):
    x = float(input(f"Enter systolic BP reading {i+1}: "))
    readings.append(x)

# Compute mean
total = 0
for x in readings:
    total = total + x
mean_bp = total / n

# Compute standard deviation
sq_diff = 0
for x in readings:
    sq_diff = sq_diff + (x - mean_bp) ** 2
std_bp = math.sqrt(sq_diff / n)

print(f"\nMean BP = {mean_bp:.2f} mmHg")
print(f"Std Dev BP = {std_bp:.2f} mmHg\n")

# Classify each reading
for x in readings:
    if x < 120:
        cat = "Normal"
    elif x < 130:
        cat = "Elevated"
    else:
        cat = "Hypertensive"
    print(f"BP = {x} mmHg -> {cat}")
```

**Explanation (7 marks):** Correctly initializing accumulators (1), applying summation formulas in a `for` loop (2), using `math.sqrt()` for standard deviation (1), nested `for` loop with `if-elif-else` for classification (2), formatting output (1).

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (b)

```python
E0 = 1.0
r = 0.7
threshold = 1e-3
k = 0
E = E0

print(f"k = {k}, E = {E:.6f}")

while E >= threshold:
    k = k + 1
    E = E0 * (r ** k)
    print(f"k = {k}, E = {E:.6f}")

print(f"\nError falls below {threshold} after {k} iterations")
```

**Mathematical verification:**

Set $E_k < 10^{-3}$:

$$0.7^{k} < 10^{-3} \implies k \cdot \ln(0.7) < \ln(10^{-3})$$

Since $\ln(0.7) < 0$, dividing flips the inequality:

$$k > \frac{\ln(10^{-3})}{\ln(0.7)} = \frac{-6.9078}{-0.3567} \approx 19.36$$

So the first integer iteration is $k = 20$. The `while` loop terminates after 20 iterations.

**Explanation (7 marks):** Initialization of variables (1), exponential update inside loop body (2), correct use of `>=` in `while` condition (1), update of counter `k` (1), final print statement (2).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (Forensic Analysis):**
>
> 1. **Indentation Errors** — Python uses 4-space indentation. Mixed tabs/spaces will cause `IndentationError`. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Missing colon (`:`)** after `if`, `elif`, `else`, `for`, `while` — syntax error. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 3. **Using `=` instead of `==`** in the condition of `if` or `while` — this is an *assignment*, not a comparison, and causes logic error or infinite loop. Loss: **3 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting to update the loop variable** in a `while` loop → infinite loop. Loss: **3 marks**.
> 5. **Using `range(stop)` incorrectly** — students often forget that `stop` is **exclusive** and write `range(1, n)` expecting to include $n$. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 6. **Boundary errors in `if-elif`** — e.g., writing `if x < 25: ... elif x < 30: ...` and forgetting to handle `x == 25` and `x == 30` in the intended category. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 7. **No output formatting** — leaving the answer as a bare integer/float without context. Examiners expect a descriptive `print()` statement. Loss: **1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exams:**

- ✅ `if-elif-else` uses **top-down evaluation**; only **one branch** executes.
- ✅ The `else` block is **optional** but acts as a catch-all.
- ✅ `for` loop is for **definite iteration** over an iterable (list, tuple, string, `range()`).
- ✅ `while` loop is for **indefinite iteration** based on a Boolean condition.
- ✅ `range(a, b, s)` is **exclusive** of $b$; default `start = 0`, `step = 1$.
- ✅ `break` exits the **entire loop**; `continue` skips the **current iteration**.
- ✅ Always **initialize** the loop variable before `while` and **update** it inside the body.
- ✅ Comparison uses `==`, `!=`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`; assignment uses `=`.
- ✅ Logical operators: `and`, `or`, `not` — they combine Boolean expressions.
- ✅ Indentation (4 spaces) is **syntactically mandatory** in Python.
- ✅ Nested loops multiply the total iterations: $T_{\text{total}} = m \times n$ for an $m \times n$ structure.
- ✅ For biomedical data: prefer `for` for fixed-length signals, `while` for iterative solvers and real-time loops.
- ✅ Boundary classification (e.g., BMI, BP, temperature) requires careful ordering of `if-elif` conditions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defining and Calling Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT636 - COMPUTATIONAL METHODS IN BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Defining and Calling Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of computational programming and algorithm design, a **function** is a named, self-contained, and reusable block of statements that performs a specific, well-defined computational task. A function may accept zero or more inputs (called **parameters** or **arguments**), process them through a sequence of operations, and optionally return a computed result to the caller.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Computational Methods in Biomedical Engineering*, functions constitute the fundamental modular building block used to decompose complex biomedical signal-processing, image-analysis, and statistical-computation problems into smaller, manageable, and logically independent units.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A function is a modular, reusable, and parameterized block of code that encapsulates a specific computational task, allowing systematic decomposition of complex biomedical algorithms (such as ECG filtering, FFT computation, or image segmentation) into testable, debuggable, and maintainable components.

### 1.2 Components of a Function

A complete function definition contains five canonical components:

| Component | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Function Header** | Declares the function name, return type, and parameter list |
| **Parameters (Formal Arguments)** | Variables that receive values from the calling environment |
| **Function Body** | The sequence of statements that perform the actual computation |
| **Return Statement** | Specifies the value to be sent back to the caller |
| **Scope (Local Namespace)** | The isolated variable environment created upon invocation |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vending Machine" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** A function behaves exactly like a **vending machine** in a railway station.

| Vending Machine Analogy | Programming Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The machine itself with a name plate | Function name and definition |
| The coin slot and selection buttons | Parameters / arguments (inputs) |
| The internal mechanism and inventory | Function body (computation logic) |
| The dispensed can or bottle | Return value (output) |
| Inserting a coin and pressing a button | Function call (invocation) |
| The receipt (if any) | Side effects or global variable modifications |

Just as a single vending machine design can serve thousands of customers (function can be called many times), a well-defined function in biomedical software can process millions of ECG samples with a single line of code in the calling program.

---

## 1.4 Why Functions are Indispensable in Biomedical Engineering

In biomedical computational applications, the same mathematical operation must often be repeated across thousands to millions of data points. For example:

- Computing the **mean arterial pressure (MAP)** from systolic and diastolic readings for every patient in a database.
- Applying a **low-pass Butterworth filter** to each sample of a 24-hour Holter ECG recording.
- Calculating the **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** for every epoch in a polysomnography (PSG) sleep study.

> [!TIP]
> Without functions, the same code would be duplicated thousands of times, making the program **slow**, **error-prone**, and **impossible to maintain**. Functions convert repetition into a single reusable definition.

---

## 1.5 Defining vs. Calling — The Core Distinction

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly emphasizes two distinct operations:

1. **Defining a Function (Function Definition / Declaration):** Writing the actual code block that specifies *what* the function does. This is done **once**.
2. **Calling a Function (Function Invocation):** Executing the function from elsewhere in the program using its name followed by parentheses containing any required arguments. This can be done **multiple times**.

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU examiner pitfall: students often confuse **defining** with **calling**. A function that is defined but never called simply exists as dormant code — it performs no action until explicitly invoked.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 The Function Lifecycle — Four Sequential Phases

Every function interaction in a program follows a strict four-phase lifecycle. Understanding each phase is essential for KTU board examination answers.

### Phase 1: Function Declaration / Prototype
Before use, the compiler or interpreter must be informed of the function's existence. In languages like C, this is a *prototype* statement. In Python (which is the primary tool in PEEBT636), this is implicit when the `def` block is encountered.

### Phase 2: Function Definition
The complete body of operations is written and stored in memory. This includes parameter binding placeholders, local variable declarations, and the return logic.

### Phase 3: Function Call (Invocation)
The control of execution transfers from the *caller* to the *function entry point*. The actual arguments are evaluated and bound to the formal parameters.

### Phase 4: Return and Control Restoration
Once the function finishes (either by reaching a `return` statement or the end of the body), the computed result (if any) is sent back to the caller, and execution continues from the line immediately after the call.

---

## 2.2 Parameter Passing Mechanisms

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists three parameter passing techniques:

| Mechanism | Description | Use Case in Biomedical Computing |
|---|---|---|
| **Positional Arguments** | Arguments matched to parameters by their order in the definition | `process_signal(signal, fs=250)` |
| **Keyword Arguments** | Arguments passed by explicitly naming the parameter | `process_signal(data, cutoff=40, order=4)` |
| **Default Arguments** | Parameters with predefined fallback values | `def filter(signal, order=2, type='lowpass')` |

---

## 2.3 Types of Functions

The KTU syllabus categorizes functions as follows:

1. **Built-in Functions** — Predefined by the language (e.g., `len()`, `print()`, `abs()`, `sum()`)
2. **User-Defined Functions (UDF)** — Written by the programmer for specific biomedical tasks
3. **Lambda / Anonymous Functions** — Single-expression inline functions (e.g., `lambda x: x**2`)
4. **Recursive Functions** — Functions that call themselves (e.g., computing factorial, Fibonacci)
5. **Higher-Order Functions** — Functions that accept or return other functions (e.g., `map()`, `filter()`)

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula & Syntax Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the high-yield syntax elements tested in KTU university examinations for PEEBT636.

| Element | General Syntax Form | Biomedical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Function Definition** | `def name(params): body` | `def compute_mean(signal): return sum(signal)/len(signal)` |
| **Function Call (Positional)** | `name(arg1, arg2)` | `compute_mean(ecg_data)` |
| **Function Call (Keyword)** | `name(param=value)` | `filter(cutoff=40, order=4)` |
| **Default Parameter** | `def f(x, y=10)` | `def amplify(signal, gain=2.0)` |
| **Return Statement** | `return value` | `return filtered_signal` |
| **Multiple Returns (Tuple)** | `return a, b, c` | `return mean, std, peak` |
| **Docstring** | `"""description"""` | Documents the function for clinical use |
| **Lambda Form** | `lambda args: expr` | `squared = lambda x: x**2` |
| **Recursion Base Case** | `if condition: return value` | `if n==0: return 1` |
| **Global Declaration** | `global var_name` | For sharing state across functions |

---

## 2.5 Scope and Lifetime of Variables

- **Local Scope:** Variables declared inside a function exist only during its execution and are destroyed upon return.
- **Global Scope:** Variables declared at the module level are accessible throughout the file.
- **Enclosing (Closure) Scope:** Relevant for nested functions where inner functions can access outer function variables.
- **Built-in Scope:** Contains pre-defined identifiers like `print`, `len`, `range`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lifetime ≠ Scope.** A global variable has program-wide scope but exists for the entire program lifetime. A local variable has function-only scope but exists only during that single invocation.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
