# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part A pattern: 2-mark or 3-mark short questions, answer **any 5 out of 7** in the actual ESE. For this section, 3-mark direct conceptual questions with crisp model answers are presented.

### Question A1 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Style]**
**State the Intermediate Value Theorem. Why is it essential for the Bisection method?**

**Model Answer:**
The Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT) states that if $f$ is continuous on a closed interval $[a, b]$ and $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$, then there exists at least one $\alpha \in (a, b)$ such that $f(\alpha) = 0$.

The IVT is the **theoretical foundation** of the Bisection method because it **guarantees the existence of a root** inside any initial bracket where the function values at the endpoints have opposite signs. This guarantee is what allows Bisection to *unconditionally* halve the interval and retain the bracketing property at every step, ensuring convergence irrespective of how complex $f$ is, as long as continuity and a sign change are present. **(3 marks: 1 for IVT statement, 1 for linking to bisection, 1 for convergence guarantee)**

### Question A2 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, June 2024 Style]**
**State the Newton-Raphson iteration formula. List two conditions under which it fails.**

**Model Answer:**
The Newton-Raphson iteration formula is:

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$$

**Two failure conditions:**
1. **Zero derivative:** If $f'(x_n) = 0$ (or is numerically negligible) at the current iterate, the formula involves division by zero, leading to a vertical tangent and an undefined next step.
2. **Poor initial guess:** If $x_0$ is far from the true root, the tangent line at $x_0$ may intersect the x-axis at a point that is *further* from the root, causing **divergence** or **cycling** into a chaotic orbit.

**(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 each for two failure modes)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B pattern: Each question carries **14 marks**, with sub-parts typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The examiner provides an internal choice between two questions.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the Bisection method with a clear algorithm and a flowchart description. State its order of convergence and derive the iteration count needed to achieve a tolerance $\varepsilon$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Bisection method, find the root of $f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5$ in the interval $[2, 3]$ correct to **2 decimal places**. Show all iterations in a table. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Bisection method explanation (7 marks):**

- **Definition** (1 mark): Bisection is a bracketing method that repeatedly halves the interval $[a, b]$ containing a root of a continuous function $f$.
- **Algorithm** (3 marks): Steps — verify $f(a) f(b) < 0$ → compute $c = (a+b)/2$ → check $f(c) \cdot f(a)$ → update bracket → repeat until $\vert b - a \vert < \varepsilon$.
- **Order of convergence** (1 mark): Linear, with convergence factor $1/2$.
- **Iteration-count derivation** (2 marks): After $n$ iterations, the interval length is $(b-a)/2^n$. Requiring this $\leq \varepsilon$ gives $n \geq \log_2((b-a)/\varepsilon)$.

**(b) Bisection computation (7 marks):** Present iterations in a structured table:

| $n$ | $a$ | $b$ | $c = (a+b)/2$ | $f(c)$ | New Bracket |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.000 | 3.000 | 2.5000 | $+5.6250$ | $[2.000,\ 2.500]$ |
| 2 | 2.000 | 2.500 | 2.2500 | $+1.8906$ | $[2.000,\ 2.250]$ |
| 3 | 2.000 | 2.250 | 2.1250 | $+0.3457$ | $[2.000,\ 2.125]$ |
| 4 | 2.000 | 2.125 | 2.0625 | $-0.3521$ | $[2.0625,\ 2.125]$ |
| 5 | 2.0625 | 2.125 | 2.09375 | $-0.0069$ | $[2.09375,\ 2.125]$ |
| 6 | 2.09375 | 2.125 | 2.10938 | $+0.1670$ | $[2.09375,\ 2.10938]$ |
| 7 | 2.09375 | 2.10938 | 2.10156 | $+0.0800$ | $[2.09375,\ 2.10156]$ |
| 8 | 2.09375 | 2.10156 | 2.09766 | $+0.0365$ | $[2.09375,\ 2.09766]$ |

Stopping when $\vert b - a \vert = 0.0039 < 0.01$: $\alpha \approx 2.094$.

(7 marks: 1 for table headers, 4 for correct entries, 1 for sign-change logic, 1 for final answer and stopping condition.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:**
> - Do **not** forget to verify $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$ before starting — **(−1 mark)**.
> - Always show the sign of $f(c)$ in the table; the bracket-update logic depends on it — **(−1 mark)** if omitted.
> - State the **stopping criterion explicitly** — common 2-mark deduction.
> - Do not truncate the iteration count early; tabulate until the tolerance is **met**, not just until 4–5 rows are filled.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2 — Internal Choice Alternative]**

**(a)** Derive the Newton-Raphson iteration formula from the first-order Taylor series expansion. State its order of convergence with a brief justification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The plasma concentration of a drug after IV administration follows $C(t) = 80 e^{-0.2 t} - 20$ (mg/L, $t$ in hours). Using Newton-Raphson with $t_0 = 5$ h, find the time $t$ at which the drug concentration becomes zero. Perform **3 iterations** and present results in a table. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Derivation of Newton-Raphson (7 marks):**

Let $\alpha$ be the true root, and $x_n$ the current approximation. First-order Taylor expansion about $x_n$:

$$f(\alpha) = f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(\alpha - x_n) + O\!\left((\alpha - x_n)^2\right)$$

Since $f(\alpha) = 0$, dropping the higher-order term:

$$0 = f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(\alpha - x_n)$$

Solving for $\alpha$:

$$\alpha = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$$

Since $\alpha$ is approximated by $x_{n+1}$:

$$\boxed{\,x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}\,}$$

**Order of convergence — Quadratic (2):** Substituting $x_n = \alpha + e_n$ in the full second-order Taylor expansion gives $e_{n+1} = \dfrac{f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} e_n^2$, showing the error is squared each step. Hence order 2 (quadratic). **(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for derivation, 1 for final boxed formula, 2 for order proof)**

**(b) Newton-Raphson computation (7 marks):**

For $C(t) = 80 e^{-0.2 t} - 20$:
- $f(t) = 80 e^{-0.2 t} - 20$
- $f'(t) = -16 e^{-0.2 t}$
- Iteration: $t_{n+1} = t_n - \dfrac{80 e^{-0.2 t_n} - 20}{-16 e^{-0.2 t_n}} = t_n + \dfrac{80 e^{-0.2 t_n} - 20}{16 e^{-0.2 t_n}}$

| $n$ | $t_n$ (h) | $f(t_n)$ | $f'(t_n)$ | $t_{n+1}$ (h) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 5.000 | $+9.436$ | $-5.887$ | $6.603$ |
| 1 | 6.603 | $+1.367$ | $-4.273$ | $6.923$ |
| 2 | 6.923 | $+0.027$ | $-4.005$ | $6.930$ |

Stopping after 3 iterations: $\alpha \approx 6.930$ hours. **(7 marks: 1 for derivative, 2 for iteration formula, 3 for table, 1 for final answer)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:**
> - Forgetting to **compute $f'(t)$ explicitly** in part (b) is a **2-mark** deduction — examiners check for this step before reading the table.
> - In part (a), writing only the final formula **without the Taylor-series derivation** loses **4 out of 7 marks** — derivation is mandatory.
> - Sign errors in the table (e.g., writing $-9.436$ instead of $+9.436$) propagate to wrong $t_{n+1}$; **double-check signs**.
> - For the order-of-convergence sub-part, students often write "order 2" without showing the error relation $e_{n+1} = C e_n^2$ — this loses **1.5 marks** in the KTU valuation key.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2**

- **Root-finding problem** asks: find $\alpha$ such that $f(\alpha) = 0$.
- **Bisection** is bracketing, requires $f(a) f(b) < 0$, convergence is **linear** (factor $1/2$), and iterations needed = $\lceil \log_2((b-a)/\varepsilon) \rceil$.
- **Newton-Raphson** is open, uses the tangent line: $x_{n+1} = x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n)$, convergence is **quadratic**, but may **diverge** for poor $x_0$ or near $f'(x) = 0$.
- **Secant method** approximates the derivative using the secant slope; super-linear convergence (~1.618), no derivative required.
- **False-Position (Regula Falsi)** uses linear interpolation between $(a, f(a))$ and $(b, f(b))$; preserves the bracket but may stagnate on one side.
- **Gauss Elimination** = forward elimination + back substitution; pivoting is needed for numerical stability.
- **LU Decomposition** = factorise $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{LU}$; useful for multiple right-hand sides.
- **Multivariate Newton-Raphson** uses the **Jacobian** matrix; solve $\mathbf{J} \Delta \mathbf{x} = -\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})$ at each iteration.
- **Stopping criteria**: $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < \varepsilon$, $\vert f(x_{n+1}) \vert < \varepsilon$, or maximum iteration count.
- **Biomedical applications**: pharmacokinetic clearance times, ECG R-peak detection, biomechanical equilibrium, tumor doubling time, hemodynamic pressure-flow calculations.
- **Always verify** the bracket condition $f(a) f(b) < 0$ for Bisection, and **always compute $f'(x)$** for Newton-Raphson.
- **KTU 2024 valuation pattern**: Show the **algorithm, the iteration table, and the stopping-criterion check** in every long-answer numerical problem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs) and Their Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT636 - COMPUTATIONAL METHODS IN BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs) and Their Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of an Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** An **Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE)** is a mathematical equation that relates an unknown function of **one independent variable** to its derivatives with respect to that variable. The general form is:
>
> $$F\!\left(x,\; y,\; \frac{dy}{dx},\; \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}},\;\dots,\; \frac{d^{n}y}{dx^{n}}\right) = 0$$

- **Order** of an ODE $\;\rightarrow\;$ the order of the highest derivative present.
- **Degree** $\;\rightarrow\;$ the power of the highest-order derivative, **after** clearing fractions and radicals.
- **Linear ODE** $\;\rightarrow\;$ of the form $a_n(x)y^{(n)} + a_{n-1}(x)y^{(n-1)} + \dots + a_1(x)y' + a_0(x)y = r(x)$.
- **Non-Linear ODE** $\;\rightarrow\;$ contains products, powers, or transcendental functions of $y$ or its derivatives.

### 1.2 Classification Snapshot

| Category | Example Equation | Order | Linear? |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| First-order Linear | $\dfrac{dy}{dx} + P(x)y = Q(x)$ | 1 | Yes |
| Separable | $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{x}{y}$ | 1 | No (but reducible) |
| Second-order Constant Coeff. | $y'' + 4y' + 3y = e^{x}$ | 2 | Yes |
| Non-linear (Logistic) | $\dfrac{dN}{dt} = rN\!\left(1-\dfrac{N}{K}\right)$ | 1 | No |

> [!NOTE]
> **Initial Value Problem (IVP):** When the value of the solution (or its derivatives up to order $n-1$) is specified at a single point $x = x_0$, the ODE plus these conditions is called an **IVP**. In biomedical modeling, the value at $t = 0$ usually represents the baseline state of a patient or biological system.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Why ODEs Matter in Biomedical Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — The "Slope Tape Recorder":**
> Imagine you are blindfolded and walking along a hill. You cannot see the **height** $y$, but a friend whispers in your ear the **slope** of the ground $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ at every point. Can you reconstruct the hill? **Yes** — provided you remember your starting height $y(x_0) = y_0$. An ODE is exactly that: a rule about the **rate of change** of a biological quantity (drug concentration, cell population, membrane voltage), and integration reconstructs the full story from an initial condition.

**Real-world biomedical meanings of the derivative $\dfrac{dy}{dt}$:**

- Drug concentration $\;\rightarrow\;$ rate of elimination by the kidneys/liver.
- Cell population $\;\rightarrow\;$ rate of mitosis minus apoptosis.
- Membrane voltage $\;\rightarrow\;$ rate of ion flow across a neuron.
- Body temperature $\;\rightarrow\;$ rate of heat exchange with surroundings.

### 1.4 Physical Constants Used in Biomedical ODE Models

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $k$ | Elimination rate constant | $\mathbf{0.1 \text{ to } 1.0}$ | $\text{hr}^{-1}$ |
| $V_d$ | Volume of distribution | $\mathbf{0.6 \text{ L/kg}}$ (approx. body water) | $\text{L/kg}$ |
| $R$ | Universal gas constant | $\mathbf{8.314}$ | $\text{J/(mol·K)}$ |
| $F$ | Faraday constant | $\mathbf{96{,}485}$ | $\text{C/mol}$ |
| $r$ | Cell growth rate | $\mathbf{0.02 \text{ to } 0.5}$ | $\text{hr}^{-1}$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 First-Order ODE Solution Strategies

**(a) Separable Equations** — when the equation can be written as $M(y)\,dy = N(x)\,dx$.

**(b) Linear First-Order** — use the **Integrating Factor (IF)**:

$$IF = e^{\int P(x)\,dx}$$

The standard form is $y' + P(x)y = Q(x)$, and the solution is:

$$y \cdot IF = \int Q(x) \cdot IF \, dx + C$$

**(c) Exact Equations** — when $\dfrac{\partial M}{\partial y} = \dfrac{\partial N}{\partial x}$.

**(d) Homogeneous Equations** — when $f(x,y)$ is a function of $y/x$ only; substitute $y = vx$.

### 2.2 Higher-Order Linear ODEs with Constant Coefficients

For $a_n y^{(n)} + a_{n-1}y^{(n-1)} + \dots + a_0 y = 0$, the **Auxiliary Equation (AE)** is formed by replacing $D^k$ with $m^k$:

$$a_n m^n + a_{n-1} m^{n-1} + \dots + a_0 = 0$$

**Roots dictate the Complementary Function $y_c$:**

| Nature of Roots | Form of Term in $y_c$ |
| :--- | :--- |
| Real & distinct $m_1, m_2$ | $C_1 e^{m_1 x} + C_2 e^{m_2 x}$ |
| Real & repeated $m$ (multiplicity $k$) | $(C_1 + C_2 x + \dots + C_k x^{k-1})\,e^{mx}$ |
| Complex $m = \alpha \pm i\beta$ | $e^{\alpha x}\bigl(C_1 \cos\beta x + C_2 \sin\beta x\bigr)$ |

The **Particular Integral (PI)** $y_p$ is found by inspection, the method of undetermined coefficients, or variation of parameters.

### 2.3 Numerical Methods for IVPs (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For most biomedical ODEs, **analytical solutions do not exist**. We therefore use numerical methods to march from $t_n$ to $t_{n+1}$ using step size $h = t_{n+1} - t_n$.

**Euler's Method (first-order accurate, $O(h)$):**

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + h\, f(t_n, y_n)$$

**Modified Euler / Heun's Method (second-order, $O(h^2)$):**

$$y_{n+1}^{*} = y_n + h\, f(t_n, y_n) \quad \text{(Predictor)}$$
$$y_{n+1} = y_n + \tfrac{h}{2}\bigl[f(t_n, y_n) + f(t_{n+1}, y_{n+1}^{*})\bigr] \quad \text{(Corrector)}$$

**Classical 4th-Order Runge–Kutta (RK4) — $O(h^4)$ accuracy, the KTU favourite:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= h\, f(t_n,\; y_n) \\
k_2 &= h\, f\!\left(t_n + \tfrac{h}{2},\; y_n + \tfrac{k_1}{2}\right) \\
k_3 &= h\, f\!\left(t_n + \tfrac{h}{2},\; y_n + \tfrac{k_2}{2}\right) \\
k_4 &= h\, f(t_n + h,\; y_n + k_3) \\
y_{n+1} &= y_n + \tfrac{1}{6}(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why RK4 dominates biomedical simulation:** It gives high accuracy without requiring derivatives of $f$, which is crucial when $f(t,y)$ comes from a complex physiological model (e.g., Hodgkin–Huxley nerve equations).

### 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet — ODE Master Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table collects **every formula you must memorize** for Module-3 KTU questions.

| $\#$ | Equation / Method | Final Formula | Typical Use in Biomedicine |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | First-order linear IF | $y \cdot e^{\int P dx} = \int Q e^{\int P dx} dx + C$ | Drug absorption |
| 2 | Exponential decay | $y(t) = y_0 e^{-kt}$ | Drug clearance |
| 3 | Logistic growth | $N(t) = \dfrac{K}{1 + Ae^{-rt}}$ | Tumor / bacterial growth |
| 4 | Newton's law of cooling | $T(t) = T_s + (T_0 - T_s)e^{-kt}$ | Thermal regulation |
| 5 | RC circuit charge | $V(t) = V_0 e^{-t/RC}$ | Pacemaker / defibrillator |
| 6 | Euler step | $y_{n+1} = y_n + h f_n$ | Quick approx. |
| 7 | Modified Euler (Heun) | $y_{n+1} = y_n + \tfrac{h}{2}(f_n + f_{n+1}^{*})$ | Improved Euler |
| 8 | RK4 update | $y_{n+1} = y_n + \tfrac{1}{6}(k_1+2k_2+2k_3+k_4)$ | High-accuracy biomedical sim |
| 9 | Local Truncation Error (LTE) Euler | $LTE = \dfrac{h^2}{2}y''(\xi)$ | Error estimation |
| 10 | LTE for RK4 | $LTE = O(h^5)$ | Very low error |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb for biomedical RK4:** When solving stiff equations (e.g., nerve action potentials), implicit methods or adaptive step-size RK45 are preferred — but KTU 2024 Module-3 questions only require classical RK4.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Pharmacokinetics** — predicting plasma drug concentration vs. time.
- **Bioheat transfer (Pennes' equation simplification)** — thermal dose in hyperthermia treatment.
- **Compartmental analysis** — iodine tracer movement in thyroid studies.
- **Population dynamics** — cancer cell proliferation, bacterial colonies.
- **Circuit analogs of physiological systems** — RC circuit $\equiv$ first-order drug kinetics.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Solution of a First-Order Linear ODE (Pharmacokinetic Application)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example Problem:** A single intravenous bolus of a drug produces the ODE
> $$\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -k\,C, \qquad C(0) = C_0$$
> where $C(t)$ is the plasma concentration (mg/L) and $k$ is the elimination rate constant (hr$^{-1}$). Find $C(t)$ analytically.

**Step 1 — Identify the ODE type.**
The equation can be rewritten as $\dfrac{dC}{dt} + k\,C = 0$. Comparing with the standard form $\dfrac{dy}{dx} + P(x)y = Q(x)$, we have $P(t) = k$ and $Q(t) = 0$.

**Step 2 — Compute the Integrating Factor (IF).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IF &= e^{\int P(t)\, dt} \\
   &= e^{\int k\, dt} \\
   &= e^{kt}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply the standard form by the IF.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{kt}\,\dfrac{dC}{dt} + k\,e^{kt}\,C &= 0 \\
\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(e^{kt}\,C\right) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

This works because the LHS collapses into a **perfect derivative** — the defining property of the IF.

**Step 4 — Integrate both sides with respect to $t$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int \dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(e^{kt}\,C\right) dt &= \int 0\, dt \\
e^{kt}\,C &= A \quad \text{(A is the integration constant)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the initial condition $C(0) = C_0$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{k(0)}\,C(0) &= A \\
1 \cdot C_0 &= A \\
A &= C_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve explicitly for $C(t)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{kt}\,C &= C_0 \\
C(t) &= C_0\,e^{-kt}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomedical interpretation:** This is the **mono-exponential decay** curve used to estimate a drug's **half-life** $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$. For example, if $k = 0.1\;\text{hr}^{-1}$, then $t_{1/2} \approx 6.93$ hours — typical of many antibiotics.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Euler's Method from the Taylor Series

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Taylor expansion of $y(t)$ about $t_n$ is:
> $$y(t_{n+1}) = y(t_n) + h\,y'(t_n) + \dfrac{h^2}{2!}y''(t_n) + \dfrac{h^3}{3!}y'''(t_n) + \dots$$

**Step 1 — Recognize that for an ODE $y' = f(t,y)$.**

So $y'(t_n) = f(t_n, y_n)$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t_{n+1}) &= y(t_n) + h\,f(t_n,y_n) + \dfrac{h^2}{2!}f'(t_n,y_n) + \dots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Truncate after the first derivative term.**
Dropping all $h^2$ and higher terms:

$$
y(t_{n+1}) \;\approx\; y(t_n) + h\,f(t_n,y_n)
$$

**Step 3 — Replace continuous $y(t_n)$ by numerical estimate $y_n$.**

$$
y_{n+1} = y_n + h\,f(t_n,y_n)
$$

This is **Euler's Method.** The local truncation error is dominated by the first dropped term:

$$
LTE = \dfrac{h^2}{2}\,y''(\xi) = O(h^2) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{global error} = O(h)
$$

---

### 3.3 Full RK4 Derivation (Detailed Step-Wise)

The goal: approximate $y(t_{n+1})$ with an update that is **fourth-order accurate**, but uses only function evaluations at intermediate points.

**Step 1 — General 4-stage Runge–Kutta form.**

$$
y_{n+1} = y_n + a_1 k_1 + a_2 k_2 + a_3 k_3 + a_4 k_4
$$

where each $k_i = h\, f(t_n + c_i h,\; y_n + \text{combination of previous } k_j)$.

**Step 2 — Match Taylor coefficients to obtain the classical RK4 (Butcher tableau).**

By requiring the method to reproduce the Taylor series up to $h^4$, the following unique coefficients emerge:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= h\, f(t_n,\, y_n) \\
k_2 &= h\, f\!\left(t_n + \tfrac{h}{2},\, y_n + \tfrac{k_1}{2}\right) \\
k_3 &= h\, f\!\left(t_n + \tfrac{h}{2},\, y_n + \tfrac{k_2}{2}\right) \\
k_4 &= h\, f(t_n + h,\, y_n + k_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Final weighted combination.**

$$
y_{n+1} = y_n + \tfrac{1}{6}(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory trick:** $k_2$ and $k_3$ are *midpoint-like* evaluations; $k_1$ and $k_4$ are *endpoint* evaluations. The weights $(1, 2, 2, 1)$ mimic Simpson's 1/3 rule for integration.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection Guide

| Property | Euler | Modified Euler | RK4 |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Order of accuracy | $O(h)$ | $O(h^2)$ | $O(h^4)$ |
| Function evals per step | 1 | 2 | 4 |
| Stability (good for stiff?) | No | No | No (use RK45 for stiff) |
| Recommended $h$ for KTU problems | $0.01$ | $0.05$ | $0.1$ |
| Effort (hand computation) | Lowest | Medium | High |
| KTU exam frequency | High | Medium | Very High |

---

### 3.5 Second-Order ODE: Damped Harmonic Oscillator (Biomechanical Model)

The equation $\;m\,\ddot{x} + c\,\dot{x} + k\,x = 0\;$ models a **prosthetic limb suspension** or **arterial wall vibration**.

**Step 1 — Write the auxiliary equation.**

$$
m\,r^2 + c\,r + k = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $r$ using the quadratic formula.**

$$
r = \dfrac{-c \pm \sqrt{c^2 - 4mk}}{2m}
$$

**Step 3 — Three physical regimes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Overdamped:}\quad & c^2 > 4mk,\;\; x(t) = C_1 e^{r_1 t} + C_2 e^{r_2 t} \\
\text{(ii) Critically damped:}\quad & c^2 = 4mk,\;\; x(t) = (C_1 + C_2 t)\,e^{rt} \\
\text{(iii) Underdamped:}\quad & c^2 < 4mk,\;\; x(t) = e^{\alpha t}\bigl(C_1 \cos\omega_d t + C_2 \sin\omega_d t\bigr) \\
&\text{where}\;\; \alpha = -\tfrac{c}{2m},\;\;\; \omega_d = \tfrac{\sqrt{4mk - c^2}}{2m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomedical meaning:** Under-damped oscillation mimics **Korotkoff sounds** during blood pressure measurement. Critical damping is engineered into **forceps and prosthetics** for fastest return without overshoot.

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flowchart — ODE Classification & Solution Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start with ODE] --> B{Order?}
    B -- Order 1 --> C{Linear in y?}
    B -- Order >= 2 --> D{Constant coefficients?}
    C -- Yes --> E[Integrating Factor Method]
    C -- No --> F{Separable?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Separate and Integrate]
    F -- No --> H{Exact?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Integrate M dx plus N dy]
    H -- No --> J[Homogeneous? Bernoulli?]
    D -- Yes --> K[Form Auxiliary Equation]
    K --> L{Roots?}
    L -- Real distinct --> M[CF: C1 e m1 x plus C2 e m2 x]
    L -- Repeated --> N[CF: C1 plus C2 x times e mx]
    L -- Complex --> O[CF: e alpha x C1 cos bx plus C2 sin bx]
    M --> P[Add Particular Integral]
    N --> P
    O --> P
    E --> Q[Apply Initial Conditions]
    G --> Q
    I --> Q
    J --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Final Closed Form Solution]
```

### 4.2 RK4 Algorithm Block Diagram (Computational Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start with t0 y0 h and target t final] --> S2[Set t = t0 and y = y0]
    S2 --> S3[Compute k1 = h f of t and y]
    S3 --> S4[Compute k2 = h f of t plus h over 2 and y plus k1 over 2]
    S4 --> S5[Compute k3 = h f of t plus h over 2 and y plus k2 over 2]
    S5 --> S6[Compute k4 = h f of t plus h and y plus k3]
    S6 --> S7[Update y = y plus 1 over 6 times k1 plus 2 k2 plus 2 k3 plus k4]
    S7 --> S8[Update t = t plus h]
    S8 --> S9{t greater than t final?}
    S9 -- No --> S3
    S9 -- Yes --> S10[Output solution trajectory]
```

### 4.3 Pharmacokinetic One-Compartment Model — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IV Bolus Dose D at t=0] --> B[Central Compartment Volume Vd]
    B -- Elimination rate kC --> C[Excretion via Kidneys]
    B -- Plasma Concentration C of t --> D[Measurement Site Blood Sample]
    D -- Feedback --> E[Adjust Dosing Regimen]
    B -- governed by dC over dt equals minus kC --> F[Solution C of t equals C0 e to power minus kt]
```

### 4.4 Comparison of Numerical Methods — Stability Landscape

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Choose Numerical Method] --> M2{Equation Stiff?}
    M2 -- No --> M3{Required Accuracy?}
    M3 -- Low --> M4[Euler Method O of h]
    M3 -- Medium --> M5[Modified Euler or RK2 O of h squared]
    M3 -- High --> M6[RK4 O of h to the fourth]
    M2 -- Yes --> M7[Implicit Backward Euler or RK45]
    M4 --> M8[Step Size h small for stability]
    M5 --> M8
    M6 --> M8
    M7 --> M9[Adaptive Step Size Control]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual summary for students:** Diagram 4.1 is your **decision-making map** in the exam — start at the top, follow the diamond branches, and you will always arrive at the correct solution strategy. Diagram 4.2 is the RK4 **algorithm you must reproduce** in long-answer questions.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank, Module 3]**
*Define the order and degree of an Ordinary Differential Equation. Classify the following equations:*
*(i) $\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2 + 5y = 0$*
*(ii) $\dfrac{d^3y}{dx^3} + 3\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \sin x$*

**Model Answer:**
- **Order** = highest-order derivative present. **Degree** = power of that derivative (when expressed as a polynomial in derivatives, with no radicals/fractions).
- (i) Highest derivative is $\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}$ raised to power **1** $\rightarrow$ **Order 2, Degree 1, Non-linear** (because of the squared first-derivative term).
- (ii) Highest derivative is $\dfrac{d^3y}{dx^3}$ raised to power **1** $\rightarrow$ **Order 3, Degree 1, Linear**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**
*State the classical 4th-order Runge–Kutta formula. Why is it preferred over Euler's method in biomedical simulations?*

**Model Answer:**
Given $y' = f(t,y)$ with $y(t_0) = y_0$ and step size $h$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= h f(t_n,\,y_n) \\
k_2 &= h f\!\left(t_n+\tfrac{h}{2},\,y_n+\tfrac{k_1}{2}\right) \\
k_3 &= h f\!\left(t_n+\tfrac{h}{2},\,y_n+\tfrac{k_2}{2}\right) \\
k_4 &= h f(t_n+h,\,y_n+k_3) \\
y_{n+1} &= y_n + \tfrac{1}{6}(k_1+2k_2+2k_3+k_4)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasons preferred in biomedical simulation:**
1. **High accuracy** ($O(h^4)$) — needed for predicting drug levels precisely.
2. **No derivative evaluations** of $f$ — only function values, simplifying implementation.
3. **Better stability** than Euler for the same step size.
4. **Less round-off error** for moderate $h$ — important when simulating long physiological time courses.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pattern:** Each Part-B question has two sub-parts, typically 7 marks each. You are required to answer **ONE full question** out of the two provided choices.

---

### CHOICE A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a)** A patient is administered an IV bolus dose of a drug. The plasma concentration follows:

$$\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -k\,C,\qquad C(0) = C_0$$

Solve this ODE analytically. If $C_0 = 100$ mg/L and $k = 0.2\;\text{hr}^{-1}$, find the concentration after 5 hours and the half-life of the drug. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Solve the same IVP using **Euler's Method** with step size $h = 1$ hr. Compute $C_1, C_2, C_3$ and compare with the analytical solution at $t = 3$ hr. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — CHOICE A

**Part (a) — Analytical Solution [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Separate variables.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{dC}{dt} &= -k\,C \\
\dfrac{dC}{C} &= -k\,dt
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate both sides.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int \dfrac{dC}{C} &= -k \int dt \\
\ln \vert C \vert &= -kt + A
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Exponentiate.**

$$
C(t) = e^{-kt + A} = B\,e^{-kt},\;\; \text{where } B = e^A
$$

**Step 4 — Apply initial condition $C(0) = C_0$.**

$$
C_0 = B\,e^{0} \;\Rightarrow\; B = C_0
$$

**Step 5 — Final analytical expression.**

$$
C(t) = C_0\,e^{-kt}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical substitution at $t = 5$ hr.**

$$
C(5) = 100 \cdot e^{-0.2 \times 5} = 100 \cdot e^{-1} \approx 100 \cdot 0.3679 = 36.79 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Step 7 — Half-life formula.**

$$
t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} = \dfrac{0.6931}{0.2} \approx 3.466 \text{ hr}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Euler's Method [7 Marks]**

**Algorithm:** $C_{n+1} = C_n + h \cdot f(t_n, C_n)$ where $f(t,C) = -k\,C$.

**Iteration 1 — compute $C_1$ at $t_1 = 1$ hr.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(t_0, C_0) &= -0.2 \times 100 = -20 \\
C_1 &= 100 + (1)(-20) = 80.00 \text{ mg/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2 — compute $C_2$ at $t_2 = 2$ hr.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(t_1, C_1) &= -0.2 \times 80 = -16 \\
C_2 &= 80 + (1)(-16) = 64.00 \text{ mg/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 3 — compute $C_3$ at $t_3 = 3$ hr.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(t_2, C_2) &= -0.2 \times 64 = -12.8 \\
C_3 &= 64 + (1)(-12.8) = 51.20 \text{ mg/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison at $t = 3$ hr:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Analytical: } & C(3) = 100\,e^{-0.6} = 54.88 \text{ mg/L} \\
\text{Euler: } & C_3 = 51.20 \text{ mg/L} \\
\text{Error: } & 54.88 - 51.20 = 3.68 \text{ mg/L} \;\; (\approx 6.7\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Inference:** Euler's method underestimates the concentration because the true slope decreases as $C$ decreases, but Euler uses the **initial** slope over the whole interval.

---

### CHOICE B — 14 Marks (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a)** Derive the **RC circuit charging equation** from first principles using Kirchhoff's voltage law and discuss its analogy to the **single-compartment pharmacokinetic model**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Apply **RK4** with $h = 0.5$ to solve $\dfrac{dy}{dt} = -2y + t$, $y(0) = 1$, for one full step. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — CHOICE B

**Part (a) — RC Circuit & PK Analogy [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL).**

For a series RC circuit with supply $V_s$:

$$
V_s = V_R + V_C = R\,i + \dfrac{q}{C}
$$

**Step 2 — Express current as $i = \dfrac{dq}{dt}$.**

$$
V_s = R\,\dfrac{dq}{dt} + \dfrac{q}{C}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to standard first-order linear form.**

$$
\dfrac{dq}{dt} + \dfrac{1}{RC}\,q = \dfrac{V_s}{R}
$$

**Step 4 — Integrating Factor method.**

$$
IF = e^{t/RC}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(q\,e^{t/RC}\right) &= \dfrac{V_s}{R}\,e^{t/RC} \\
q\,e^{t/RC} &= V_s\,C\,e^{t/RC} + K \\
q(t) &= V_s\,C + K\,e^{-t/RC}
\end{aligned}
$$

With $q(0) = 0$: $K = -V_s\,C$. Hence **voltage across capacitor:**

$$
V_C(t) = \dfrac{q(t)}{C} = V_s\!\left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Analogy with pharmacokinetics.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RC circuit:}\quad & V_C(t) = V_s(1 - e^{-t/RC}) \\
\text{Drug model:}\quad & C(t) = C_0\,e^{-kt} \quad (\text{decay}) \\
\text{Equivalent drug input:}\quad & C(t) = C_{\infty}(1 - e^{-kt}) \quad (\text{infusion})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The mapping:** $V_s \leftrightarrow$ constant infusion rate; $RC \leftrightarrow 1/k$ (time constant); $V_C \leftrightarrow$ plasma concentration. This is why electrical analog circuits are used to teach pharmacokinetics.

---

**Part (b) — RK4 Single Step [7 Marks]**

ODE: $\dfrac{dy}{dt} = f(t,y) = -2y + t$, $y(0) = 1$, $h = 0.5$.

**Compute $k_1$:**

$$
k_1 = h \cdot f(t_0,y_0) = 0.5 \cdot (-2(1) + 0) = 0.5 \cdot (-2) = -1.0000
$$

**Compute $k_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_0 + \tfrac{h}{2} &= 0 + 0.25 = 0.25 \\
y_0 + \tfrac{k_1}{2} &= 1 + (-0.5) = 0.5 \\
k_2 &= 0.5 \cdot f(0.25,\, 0.5) \\
    &= 0.5 \cdot (-2(0.5) + 0.25) \\
    &= 0.5 \cdot (-1 + 0.25) \\
    &= 0.5 \cdot (-0.75) = -0.3750
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute $k_3$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_0 + \tfrac{k_2}{2} &= 1 + (-0.1875) = 0.8125 \\
k_3 &= 0.5 \cdot f(0.25,\, 0.8125) \\
    &= 0.5 \cdot (-2(0.8125) + 0.25) \\
    &= 0.5 \cdot (-1.625 + 0.25) \\
    &= 0.5 \cdot (-1.375) = -0.6875
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute $k_4$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_0 + h &= 0.5 \\
y_0 + k_3 &= 1 + (-0.6875) = 0.3125 \\
k_4 &= 0.5 \cdot f(0.5,\, 0.3125) \\
    &= 0.5 \cdot (-2(0.3125) + 0.5) \\
    &= 0.5 \cdot (-0.625 + 0.5) \\
    &= 0.5 \cdot (-0.125) = -0.0625
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final RK4 update:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1 &= y_0 + \tfrac{1}{6}(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4) \\
    &= 1 + \tfrac{1}{6}\bigl(-1.0000 + 2(-0.3750) + 2(-0.6875) + (-0.0625)\bigr) \\
    &= 1 + \tfrac{1}{6}(-1.0000 - 0.7500 - 1.3750 - 0.0625) \\
    &= 1 + \tfrac{1}{6}(-3.1875) \\
    &= 1 - 0.53125 \\
    &= 0.46875
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification (Analytical solution of the same ODE):**
> The integrating-factor solution is $y(t) = \dfrac{4t - 3 + 5e^{-2t}}{4}$.
> At $t = 0.5$: $y(0.5) = \dfrac{2 - 3 + 5e^{-1}}{4} = \dfrac{-1 + 1.8394}{4} \approx 0.4599$.
> RK4 error $\approx 0.0088$ — excellent agreement for one step of size $0.5$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the initial condition.** Always substitute $y(0) = y_0$ to find the integration constant. *(−2 marks if missed)*
> 2. **Confusing $k_1$, $k_2$, $k_3$, $k_4$ in RK4.** The **midpoint** evaluations use $h/2$ and $k_1/2$, not $k_2/2$. *(−1 mark per wrong $k$)*
> 3. **Wrong sign in drug decay.** The ODE is $dC/dt = -kC$ (decay) — a positive $k$ reduces concentration. Writing $dC/dt = +kC$ shows exponential growth, which is biologically wrong here.
> 4. **Mixing units.** $k$ in $\text{hr}^{-1}$, $t$ in hours, $C$ in mg/L. Do not mix minutes with hours.
> 5. **Skipping the comparison step in numerical questions.** KTU examiners reward the **comparison with analytical solution**. Always include error % in numerical-method answers.
> 6. **Forgetting $|C|$ in $\ln|C|$.** When $C$ can be negative (general solutions), write $\ln \vert C \vert$. Lose 0.5 mark otherwise.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before the Exam)**

- **Order** = highest derivative; **Degree** = power of that derivative after clearing radicals.
- **Linear ODE** $\rightarrow$ use **Integrating Factor** $e^{\int P dx}$.
- **Separable ODE** $\rightarrow$ separate, integrate, apply IC.
- **Constant-coefficient higher-order ODE** $\rightarrow$ form the **Auxiliary Equation**, find roots, write CF, find PI, sum them up, apply IC.
- **Euler Method** $\rightarrow y_{n+1} = y_n + h f(t_n, y_n)$, error $O(h)$.
- **Modified Euler / Heun** $\rightarrow$ predictor-corrector pair, error $O(h^2)$.
- **RK4** $\rightarrow$ four evaluations $(k_1, k_2, k_3, k_4)$ with weights $(1, 2, 2, 1)/6$, error $O(h^4)$.
- **Drug decay** $\rightarrow C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}$, half-life $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$.
- **RC circuit** $\rightarrow V(t) = V_0(1 - e^{-t/RC})$ or $V_0 e^{-t/RC}$ depending on charging/discharging.
- **Logistic growth** $\rightarrow \dfrac{dN}{dt} = rN(1 - N/K)$, solution $N(t) = \dfrac{K}{1 + Ae^{-rt}}$.
- **Damped oscillator** $\rightarrow$ check discriminant $c^2 - 4mk$ for over/critical/under-damping.
- **Stability of numerical schemes** $\rightarrow$ smaller $h$ improves accuracy but increases round-off; RK4 is best for non-stiff problems.
- **Always** write the units of every computed physical quantity.
- **Always** compare numerical vs analytical results in long-answer questions.
- **Always** verify initial conditions are satisfied by your final closed-form solution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for RK4 coefficients — "ONE TWO TWO ONE over six":**
> The weights $1, 2, 2, 1$ in RK4 match Simpson's 1/3 rule — visualize a parabola fit through four slope estimates. This single mental picture carries you through every RK4 problem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modeling of Biological Processes (Neuronal Dynamics, Cell Electrophysiology)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT636 - COMPUTATIONAL METHODS IN BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Modeling of Biological Processes (Neuronal Dynamics, Cell Electrophysiology)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Cell Electrophysiology?

**Cell Electrophysiology** is the branch of physiology that studies the electrical properties of biological cells and tissues. It deals with the **voltage changes** across a cell's membrane (membrane potential) caused by ionic current flow, the action potentials of neurons, and the electrical activity of cardiac, skeletal, and smooth muscle cells.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Cell Electrophysiology is the study of the electrochemical gradients and gating kinetics of voltage-gated ion channels that govern the resting membrane potential and the generation, propagation, and termination of action potentials in excitable cells.*

The transmembrane potential $V_m$ is the **voltage difference** between the intracellular fluid (ICF) and the extracellular fluid (ECF):
$$V_m = V_{in} - V_{out}$$

## 1.2 What is Neuronal Dynamics?

**Neuronal Dynamics** is the mathematical description of how the membrane potential of a neuron evolves in time as a function of ionic currents, synaptic inputs, and intrinsic channel kinetics. The discipline provides computational models (from simple integrate-and-fire to detailed multi-compartment Hodgkin-Huxley models) that predict firing patterns, threshold behavior, and refractory properties.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Neuron as an Electrical Circuit

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Leaky Battery with Variable Resistors:**
> Imagine a small **leaky water tank** (the cell membrane acting as a capacitor) connected to several **adjustable taps** (ion channels acting as variable resistors). Each tap connects the tank to a reservoir at a different height (the Nernst potential for that ion). The tank holds charge like a capacitor ($C_m$); the adjustable taps leak or supply ionic current depending on how open they are (gating variables $m, h, n$). An external stimulus is like pouring water in — once the level (membrane voltage) crosses a threshold, the taps open rapidly and the tank discharges in an *action potential*.

This analogy is the foundation of the **Hodgkin-Huxley formalism** (Hodgkin & Huxley, 1952, J. Physiol.), which earned the **1963 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine** and remains the gold-standard biophysical model of neuronal excitability.

## 1.4 Standard Physical & Physiological Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Faraday constant | $F$ | **96485** | C·mol⁻¹ |
| Universal gas constant | $R$ | **8.314** | J·(mol·K)⁻¹ |
| Body temperature | $T$ | **310.15** (37 °C) | K |
| Thermal voltage at 37 °C | $RT/F$ | **26.73** | mV |
| Membrane capacitance (squid axon) | $C_m$ | **1.0** | μF·cm⁻² |
| Resting potential (typical neuron) | $V_{rest}$ | **−70** | mV |
| Sodium Nernst potential | $E_{Na}$ | **+60** | mV |
| Potassium Nernst potential | $E_K$ | **−90** | mV |
| Leak reversal potential | $E_L$ | **−50** | mV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme Module 3 explicitly mandates the **Hodgkin–Huxley (HH) framework**, the **Nernst and Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz (GHK) equations**, the **cable equation**, and a discussion of **simplified neuron models** (integrate-and-fire, FitzHugh–Nagumo).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Resting Membrane Potential — Ionic Basis

A living cell at rest maintains an **electrochemical gradient** primarily due to the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in per ATP hydrolyzed). This produces an intracellular fluid rich in K⁺ and an extracellular fluid rich in Na⁺ and Cl⁻.

### 2.1.1 The Nernst Equation (Single-Ion Equilibrium)

The **Nernst equation** gives the equilibrium (reversal) potential of a single ion species when only that ion is permeable:

$$E_X = \frac{RT}{z_X F} \ln\!\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right)$$

In physiological units, using $\log_{10}$ at 37 °C, the constant factor is $\frac{RT}{F}\ln 10 \approx 61.5$ mV:

$$E_X = \frac{61.5}{z_X}\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right)\quad \text{[mV]}$$

where $z_X$ is the ion valence (+1 for Na⁺/K⁺, −1 for Cl⁻, +2 for Ca²⁺).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the minus sign for $z=-1$?** For anions (Cl⁻), $z=-1$ flips the sign. Since [Cl⁻] is high *outside*, $\log_{10}([Cl^-]_{out}/[Cl^-]_{in}) > 0$ but $E_{Cl} < 0$ — the Cl⁻ reversal sits near $-65$ mV.

### 2.1.2 The Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation

When **multiple ions** contribute to the membrane permeability, the resting potential is a weighted average of the individual Nernst potentials — weighted by the **permeabilities**:

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}\right)$$

The **Why:** At rest, $P_K \gg P_{Na}, P_{Cl}$, so $V_m$ is pulled toward $E_K$ (≈ −90 mV), giving the observed −70 mV.

## 2.2 The Hodgkin–Huxley (HH) Framework

Hodgkin and Huxley (1952) modeled the squid giant axon's membrane as a **parallel RC circuit with three voltage-dependent conductances**:

$$C_m\,\frac{dV}{dt} = -g_{Na}\,m^3 h\,(V - E_{Na}) - g_K\,n^4\,(V - E_K) - g_L\,(V - E_L) + I_{ext}$$

### 2.2.1 Gating Variable Kinetics

Each gate $x \in \{m, h, n\}$ obeys a first-order relaxation toward its voltage-dependent steady state:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \alpha_x(V)(1-x) - \beta_x(V)\,x$$

Equivalently, using the **steady-state value** $x_\infty$ and **time constant** $\tau_x$:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{x_\infty(V) - x}{\tau_x(V)}$$

### 2.2.2 Standard Rate Functions (at 6.3 °C, in ms⁻¹)

$$\alpha_m(V) = \frac{0.1\,(V+40)}{1 - e^{-(V+40)/10}}, \quad \beta_m(V) = 4.0\,e^{-(V+65)/18}$$

$$\alpha_h(V) = 0.07\,e^{-(V+65)/20}, \quad \beta_h(V) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(V+35)/10}}$$

$$\alpha_n(V) = \frac{0.01\,(V+55)}{1 - e^{-(V+55)/10}}, \quad \beta_n(V) = 0.125\,e^{-(V+65)/80}$$

with $V$ measured in mV relative to resting potential (Hodgkin-Huxley convention).

## 2.3 Cable Theory and the Cable Equation

For spatially extended structures (dendrites, axons), the membrane potential $V(x,t)$ obeys the **cable equation**:

$$C_m\,\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} = \frac{1}{r_a}\,\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} - \frac{V}{r_m} + I_{ext}(x,t)$$

with **length constant** $\lambda = \sqrt{r_m / r_a}$ and **membrane time constant** $\tau_m = r_m C_m$.

## 2.4 Reduced Models for Computational Efficiency

| Model | Equations | Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| Integrate-and-Fire (LIF) | $C_m \frac{dV}{dt} = -g_L(V-E_L) + I$; reset on threshold | Large-scale networks (10⁴–10⁶ neurons) |
| FitzHugh–Nagumo | $\dot{v} = v - v^3/3 - w + I$; $\dot{w} = \epsilon(v + a - bw)$ | Conceptual understanding of excitability |
| Morris–Lecar | 2D reduction of HH with Ca²⁺ and K⁺ | Phase-plane analysis |
| Izhikevich | 2D, 9 parameters capturing 20+ firing types | Realistic spiking at low cost |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_X = \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln\dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$ | Nernst potential of ion $X$ | V |
| 2 | $E_X = \dfrac{61.5}{z}\log_{10}\dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$ at 37 °C | Nernst potential (clinical form) | mV |
| 3 | GHK equation (see §2.1.2) | Multi-ion resting potential | V |
| 4 | $C_m\dfrac{dV}{dt} = -\sum_j g_j\,(V-E_j) + I_{ext}$ | Membrane equation | A·F⁻¹ |
| 5 | $\dot{x} = \alpha_x(V)(1-x) - \beta_x(V)x$ | Gate kinetics | s⁻¹ |
| 6 | $x_\infty = \dfrac{\alpha_x}{\alpha_x+\beta_x}$, $\tau_x = \dfrac{1}{\alpha_x+\beta_x}$ | Steady-state & time constant | s |
| 7 | $g_{Na} = \bar g_{Na}\,m^3 h$, $g_K = \bar g_K\,n^4$ | Conductance scaling | S·cm⁻² |
| 8 | $\tau_m = r_m C_m$, $\lambda = \sqrt{r_m/r_a}$ | Cable constants | s, cm |
| 9 | $C_m\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t} = \dfrac{1}{r_a}\dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} - \dfrac{V}{r_m} + I_{ext}$ | Cable equation | V·s⁻¹ |
| 10 | $v' = v - v^3/3 - w + I$; $w' = \epsilon(v+a-bw)$ | FitzHugh–Nagumo | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical rule for KTU exams:** Always quote the sign convention explicitly ($V_{in}-V_{out}$), state temperature (6.3 °C vs 37 °C), and show the **direction of ionic current flow** (outward-positive by convention in HH).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation from Electrochemical Equilibrium

### Step 1 — Set up the chemical and electrical work balance
Consider an ion $X$ of charge $z_X$ moving from *outside* (o) to *inside* (i) across the membrane. The work done by the electrochemical gradient per mole of $X$ transferred is:

$$W_{electrochem} = \underbrace{RT\,\ln\frac{[X]_i}{[X]_o}}_{\text{chemical (osmotic) work}} + \underbrace{z_X F (V_i - V_o)}_{\text{electrical work}}$$

### Step 2 — Apply equilibrium condition
At equilibrium, the net flux is zero, and the work done in moving one mole of ion reversibly is zero:

$$RT\,\ln\frac{[X]_i}{[X]_o} + z_X F (V_i - V_o) = 0$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the membrane voltage
Rearranging, and noting $V_m = V_i - V_o$:

$$z_X F V_m = RT\,\ln\frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}$$

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{z_X F}\,\ln\frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}$$

This is the **Nernst equation**. The **Why:** The exponential dependence arises because ion movement produces an exponentially-growing potential (Nernst-Planck electrodiffusion), so the *logarithm* inverts this relationship.

### Step 4 — Convert from $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$
Using $\ln x = 2.3026\,\log_{10}x$ and $RT/F = 0.02569$ V at 310 K:

$$V_m = \frac{2.3026\,RT}{z_X F}\,\log_{10}\frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i} = \frac{0.0591}{z_X}\,\log_{10}\frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}\quad \text{[V, at 25 °C]}$$

At 37 °C, the constant becomes $61.5$ mV, hence the **physiological form** quoted in §2.1.1.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation

### Step 1 — Start with the Nernst–Planck flux equation
For a constant electric field across a thin membrane of thickness $\delta$, the steady-state flux of ion $X$ is (HH, 1949):

$$J_X = -D_X\,\frac{d[X]}{dx} - \frac{D_X\,z_X F}{RT}\,[X]\,\frac{dV}{dx}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the constant-field assumption ($dV/dx = V_m/\delta$ = const)
Integrating across the membrane with the constant-field approximation yields the **GHK current** for ion $X$:

$$I_X = P_X\,z_X^2 F^2\,\frac{V_m}{RT}\cdot\frac{[X]_i - [X]_o\,e^{-z_X F V_m/RT}}{1 - e^{-z_X F V_m/RT}}$$

where $P_X = D_X/\delta$ is the permeability.

### Step 3 — Sum the ionic currents and impose $I_{total}=0$ at rest
At rest, the net membrane current is zero. Summing the Na, K, and Cl currents (Cl enters with reversed sign because $z=-1$):

$$I_{Na} + I_K - I_{Cl} = 0$$

### Step 4 — Solve algebraically for $V_m$
After algebra (straightforward but tedious — see Hille, *Ion Channels of Excitable Membranes*, 3rd ed., Ch. 4), the result is:

$$\boxed{\,V_m = \frac{RT}{F}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)\,}$$

The **physical interpretation:** $V_m$ is a **permeability-weighted geometric mean** of the Nernst potentials, not their arithmetic mean.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Hodgkin–Huxley Membrane Equation

### Step 1 — Equivalent circuit of the membrane patch
Treat the lipid bilayer as a **parallel-plate capacitor** with capacitance $C_m$ per unit area. The ion channels are **nonlinear conductances** $g_{Na}, g_K, g_L$ in parallel with **batteries** $E_{Na}, E_K, E_L$ representing the Nernst potentials.

### Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)
Capacitive current + ionic current = applied current:

$$C_m\,\frac{dV}{dt} + I_{ionic} = I_{ext}$$

### Step 3 — Express ionic currents
$$I_{ionic} = g_{Na}(V-E_{Na}) + g_K(V-E_K) + g_L(V-E_L)$$

### Step 4 — Introduce gating-variable factorization
Hodgkin & Huxley's key insight: each conductance is the product of a **maximum (peak) conductance** $\bar g_X$ and a product of independent gating variables raised to integer powers:

$$g_{Na} = \bar g_{Na}\,m^3 h, \qquad g_K = \bar g_K\,n^4, \qquad g_L = \bar g_L$$

- **$m$** = Na⁺ activation (opens with depolarization)
- **$h$** = Na⁺ inactivation (closes with depolarization)
- **$n$** = K⁺ activation (opens slowly with depolarization)

### Step 5 — Combine into the HH master equation
$$\boxed{\,C_m\,\frac{dV}{dt} = -\bar g_{Na}\,m^3 h\,(V-E_{Na}) - \bar g_K\,n^4\,(V-E_K) - \bar g_L\,(V-E_L) + I_{ext}\,}$$

### Step 6 — Gate kinetics equation
For each gate $x \in \{m, h, n\}$, the first-order kinetic scheme is $C \xrightleftharpoons[\beta_x]{\alpha_x} O$, yielding:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \alpha_x(V)(1-x) - \beta_x(V)\,x$$

This is a **linear first-order ODE** for $x$ at fixed $V$, with steady-state $x_\infty = \alpha_x/(\alpha_x+\beta_x)$ and time constant $\tau_x = 1/(\alpha_x+\beta_x)$.

### Step 7 — Numerical integration procedure (typical KTU problem)
1. Initialize $V = V_{rest}$, $m=m_0$, $h=h_0$, $n=n_0$ at steady state.
2. Apply explicit Euler with $\Delta t = 0.01$ ms:
$$V^{k+1} = V^k + \frac{\Delta t}{C_m}\Big[-\bar g_{Na}(m^3h)(V^k-E_{Na}) - \bar g_K n^4(V^k-E_K) - \bar g_L(V^k-E_L) + I_{ext}\Big]$$
3. Update each gate similarly.
4. Repeat for $T_{total}/\Delta t$ steps; plot $V(t)$ to observe the action potential.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Cable Equation (Spatial Generalization)

### Step 1 — Discretize the axon into segments
Each cylindrical segment of length $\Delta x$ and diameter $d$ has membrane capacitance $c_m \pi d \Delta x$, membrane resistance $r_m / (\pi d \Delta x)$, and axial resistance $r_a \Delta x / (\pi d^2/4)$ (with $r_a, r_m$ specific resistivities).

### Step 2 — Apply KCL at an interior node
The axial current entering from the left, minus that leaving to the right, must equal the membrane (capacitive + leak) current:

$$\frac{1}{r_a}\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} = c_m\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} + \frac{V}{r_m} - I_{ext}$$

### Step 3 — Standardize notation (using specific parameters)
With $C_m = c_m$, $R_m = r_m$, $R_i = r_a$:

$$C_m\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} = \frac{1}{R_i}\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} - \frac{V}{R_m} + I_{ext}$$

Defining the **length constant** $\lambda = \sqrt{R_m/R_i}$ and **time constant** $\tau_m = R_m C_m$, the steady-state spatial solution of an infinite cable decays as $V(x) = V_0 e^{-x/\lambda}$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Why Each Model Matters

| Property | HH (4D) | Morris–Lecar (2D) | FitzHugh–Nagumo (2D) | LIF (1D) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| State variables | $V, m, h, n$ | $V, w$ | $v, w$ | $V$ |
| Captures action potential shape | ✅ Exact | ✅ Good | ❌ Qualitative | ❌ Spike only |
| Captures refractoriness | ✅ Yes | ⚠ Partial | ⚠ Partial | ❌ No |
| Firing rate accuracy | ✅ High | ✅ High | ⚠ Medium | ⚠ Approx |
| Computational cost | High | Medium | Low | Very low |
| Analytical tractability | Low | High | High | Trivial |
| Recommended use | Mechanistic insight | Phase-plane teaching | Conceptual BCT | Large networks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The FitzHugh–Nagumo model is obtained by applying **singular perturbation** to the HH equations, exploiting the timescale separation $\tau_m, \tau_h \ll \tau_n$. This yields a 2D system that preserves the *qualitative* phase portrait (limit cycle, threshold, refractory ring) while discarding quantitative ionic detail.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Anatomical Architecture of a Neuron

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0["DENDRITES"] --> A1["SOMA CELL BODY"]
    A1 --> A2["AXON HILLOCK INITIAL SEGMENT"]
    A2 --> A3["AXON"]
    A3 --> A4["MYELIN SHEATH NODES OF RANVIER"]
    A4 --> A5["AXON TERMINAL BOUTON"]
    A5 --> A6["SYNAPTIC CLEFT"]
    A6 --> A7["POSTSYNAPTIC NEURON"]
    style A0 fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style A1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style A2 fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style A3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style A4 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A5 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A6 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A7 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering mapping:** The axon hillock is the **trigger zone** where the action potential is initiated because it has the highest density of voltage-gated Na⁺ channels.

## 4.2 Hodgkin–Huxley Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NA["BARRIER OUTSIDE EXTRACELLULAR FLUID"] --> NODE1
    NODE1 --- CM["CAPACITOR CM"]
    NODE1 --- GNA["VARISTOR GNA M CUBED H"]
    NODE1 --- GK["VARISTOR GK N TO THE 4"]
    NODE1 --- GL["RESISTOR GL"]
    GNA --- ENA["BATTERY ENA PLUS 60 MV"]
    GK --- EK["BATTERY EK MINUS 90 MV"]
    GL --- EL["BATTERY EL MINUS 50 MV"]
    ENA --- NODE2
    EK --- NODE2
    EL --- NODE2
    NODE2 --- IN["INTRACELLULAR FLUID"]
    IEXT["CURRENT SOURCE IEXT"] --- NODE1
    style CM fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style GNA fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style GK fill:#3CB371,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style GL fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style ENA fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style EK fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style EL fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** Drawing the equivalent circuit with **all three variable conductances in parallel** with the membrane capacitance and a current source $I_{ext}$ is a guaranteed **3-mark question** in KTU Module 3.

## 4.3 State Transition Diagram of an Ion Channel (HH formalism)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ClosedC1
    ClosedC1 --> ClosedC2 : alpha m
    ClosedC2 --> ClosedC3 : alpha m
    ClosedC3 --> Open : alpha m
    Open --> ClosedC3 : beta m
    ClosedC3 --> ClosedC2 : beta m
    ClosedC2 --> ClosedC1 : beta m
    Open --> Inactivated : fast h gate closes
    Inactivated --> ClosedC1 : recovery on repolarization
    note right of Open : SODIUM CHANNEL OPEN CONDUCTIVE
    note right of Inactivated : SODIUM CHANNEL INACTIVATED REFRACTORY
```

## 4.4 Action Potential Phases — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PH0["RESTING STATE VM MINUS 70 MV"] --> PH1["STIMULUS APPLIED DEPOLARIZATION"]
    PH1 --> PH2["THRESHOLD CROSSED VM MINUS 55 MV"]

    PH3 --> PH4["REPOLARIZATION K EFFLUX NA INACTIVATION"]
    PH4 --> PH5["HYPERPOLARIZATION VM BELOW RESTING UNDERSHOOT"]
    PH5 --> PH6["RESTORATION NA K PUMP AND K CHANNELS CLOSE"]

    style PH1 fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#000,color:#000

```
