### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) **Perform the Needleman–Wunsch global alignment** of the following two DNA sequences using the scoring scheme: match = +1, mismatch = −1, gap = −2. Show the complete DP matrix and the optimal alignment with its score. (7 marks)

**Sequences:**  
- $S_1$ = `AGCT`  
- $S_2$ = `AGT`

**Step 1 — Initialize $5 \times 4$ matrix with $F[0,0]=0$, $F[i,0] = -2i$, $F[0,j] = -2j$.**

$$ \begin{aligned}
& \begin{array}{c|cccc}
 & - & A & G & T \\
\hline
- & 0 & -2 & -4 & -6 \\
A & -2 &  &  &  \\
G & -4 &  &  &  \\
C & -6 &  &  &  \\
T & -8 &  &  &  \\
\end{array}
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 2 — Fill the matrix using NW recurrence.**

$$ \begin{aligned}
& \begin{array}{c|cccc}
 & - & A & G & T \\
\hline
- & 0 & -2 & -4 & -6 \\
A & -2 & \mathbf{1}^{\diagup} & -1^{\leftarrow} & -3^{\leftarrow} \\
G & -4 & -1^{\uparrow} & \mathbf{2}^{\diagup} & 0^{\diagup} \\
C & -6 & -3^{\uparrow} & 0^{\uparrow} & \mathbf{1}^{\diagup} \\
T & -8 & -5^{\uparrow} & -2^{\uparrow} & 1^{\diagup} \\
\end{array}
\end{aligned} $$

(Bold cells with $\diagup$ indicate diagonal moves; $\uparrow$ = up, $\leftarrow$ = left.)

**Step 3 — Sample cell calculation $F[3,3]$ (C vs T):**

$$ \begin{aligned}
F[3,3] &= \max \begin{cases} F[2,2] + s(C,T) = 2 + (-1) = 1 \\ F[2,3] + g = 0 + (-2) = -2 \\ F[3,2] + g = 0 + (-2) = -2 \end{cases} \\
F[3,3] &= 1 \quad \text{(diagonal: C–T mismatch, score = 1)} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 4 — Traceback from $F[4,3] = 1$:**

$$ \begin{aligned}
\text{Optimal Global Alignment} : \quad & A\ G\ C\ T \\
                                       & A\ G\ -\ T \\
\text{Score} : \quad & +1 +1 -2 +1 = 1 \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned} $$

#### (b) Explain the **Smith–Waterman local alignment algorithm**. Construct the DP matrix for the same sequences (AGCT vs AGT) and identify the optimal local alignment. (7 marks)

**Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**

1. Initialize $F[i,0] = F[0,j] = 0$ (no penalty for starting anywhere).
2. Recurrence: $F[i,j] = \max\{0,\ F[i-1,j-1]+s,\ F[i-1,j]+g,\ F[i,j-1]+g\}$.
3. Traceback starts at the **cell with maximum score**, stops at first cell with value 0.

**Step-by-step DP Matrix Construction (4 marks):**

$$ \begin{aligned}
& \begin{array}{c|cccc}
 & - & A & G & T \\
\hline
- & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
A & 0 & \mathbf{1}^{\diagup} & 0^{\leftarrow} & 0^{\leftarrow} \\
G & 0 & 0^{\uparrow} & \mathbf{2}^{\diagup} & 1^{\diagup} \\
C & 0 & 0^{\uparrow} & 1^{\uparrow} & \mathbf{1}^{\diagup} \\
T & 0 & 0^{\uparrow} & 0^{\uparrow} & \mathbf{2}^{\diagup} \\
\end{array}
\end{aligned} $$

**Cell $F[2,2]$ (G vs G):**

$$ \begin{aligned}
F[2,2] &= \max \begin{cases} 0 \\ F[1,1] + s(G,G) = 1 + 1 = 2 \\ F[1,2] + g = 0 + (-2) = -2 \\ F[2,1] + g = 0 + (-2) = -2 \end{cases} \\
F[2,2] &= 2 \quad \text{(diagonal: G–G match)} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned} $$

**Optimal Local Alignment:**

$$ \begin{aligned}
\text{Best Local Alignment} : \quad & A\ G \\
                                   & A\ G \\
\text{Score} : \quad & 1 + 1 = 2 \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned} $$

The local alignment correctly identifies the conserved prefix "AG" and **does not penalize** the mismatched C/tail region (which would be penalized in global alignment).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

#### (a) **Explain the concept of substitution matrices in protein alignment.** Derive the **log-odds score formula** and discuss the **PAM and BLOSUM matrix families** with examples. (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
A substitution matrix $S$ is a $20 \times 20$ scoring table where $S[a,b]$ quantifies the biological probability of amino acid $a$ being replaced by $b$ during evolution.

**Derivation of Log-Odds Score (3 marks):**

Let $q_{ab}$ = observed joint probability of $a$ aligning with $b$ in trusted alignments.  
Let $p_a, p_b$ = background probabilities of $a$ and $b$ in random sequences.

The **odds ratio** of observing $a, b$ aligned vs. random alignment is:

$$ \text{Odds} = \frac{q_{ab}}{p_a \cdot p_b} $$

Taking $\log_2$ to convert multiplicative odds to additive score (suitable for DP addition):

$$ \boxed{s(a,b) = \log_2 \left( \frac{q_{ab}}{p_a \cdot p_b} \right) } $$

- If $q_{ab} > p_a p_b$ (substitution favored) $\Rightarrow s(a,b) > 0$  
- If $q_{ab} < p_a p_b$ (substitution rare) $\Rightarrow s(a,b) < 0$

**PAM Family (1.5 marks):**
- **PAM-1:** Unit evolutionary matrix (1 substitution per 100 residues).
- **PAM-250:** Obtained by matrix exponentiation $(PAM1)^{250}$. Represents proteins separated by 250 million years of evolution. Useful for *distant* relationships.
- Built by Margaret Dayhoff (1978) from 71 protein families.

**BLOSUM Family (1.5 marks):**
- **BLOSUM-$k$:** Built by clustering sequences with $\geq k\%$ identity. BLOSUM62 clusters at 62% identity (the BLAST default).
- Built by Henikoff & Henikoff (1992) from BLOCKS database.
- BLOSUM80 → closely related; BLOSUM45 → distantly related.

**Key difference:** PAM extrapolates *forward in time* from close relatives; BLOSUM samples *actual observed blocks* at various identity levels.

#### (b) **Explain the BLAST algorithm.** Discuss the role of **E-value and bit score** in determining the statistical significance of a database hit. Compute the E-value for a hit with raw score $S = 60$ against a database of size $N = 10^9$ residues, using $\lambda = 0.267$ and $K = 0.041$. Assume query length $m = 500$. (7 marks)

**BLAST Algorithm — Basic Local Alignment Search Tool (Altschul et al., 1990) (3 marks):**

**Phase 1 — Seeding:**
- Break query into overlapping **words** of length $W$ (default $W = 3$ for proteins, $W = 11$ for DNA).
- For each word, pre-compute a list of **neighboring high-scoring words** (above threshold $T$) using the substitution matrix.
- Scan the database for **exact matches** of these high-scoring words.

**Phase 2 — Extension (HSP — High-Scoring Segment Pair):**
- Extend each seed in **both directions** along the diagonal.
- Stop extension when cumulative score drops below the best score so far by a margin $X_{\text{drop}$).

**Phase 3 — Significance Evaluation:**
- For each HSP, compute raw score $S$ and convert to **bit score** $S'$.
- Compute **E-value** to assess chance probability.

**E-value and Bit Score Definitions (2 marks):**

$$ E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S} $$

$$ S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2} $$

- **E-value:** Expected number of hits with score $\geq S$ by chance alone. Lower E = more significant. Threshold $E \leq 10^{-5}$ is standard.
- **Bit score:** Length-independent normalized score. Higher bit score = better alignment, regardless of database size.

**Numerical Computation (2 marks):**

Given: $S = 60$, $N = 10^9$, $\lambda = 0.267$, $K = 0.041$, $m = 500$.

Effective database length: $n = N = 10^9$.

$$ \begin{aligned}
E &= K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S} \\
  &= 0.041 \times 500 \times 10^9 \times e^{-0.267 \times 60} \\
  &= 0.041 \times 500 \times 10^9 \times e^{-16.02} \\
\end{aligned} $$

Computing the exponential:

$$ e^{-16.02} \approx 1.118 \times 10^{-7} $$

Substituting:

$$ \begin{aligned}
E &\approx 0.041 \times 500 \times 10^9 \times 1.118 \times 10^{-7} \\
  &\approx 2.05 \times 10^{10} \times 1.118 \times 10^{-7} \\
  &\approx 2291.9
\end{aligned} $$

$$ \boxed{E \approx 2.29 \times 10^3 } $$

**Bit score:**

$$ \begin{aligned}
S' &= \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2} \\
   &= \frac{0.267 \times 60 - \ln(0.041)}{\ln 2} \\
   &= \frac{16.02 - (-3.194)}{0.6931} \\
   &= \frac{19.214}{0.6931} \\
   &\approx 27.72 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned} $$

**Interpretation:** Despite a bit score of ~28 bits (which is decent), the E-value of ~2292 is *astronomically high*, indicating this is almost certainly a chance occurrence. This is because the database is **enormous** ($10^9$ residues), and a moderately high score $S=60$ cannot overcome that size penalty. A biologically significant hit would typically require $E \leq 10^{-5}$, necessitating a much higher raw score (e.g., $S \geq 130$ for this database size).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Forgetting to initialize the boundary row/column** with cumulative gap penalties (loses 1 mark).
> 2. **Confusing traceback directions** — students often write $\uparrow$ where it should be $\leftarrow$ (loses 0.5–1 mark per cell).
> 3. **Mixing up local vs global** — using $\max\{..., 0\}$ floor in Needleman–Wunsch is a fatal error.
> 4. **PAM vs BLOSUM confusion** — students often claim BLOSUM is for DNA; it is strictly for **proteins**.
> 5. **E-value formula sign error** — students write $E = K m n e^{+\lambda S}$ (positive exponent), causing astronomically wrong answers.
> 6. **Skipping unit analysis** — the KTU examiner awards a bonus mark for mentioning "half-bits" or "bits" as the unit of log-odds scores.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Module 4 Recall Points**

- **Alignment Types:** Global (NW) end-to-end vs Local (SW) sub-region; traceback starts at $F[m,n]$ vs $\max F[i,j]$.
- **Boundary Conditions:** NW → $F[i,0]=ig$, $F[0,j]=jg$; SW → $F[i,0]=F[0,j]=0$.
- **Recurrence Core:** All three moves (diagonal, up, left) with substitution score + gap penalty.
- **Smith–Waterman Floor:** Always include $\max\{..., 0\}$; traceback stops at zero.
- **Affine Gap Penalty:** $\gamma(k) = g_o + (k-1) g_e$; uses 3 matrices $M, I_x, I_y$ (Gotoh algorithm).
- **Log-Odds Score:** $s(a,b) = \log_2(q_{ab}/(p_a p_b))$ — universally used for substitution matrices.
- **PAM:** Dayhoff; $PAM250 = (PAM1)^{250}$; based on phylogenetic extrapolation.
- **BLOSUM:** Henikoff; clustered at $k\%$ identity; BLOSUM62 is BLAST default.
- **BLAST:** Three phases — Seeding, Extension (HSP), Significance Evaluation.
- **E-value:** $E = K m n e^{-\lambda S}$; threshold $\leq 10^{-5}$ for significance.
- **Bit Score:** $S' = (\lambda S - \ln K)/\ln 2$; length-independent, additive in bits.
- **Time Complexity:** NW/SW = $O(mn)$; BLAST = $O(m+n)$ heuristic; Hirschberg = $O(mn)$ time, $O(m+n)$ space.
- **FASTA vs BLAST:** FASTA uses $k$-tuple lookup + dynamic programming for optima; BLAST is faster but less sensitive for very distant homologs.
- **Multiple Sequence Alignment:** Progressive (ClustalW) uses guide tree + pairwise alignments iteratively.
- **Engineering Applications:** Variant calling (GATK), genome assembly (BWA), phylogenetics, vaccine design, drug discovery.
- **Identity vs Similarity:** Identity = exact match count; Similarity = BLOSUM-weighted cumulative score.

> **KTU 2024 Examination Mantra:** *"Always show the DP matrix, mark the traceback arrows, and state the boundary initialization. A clean diagram + correct arithmetic = full marks."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structural Bioinformatics: Molecular Modeling and Simulation Techniques for Studying Protein Structures and Interactions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT636 - COMPUTATIONAL METHODS IN BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Structural Bioinformatics: Molecular Modeling and Simulation Techniques for Studying Protein Structures and Interactions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Structural Bioinformatics** is a sub-discipline of bioinformatics that applies computational algorithms, statistical methods, and physical chemistry principles to the **prediction, analysis, visualization, and simulation of three-dimensional (3D) biomolecular structures** — primarily proteins, nucleic acids, and their complexes — to understand their biological function, dynamics, and molecular interactions.

It integrates concepts from:
- **Molecular biology** (sequence → structure → function paradigm)
- **Classical mechanics** (Newtonian dynamics of atoms)
- **Statistical thermodynamics** (Boltzmann distributions, ensembles)
- **Computational science** (algorithms, optimization, numerical integration)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Origami Blueprint Analogy**
> Imagine a protein as an extremely complex piece of origami made from a single long chain of paper (the amino acid sequence). **Structural Bioinformatics** is the discipline that:
> 1. Predicts *how the paper will fold* (3D structure prediction),
> 2. Records the *folds of every origami ever made* (PDB database),
> 3. *Shakes the origami virtually* to see which parts are stiff and which flap around (Molecular Dynamics),
> 4. And tries to *dock two finished origami pieces together* to see if they lock like puzzle pieces (Molecular Docking).

The fundamental dogma of structural bioinformatics is the **Sequence → Structure → Function** relationship: knowing the 3D structure of a protein explains its biological role far better than the sequence alone.

### 1.3 Hierarchical Organization of Protein Structure

| Level | Description | Stabilizing Forces | Typical Span |
|-------|-------------|---------------------|--------------|
| **Primary** | Linear sequence of amino acids linked by peptide bonds | Covalent peptide bonds | 1D string |
| **Secondary** | Local regular folding (α-helices, β-sheets, turns, loops) | Hydrogen bonds (backbone) | 2D local |
| **Tertiary** | 3D folding of the entire polypeptide chain | Hydrophobic interactions, disulfide bridges, ionic bonds, H-bonds, VdW | 3D global |
| **Quaternary** | Assembly of multiple polypeptide subunits | Same as tertiary, plus interfacial contacts | Multi-chain complex |

### 1.4 The Role of the *Central Dogma of Structural Biology*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sequence determines Structure, Structure determines Function.**
> This is the governing principle. Mutations in sequence (e.g., sickle-cell hemoglobin Glu6Val) change the 3D fold, which changes the biological function. Computational methods attempt to invert this relationship: given the sequence, predict the fold.

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants in Biomolecular Modeling

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Units |
|----------|--------|-------|-------|
| Boltzmann constant | **k_B** | **1.380649 × 10⁻²³** | J/K |
| Avogadro's number | **N_A** | **6.02214076 × 10²³** | mol⁻¹ |
| Gas constant | **R** | **8.314462618** | J/(mol·K) |
| Vacuum permittivity | **ε₀** | **8.8541878128 × 10⁻¹²** | F/m |
| Elementary charge | **e** | **1.602176634 × 10⁻¹⁹** | C |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Major Computational Methods in Structural Bioinformatics

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme groups these techniques into four pillars: **(1) Structure Prediction, (2) Energy Minimization, (3) Molecular Dynamics (MD), and (4) Molecular Docking**.

#### Pillar 1 — Comparative (Homology) Modeling
- **Idea:** If protein A (target) has a known close relative protein B (template, ≥30% sequence identity), assume they share the same fold.
- **Steps:**
  1. Template identification via BLAST/PSI-BLAST against PDB.
  2. Sequence alignment (pairwise or multiple).
  3. Backbone generation from template coordinates.
  4. Loop modeling for insertions/deletions.
  5. Side-chain modeling (rotamer libraries).
  6. Energy refinement.

#### Pillar 2 — *Ab Initio* (De Novo) Modeling
- Builds structure **from scratch** using only physicochemical principles.
- Uses fragment assembly (e.g., **ROSETTA** approach): short fragments from PDB combined by Monte Carlo sampling.
- Computationally expensive; suitable only for small proteins (<150 residues).

#### Pillar 3 — Threading (Fold Recognition)
- Compares the target sequence against a library of known folds.
- Optimal when sequence identity is too low for homology (<25%) but structural similarity exists.

### 2.2 Molecular Mechanics Force Fields — The Heart of Modeling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **force field** is a mathematical expression describing the potential energy of a molecular system as a function of atomic positions. It encodes all bonded and non-bonded interactions.

The total potential energy **V_total** of a biomolecular system is the sum of bonded and non-bonded terms:

$$V_{\text{total}} = V_{\text{bond}} + V_{\text{angle}} + V_{\text{torsion}} + V_{\text{vdW}} + V_{\text{elec}}$$

#### 2.2.1 Bonded Terms

**(a) Bond stretching** (harmonic potential):
$$V_{\text{bond}} = \sum_{\text{bonds}} k_{b}\,\bigl(r_{ij} - r_{ij}^{\text{eq}}\bigr)^{2}$$

where $k_b$ is the force constant (typically **200–500 kcal/mol/Å²**), $r_{ij}$ is the instantaneous bond length, and $r_{ij}^{\text{eq}}$ is the equilibrium length.

**(b) Angle bending** (harmonic potential):
$$V_{\text{angle}} = \sum_{\text{angles}} k_{\theta}\,\bigl(\theta_{ijk} - \theta_{ijk}^{\text{eq}}\bigr)^{2}$$

where $k_\theta$ is in **kcal/mol/rad²** and $\theta_{ijk}$ is the bond angle formed by atoms i–j–k.

**(c) Torsion (dihedral) potential**:
$$V_{\text{torsion}} = \sum_{\text{dihedrals}} \frac{V_{n}}{2}\,\bigl[1 + \cos\bigl(n\phi_{ijkl} - \gamma\bigr)\bigr]$$

where $V_n$ is the barrier height, $n$ is the periodicity (multiplicity), $\phi$ is the dihedral angle, and $\gamma$ is the phase offset.

#### 2.2.2 Non-Bonded Terms

**(d) Van der Waals (Lennard-Jones 12-6 potential)**:
$$V_{\text{vdW}} = \sum_{i<j}\, 4\varepsilon_{ij}\!\left[\left(\frac{\sigma_{ij}}{r_{ij}}\right)^{12} - \left(\frac{\sigma_{ij}}{r_{ij}}\right)^{6}\right]$$

The $\bigl(\tfrac{\sigma}{r}\bigr)^{12}$ term represents Pauli repulsion (short-range), and the $\bigl(\tfrac{\sigma}{r}\bigr)^{6}$ term represents London dispersion attraction.

**(e) Electrostatic (Coulomb's law)**:
$$V_{\text{elec}} = \sum_{i<j}\, \frac{q_{i}\,q_{j}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}\,r_{ij}}$$

where $q_i$ and $q_j$ are partial atomic charges (in units of $e$).

> [!NOTE]
> Popular force fields: **AMBER**, **CHARMM**, **GROMOS**, **OPLS**. Each differs in parameter derivation methodology but shares the same functional form above.

### 2.3 Energy Minimization Techniques

Once a structure is built, the geometry must be relaxed to a local minimum of $V_{\text{total}}$.

| Method | Order | Use Case | Speed |
|--------|-------|----------|-------|
| **Steepest Descent (SD)** | 1st-order | Initial rough relaxation; far from minimum | Fast |
| **Conjugate Gradient (CG)** | 1st-order (improved) | Final refinement near minimum | Medium |
| **Newton–Raphson (NR)** | 2nd-order (needs Hessian) | Small systems, exact minimum | Slow, memory-heavy |

### 2.4 Molecular Dynamics (MD) — The Time Evolution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> MD simulates the *physical motion* of every atom over time by numerically integrating Newton's second law:
> $$F_i = m_i\,a_i = -\nabla_{i} V_{\text{total}}$$

The most common integrator is the **Velocity Verlet** algorithm:
$$r_i(t+\Delta t) = r_i(t) + v_i(t)\,\Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2}\,a_i(t)\,\Delta t^{2}$$
$$v_i(t+\Delta t) = v_i(t) + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\bigl[a_i(t) + a_i(t+\Delta t)\bigr]\,\Delta t$$

A typical MD timestep is **Δt = 2 fs (femtoseconds)**.

### 2.5 Statistical Ensembles in MD

| Ensemble | Fixed Quantities | Application |
|----------|------------------|-------------|
| **NVE** (microcanonical) | Number, Volume, Energy | Basic MD, ideal for energy conservation tests |
| **NVT** (canonical) | Number, Volume, Temperature | Equilibration with thermostat (Berendsen, Nosé-Hoover) |
| **NPT** (isothermal–isobaric) | Number, Pressure, Temperature | Realistic biomolecular simulation (barostat needed) |

### 2.6 Molecular Docking

Predicts the **preferred orientation and binding affinity** of two molecules when forming a complex.

- **Protein–Ligand Docking:** Search algorithm samples ligand conformations; **scoring function** ranks poses.
  - Example tools: **AutoDock Vina**, **Glide**, **GOLD**.
- **Protein–Protein Docking:** Models interaction surfaces.
  - Example tools: **HADDOCK**, **ClusPro**, **ZDOCK**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Scoring functions** estimate binding free energy $\Delta G_{\text{bind}}$. Components typically include hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic effect, entropy penalty, and electrostatics.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Term | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_i = -\nabla_i V$ | Force from potential gradient | kcal/mol/Å |
| 2 | $V_{\text{bond}} = k_b(r - r_{eq})^2$ | Harmonic bond stretch energy | kcal/mol |
| 3 | $V_{\text{angle}} = k_\theta(\theta - \theta_{eq})^2$ | Harmonic angle bend energy | kcal/mol |
| 4 | $V_{\text{tors}} = \tfrac{V_n}{2}[1+\cos(n\phi-\gamma)]$ | Torsional (rotational) energy | kcal/mol |
| 5 | $V_{\text{LJ}} = 4\varepsilon[(\sigma/r)^{12}-(\sigma/r)^6]$ | Lennard-Jones VdW energy | kcal/mol |
| 6 | $V_{\text{Coul}} = q_i q_j / (4\pi\varepsilon_0 r)$ | Electrostatic Coulombic energy | kcal/mol |
| 7 | $\langle E_{\text{kin}} \rangle = \tfrac{3}{2} N k_B T$ | Equipartition theorem (kinetic) | kcal/mol |
| 8 | $\Delta t \approx 1\text{–}2$ fs | MD integration timestep | femtoseconds |
| 9 | $R_g$ (radius of gyration) | Compactness metric of a structure | Å |
| 10 | **RMSD** $=\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{N}\sum_i \vert r_i^{\text{model}} - r_i^{\text{ref}} \vert^{2}}$ | Structural deviation from reference | Å |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** These equations power **rational drug design** (predicting which small molecule binds a disease target), **vaccine antigen design**, **enzyme engineering** for industrial biocatalysis, and **understanding genetic disease mutations** at atomic resolution.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Interatomic Potential to Atomic Forces

**Goal:** Show that the force on atom *i* is the negative gradient of the total potential energy.

**Step 1 — Newton's second law for atom *i*:**
$$F_i = m_i\,a_i = m_i\,\frac{d^{2} r_i}{d t^{2}}$$

where $m_i$ is the atomic mass (in atomic mass units, amu), $a_i$ is acceleration, and $r_i$ is the position vector of atom *i* in Cartesian coordinates $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$.

**Step 2 — Relate force to potential energy:**
By definition of conservative forces, force equals the negative spatial gradient of potential:
$$F_i = -\nabla_i V(r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_N)$$

**Step 3 — Expand the gradient in Cartesian components:**
$$F_{i}^{x} = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial x_i}, \qquad F_{i}^{y} = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial y_i}, \qquad F_{i}^{z} = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial z_i}$$

**Step 4 — Combine with Newton's law:**
$$m_i\,\frac{d^{2} x_i}{d t^{2}} = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial x_i}, \quad m_i\,\frac{d^{2} y_i}{d t^{2}} = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial y_i}, \quad m_i\,\frac{d^{2} z_i}{d t^{2}} = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial z_i}$$

These are the **equations of motion** integrated by MD.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Force from Lennard-Jones Potential

**Goal:** Derive the analytical force expression for the Lennard-Jones 12-6 potential.

**Step 1 — Write the LJ potential for a single pair:**
$$V_{\text{LJ}}(r) = 4\varepsilon\!\left[\left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{12} - \left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{6}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Apply $F = -dV/dr$ to obtain the force magnitude:**
$$F(r) = -\frac{d V_{\text{LJ}}}{d r} = -4\varepsilon\,\frac{d}{d r}\!\left[\left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{12} - \left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{6}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate each term using chain rule:**
$$\frac{d}{d r}\!\left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{12} = 12\,\sigma^{12}\,r^{-13}\,(-1)\,r^{-2}\cdot \sigma \cdot \text{(re-derive)}$$

Cleaner approach — let $u = \sigma/r$, so $du/dr = -\sigma/r^2$:

$$\frac{d}{dr}(\sigma/r)^{12} = -12\,\frac{\sigma^{12}}{r^{13}}$$

$$\frac{d}{dr}(\sigma/r)^{6} = -6\,\frac{\sigma^{6}}{r^{7}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**
$$F(r) = -4\varepsilon\!\left[-12\,\frac{\sigma^{12}}{r^{13}} + 6\,\frac{\sigma^{6}}{r^{7}}\right] = 4\varepsilon\!\left[12\,\frac{\sigma^{12}}{r^{13}} - 6\,\frac{\sigma^{6}}{r^{7}}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Factor the expression:**
$$F(r) = \frac{4\varepsilon}{r}\!\left[12\left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{12} - 6\left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{6}\right]$$

**Step 6 — Identify the equilibrium at $r = r_{\min}$:**
Setting $F = 0$ yields $r_{\min} = 2^{1/6}\sigma \approx 1.122\,\sigma$. This is the most stable separation distance.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Velocity Verlet Integration Algorithm

**Goal:** Derive the Velocity Verlet update equations from Taylor expansion.

**Step 1 — Taylor expansion of position at $t + \Delta t$:**
$$r(t+\Delta t) = r(t) + v(t)\,\Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2}\,a(t)\,\Delta t^{2} + \tfrac{1}{6}\,b(t)\,\Delta t^{3} + O(\Delta t^{4})$$

**Step 2 — Taylor expansion of position at $t - \Delta t$:**
$$r(t-\Delta t) = r(t) - v(t)\,\Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2}\,a(t)\,\Delta t^{2} - \tfrac{1}{6}\,b(t)\,\Delta t^{3} + O(\Delta t^{4})$$

**Step 3 — Add the two expansions:**
$$r(t+\Delta t) + r(t-\Delta t) = 2\,r(t) + a(t)\,\Delta t^{2} + O(\Delta t^{4})$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $r(t+\Delta t)$:**
$$r(t+\Delta t) = 2\,r(t) - r(t-\Delta t) + a(t)\,\Delta t^{2} + O(\Delta t^{4})$$

This is the **basic Verlet** form. It is time-reversible but does not directly use velocity.

**Step 5 — To incorporate velocity, take Taylor expansion of velocity:**
$$v(t+\Delta t) = v(t) + a(t)\,\Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2}\,b(t)\,\Delta t^{2} + O(\Delta t^{3})$$

**Step 6 — Express the next-step acceleration using updated positions:**
$$a(t+\Delta t) = -\tfrac{1}{m}\nabla V\bigl(r(t+\Delta t)\bigr)$$

**Step 7 — Average acceleration at start and end for velocity update:**
$$v(t+\Delta t) = v(t) + \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[a(t) + a(t+\Delta t)\bigr]\,\Delta t + O(\Delta t^{3})$$

This is the **Velocity Verlet algorithm**, accurate to $O(\Delta t^3)$ in position and $O(\Delta t^2)$ in velocity.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Equipartition Theorem for MD Temperature Control

**Goal:** Show that the average kinetic energy per atom equals $\tfrac{3}{2} k_B T$.

**Step 1 — Total kinetic energy of N atoms in 3D:**
$$E_{\text{kin}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \tfrac{1}{2}\,m_i\,v_i^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Each atom has 3 translational degrees of freedom (x, y, z).** Total DOF = $3N$.

**Step 3 — By classical equipartition, each quadratic DOF contributes $\tfrac{1}{2} k_B T$ to average energy:**
$$\langle E_{\text{kin}} \rangle = \tfrac{3N}{2}\,k_B T$$

**Step 4 — For a single atom:**
$$\langle E_{\text{kin,atom}} \rangle = \tfrac{3}{2}\,k_B T$$

**Step 5 — Relate to instantaneous temperature:**
$$T_{\text{inst}} = \frac{2\,E_{\text{kin}}}{3\,N\,k_B} = \frac{\sum_i m_i v_i^{2}}{3\,N\,k_B}$$

This instantaneous temperature is what thermostats (e.g., **Nosé-Hoover**, **Berendsen**) regulate during MD.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Coulomb's Law with Effective Dielectric

**Goal:** Adapt Coulomb's law for biomolecular solvation using a distance-dependent dielectric constant.

**Step 1 — Vacuum Coulomb energy:**
$$V_{\text{Coul}} = \frac{q_i q_j}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\, r_{ij}}$$

**Step 2 — In a polarizable medium (protein interior or water),** introduce a relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$:
$$V_{\text{Coul,medium}} = \frac{q_i q_j}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r\, r_{ij}} = \frac{332\, q_i q_j}{\varepsilon_r\, r_{ij}}\,\text{(in kcal/mol when } r \text{ in Å, } q \text{ in } e\text{)}$$

**Step 3 — Distance-dependent dielectric approximation** (used in early CHARMM):
$$\varepsilon_r(r) = r$$
$$V_{\text{Coul}}(r) = \frac{332\, q_i q_j}{r^{2}}\;\text{(kcal/mol)}$$

This crudely mimics solvent screening — close charges feel strongly, far charges are attenuated.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Workflow of Homology (Comparative) Protein Modeling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Target Protein Sequence] --> B[Step 1: BLAST against PDB]
    B --> C{Identity >= 30 percent?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Step 2: Select Best Template]
    C -->|No| E[Use Threading or Ab Initio]
    D --> F[Step 3: Pairwise Sequence Alignment]
    F --> G[Step 4: Build Backbone from Template]
    G --> H[Step 5: Model Loops Insertions Deletions]
    H --> I[Step 6: Place Side Chains Rotamers]
    I --> J[Step 7: Energy Minimization Refinement]
    J --> K[Step 8: Validation via Ramachandran Plot]
    K --> L[Output: 3D Model PDB File]
```

### 4.2 Molecular Dynamics Simulation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Prepare PDB Input] --> P2[Step 2: Add Hydrogens Assign Protonation]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Solvate in Water Box TIP3P]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Add Ions for Neutralization]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Energy Minimization]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Equilibration NVT 100 ps]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Equilibration NPT 1 ns]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Production MD Run 100 ns plus]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Trajectory Analysis RMSD RMSF]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10: Free Energy Calculation MM PBSA or MM GBSA]
```

### 4.3 Force Field Energy Decomposition — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TF[Total Potential Energy V total] --> BG[Bonded Terms]
    TF --> NB[Non Bonded Terms]
    BG --> B1[Bond Stretch V bond]
    BG --> B2[Angle Bend V angle]
    BG --> B3[Torsion V dihedral]
    NB --> N1[Van der Waals Lennard Jones 12 6]
    NB --> N2[Electrostatic Coulomb]
    B1 --> EQ1[kb times r minus r eq squared]
    B2 --> EQ2[k theta times theta minus theta eq squared]
    B3 --> EQ3[Vn over 2 times 1 plus cos n phi minus gamma]
    N1 --> EQ4[4 epsilon sigma over r 12 minus sigma over r 6]
    N2 --> EQ5[qi qj over 4 pi epsilon 0 r ij]
```

### 4.4 Hierarchical Organization of Protein Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[Primary Structure: AA Sequence] --> H2[Secondary Structure: Local Folds]
    H2 --> H3[Tertiary Structure: 3D Fold]
    H3 --> H4[Quaternary Structure: Multi Subunit Assembly]
    H2 --> S1[Alpha Helix H bonds i to i+4]
    H2 --> S2[Beta Sheet parallel or antiparallel]
    H2 --> S3[Random Coil Loops Turns]
    H3 --> T1[Hydrophobic Core buried residues]
    H3 --> T2[Surface Exposed Polar Residues]
    H4 --> Q1[Homodimer Identical Chains]
    H4 --> Q2[Heterodimer Different Chains]
```

### 4.5 Protein–Ligand Docking Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Receptor Protein PDB] --> D3[Docking Engine AutoDock Vina]
    D2[Ligand Small Molecule MOL2] --> D3
    D3 --> D4[Search Algorithm Genetic Algorithm]
    D3 --> D5[Sampling Ligand Conformations]
    D4 --> D6[Pose Generation thousands of candidates]
    D5 --> D6
    D6 --> D7[Scoring Function Estimate Delta G bind]
    D7 --> D8[Rank Poses by Lowest Energy]
    D8 --> D9[Top 10 Poses Returned]
    D9 --> D10[Visual Inspection in PyMOL]
    D10 --> D11[Final Hit Selection for Experimental Validation]
```

### 4.6 Statistical Thermodynamic Ensembles Used in MD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ENS[MD Ensemble Selection] --> E1[NVE Microcanonical]
    ENS --> E2[NVT Canonical]
    ENS --> E3[NPT Isothermal Isobaric]
    E1 --> E1D[Constant N V E no thermostat no barostat]
    E2 --> E2D[Berendsen Thermostat V rescale]
    E2 --> E2N[Nose Hoover Thermostat]
    E3 --> E3D[Parrinello Rahman Barostat]
    E3 --> E3C[Berendsen Barostat coupling]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define structural bioinformatics. List any four computational techniques used in it.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Structural bioinformatics is the branch of bioinformatics that uses computational tools to predict, analyze, simulate, and visualize the 3D structures of biomolecules (proteins, nucleic acids) and their complexes.

**Four computational techniques:**
1. **Homology (comparative) modeling** — building a model using a known related template structure.
2. **Ab initio modeling** — building structure *de novo* from physicochemical principles.
3. **Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulation** — solving Newton's equations for every atom over time.
4. **Molecular docking** — predicting binding orientation between a receptor and a ligand.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**What is a molecular force field? Name its five energy components.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **force field** is a mathematical model that calculates the potential energy of a molecular system as a function of atomic positions.

**Five energy components:**
1. **Bond stretching** energy: $V_{\text{bond}} = k_b(r - r_{eq})^2$
2. **Angle bending** energy: $V_{\text{angle}} = k_\theta(\theta - \theta_{eq})^2$
3. **Torsional (dihedral)** energy: $V_{\text{tors}} = \tfrac{V_n}{2}[1+\cos(n\phi - \gamma)]$
4. **Van der Waals** energy: $V_{\text{LJ}} = 4\varepsilon[(\sigma/r)^{12} - (\sigma/r)^6]$
5. **Electrostatic** energy: $V_{\text{Coul}} = q_i q_j / (4\pi\varepsilon_0 r)$

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model | Module 4, Q Style]**

**(a) Explain in detail the Lennard-Jones 12-6 potential and derive the expression for the force between two non-bonded atoms. Also describe the physical significance of σ and ε. (7 marks)**

**(b) Describe the steps involved in molecular dynamics simulation. Explain the Velocity Verlet integration algorithm with proper equations. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Lennard-Jones 12-6 potential — detailed explanation:**

The Lennard-Jones (LJ) potential models the **non-bonded van der Waals interaction** between a pair of atoms separated by distance $r$:

$$V_{\text{LJ}}(r) = 4\varepsilon\!\left[\left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{12} - \left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{6}\right]$$

**Physical significance of parameters:**

- **σ (sigma)** — the **distance at which the inter-particle potential is zero**. It represents the effective *diameter* of the atom; for $r < \sigma$, strong repulsion dominates. Typical values: σ(H) ≈ 2.5 Å, σ(C) ≈ 3.4 Å.
- **ε (epsilon)** — the **depth of the potential well** at the equilibrium distance. It quantifies the strength of the attractive interaction. Typical values: 0.01–0.5 kcal/mol.

**The $r^{-12}$ term** models the **Pauli repulsion** (rapidly rising at short range because electron clouds overlap).

**The $r^{-6}$ term** models the **London dispersion attraction** (induced-dipole/induced-dipole interaction).

**Derivation of the force:**

Force is the negative gradient of the potential:

$$F(r) = -\frac{d V_{\text{LJ}}}{d r}$$

$$F(r) = -\frac{d}{d r}\!\left\{4\varepsilon\!\left[\left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{12} - \left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{6}\right]\right\}$$

$$F(r) = -4\varepsilon\!\left[-\frac{12\,\sigma^{12}}{r^{13}} + \frac{6\,\sigma^{6}}{r^{7}}\right]$$

$$\boxed{F(r) = \frac{4\varepsilon}{r}\!\left[12\left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{12} - 6\left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{6}\right]}$$

**Equilibrium separation** occurs when $F = 0$:

$$\left(\frac{\sigma}{r_{\min}}\right)^{12} = \tfrac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\sigma}{r_{\min}}\right)^{6} \implies r_{\min} = 2^{1/6}\,\sigma \approx 1.122\,\sigma$$

---

**(b) Steps of MD simulation + Velocity Verlet derivation:**

**Step-by-step MD workflow:**
1. **Input structure** — start with PDB coordinates.
2. **Topology & parameter assignment** — apply force field (e.g., AMBER, CHARMM).
3. **Solvation** — immerse in explicit water box (TIP3P) or implicit solvent.
4. **Ion addition** — add Na⁺/Cl⁻ to neutralize.
5. **Energy minimization** — Steepest Descent (5000 steps) → Conjugate Gradient (5000 steps).
6. **Heating** — slowly raise temperature from 0 K to target (e.g., 300 K).
7. **Equilibration** — NVT (100 ps) followed by NPT (1 ns) with restraints on protein.
8. **Production MD** — run for 10–1000 ns, recording trajectory every 1–10 ps.
9. **Analysis** — compute RMSD, RMSF, Rg, hydrogen bond occupancy, principal component analysis (PCA).
10. **Free energy estimation** — MM-PBSA, MM-GBSA, or umbrella sampling.

**Velocity Verlet algorithm:**

Derivation (Taylor expand positions at $t \pm \Delta t$):

$$r(t+\Delta t) = r(t) + v(t)\,\Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2}\,a(t)\,\Delta t^{2} + O(\Delta t^{3})$$

$$v(t+\tfrac{1}{2}\Delta t) = v(t) + \tfrac{1}{2}\,a(t)\,\Delta t$$

**Step 1 — Half-step velocity:**
$$v(t+\tfrac{1}{2}\Delta t) = v(t) + \tfrac{1}{2}\,a(t)\,\Delta t$$

**Step 2 — Full-step position:**
$$r(t+\Delta t) = r(t) + v(t+\tfrac{1}{2}\Delta t)\,\Delta t$$

**Step 3 — Compute new acceleration** from updated positions:
$$a(t+\Delta t) = -\tfrac{1}{m}\nabla V(r(t+\Delta t))$$

**Step 4 — Full-step velocity:**
$$v(t+\Delta t) = v(t+\tfrac{1}{2}\Delta t) + \tfrac{1}{2}\,a(t+\Delta t)\,\Delta t$$

**Why Velocity Verlet?**
- **Time-reversible** — preserves microscopic reversibility.
- **Symplectic** — conserves a discrete Hamiltonian (energy drift is bounded).
- **Easy to implement** — only requires current $r$, $v$, and $a$.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model | Module 4, Q Style]**

**(a) Discuss the hierarchical levels of protein structure. Explain the forces that stabilize each level. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the principle and steps of homology modeling. What are its limitations? How is the quality of a homology model validated? (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Hierarchical protein structure:**

| Level | Description | Stabilizing Forces | Example |
|-------|-------------|---------------------|---------|
| **Primary** | Linear sequence of amino acids joined by peptide bonds | Covalent peptide bonds (amide linkages) | Gly–Ala–Ser–Val–… |
| **Secondary** | Local regular motifs | Hydrogen bonds between backbone C=O and N–H groups | α-helix, β-sheet, turns |
| **Tertiary** | Overall 3D fold of one chain | Hydrophobic effect, disulfide bridges (–S–S–), ionic (salt bridges), H-bonds, VdW contacts | Myoglobin fold |
| **Quaternary** | Spatial arrangement of multiple subunits | Same as tertiary, plus subunit–subunit interfacial contacts | Hemoglobin tetramer (α₂β₂) |

**Detailed discussion:**
- **Primary structure** is dictated by the gene; a single mutation (e.g., Glu→Val at position 6 of β-globin) causes sickle-cell anemia.
- **Secondary structure** arises from the geometry of the peptide bond (planar, partial double-bond character).
- **Tertiary structure** is driven primarily by the **hydrophobic effect** (non-polar residues buried inside) and secondarily by hydrogen bonds and salt bridges on the surface.
- **Quaternary structure** creates *cooperative* and *allosteric* properties, as seen in oxygen binding to hemoglobin.

---

**(b) Homology modeling — principle, steps, limitations, validation:**

**Principle:** Proteins sharing >30% sequence identity almost always share the same 3D fold. So the **structure of an unknown protein (target) can be modeled using a known related protein (template)**.

**Step-by-step procedure:**

1. **Template identification** — Run BLAST or PSI-BLAST against PDB to find templates with significant sequence similarity.
2. **Sequence alignment** — Pairwise or multiple alignment (e.g., using ClustalW, MUSCLE).
3. **Backbone construction** — Copy the Cα coordinates of aligned residues from the template.
4. **Loop modeling** — Use databases of known loop conformations or *ab initio* loops for insertions/deletions.
5. **Side-chain modeling** — Use *rotamer libraries* (preferred side-chain conformations).
6. **Model refinement** — Apply energy minimization and short MD to relieve steric clashes.
7. **Validation** — Check stereochemistry, geometry, and biological plausibility.

**Limitations:**
- Requires **at least one known template** with detectable similarity.
- Accuracy **decreases sharply** for sequence identities <25%.
- **Loop regions** (especially >10 residues) are difficult to model accurately.
- Cannot reliably model **large conformational changes** between template and target.
- **Ligand-binding sites** often shift in subtle ways not captured by homology.

**Validation methods:**
- **Ramachandran plot** — check that backbone φ, ψ angles fall in allowed regions; >90% residues should be in core allowed regions.
- **Verify3D** — checks 3D–1D compatibility of each residue.
- **PROCHECK** — overall stereochemical quality.
- **ERRAT** — non-bonded contact analysis.
- **RMSD vs. experimental structure** (when available).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing σ and ε** in the LJ potential — σ is the *zero-crossing distance* (not the equilibrium), and ε is the *well depth* (not the equilibrium energy).
> 2. **Forgetting to mention timestep size** — students often write "MD steps" without specifying Δt ≈ 1–2 fs. This loses 1 mark easily.
> 3. **Skipping the integration derivation** — for 7-mark derivations, KTU expects at least 3–4 algebraic steps leading to the final integrator formula.
> 4. **Writing "RMSD" without definition** — it is $\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{N}\sum \vert r_{\text{model}} - r_{\text{ref}} \vert^2}$, not just "the deviation".
> 5. **Naming AMBER/CHARMM without explaining** — at least one line on *what they are* is needed (force fields used to compute biomolecular potential energy).
> 6. **No mention of validation** in homology modeling answers — KTU has repeatedly asked this in 2023 and 2024 papers; missing this costs 2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Revision List for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4**

- **Structural Bioinformatics** = computational study of 3D biomolecular structures and interactions.
- **Sequence → Structure → Function** is the central dogma.
- **Four levels of protein structure**: Primary (sequence) → Secondary (α/β motifs) → Tertiary (3D fold) → Quaternary (multi-subunit).
- **Stabilizing forces**: peptide bonds, H-bonds, hydrophobic effect, VdW, ionic, disulfide bridges.
- **Force field components**: $V_{\text{bond}}$, $V_{\text{angle}}$, $V_{\text{torsion}}$, $V_{\text{LJ}}$, $V_{\text{Coul}}$.
- **Lennard-Jones parameters**: σ = collision diameter, ε = well depth; $r_{\min} = 2^{1/6}\sigma$.
- **Coulomb's law** for biomolecules: $V = 332\,q_i q_j / (\varepsilon_r r)$ in kcal/mol (r in Å, q in *e*).
- **Homology modeling** requires ≥30% sequence identity; steps: template search → align → build → loops → side chains → refine.
- **Ab initio modeling** = from scratch (e.g., ROSETTA); no template needed; expensive.
- **Threading** = used when sequence identity is low but fold exists.
- **MD principle**: integrate Newton's $F = ma = -\nabla V$ for every atom.
- **Time step** Δt ≈ **1–2 fs**; total simulation ≈ 10–1000 ns.
- **Velocity Verlet** is the standard MD integrator (time-reversible, symplectic).
- **Ensembles**: NVE (constant energy), NVT (constant T via thermostat), NPT (constant T, P via barostat).
- **Berendsen**, **Nosé-Hoover** = common thermostats; **Parrinello-Rahman**, **Berendsen** = barostats.
- **Periodic Boundary Conditions (PBC)** simulate bulk solvent in a finite box.
- **Docking** predicts receptor–ligand binding; AutoDock Vina is widely used.
- **Scoring function** estimates ΔG_bind (H-bonds, hydrophobic, electrostatic contributions).
- **PDB** = Protein Data Bank (free repository of experimental 3D structures).
- **Visualization tools**: PyMOL, VMD, Chimera.
- **Validation tools**: Ramachandran plot, Verify3D, PROCHECK.
- **RMSD** measures structural deviation; lower = better model.
- **Applications**: drug design, vaccine design, enzyme engineering, mutation analysis.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Structural Bioinformatics**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Genomics and Proteomics: Computational Tools for Analyzing Genomic and Proteomic Data to Understand Gene Expression and Protein Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT636 - COMPUTATIONAL METHODS IN BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Genomics and Proteomics: Computational Tools for Analyzing Genomic and Proteomic Data to Understand Gene Expression and Protein Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Genomics — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Genomics** is the branch of molecular biology that focuses on the **structure, function, evolution, mapping, and editing of genomes** — the complete set of an organism's DNA, including all of its genes and their non-coding sequences. Computational genomics applies algorithmic, statistical, and machine-learning methods to analyze high-throughput sequencing data at scale.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, computational genomics is the **systematic application of algorithms, statistical models, and software pipelines** to interpret nucleotide sequences (A, T, G, C), identify genes, predict regulatory elements, compare genomes across species, and quantify gene-expression levels (RNA-Seq).

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library of Life" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a human cell as an enormous library containing **3.2 billion books** (the haploid human genome, ~**3.2 × 10⁹ base pairs**). Each book (chromosome) has thousands of chapters (genes). The library is written in a 4-letter alphabet (A, T, G, C). A bioinformatician is a librarian who has never read the books but must answer questions like: *Which chapters are being read right now? How do they differ from a different edition (another species or another person)? Are there any printing errors (mutations)?*

**Computational genomics** is the automated cataloguing, indexing, and search system this librarian uses — turning raw biological text into searchable, comparable, and biologically meaningful information.

## 1.2 Proteomics — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proteomics** is the large-scale study of the **entire set of proteins** expressed by a genome, including their structure, abundance, modifications, interactions, and functions, in a given cell, tissue, or organism at a given time. Computational proteomics uses mass-spectrometry data, sequence analysis, and structural-modeling tools to identify, quantify, and characterize proteins.

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Workforce of the Cell"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** If the genome is the recipe book, then **proteins are the cooks, waiters, builders, and messengers** that actually do the work in the kitchen (cell). Two cells with identical cookbooks (the same DNA) can produce **different protein menus** depending on what's being cooked today (gene expression). Proteomics is the **shift supervisor's logbook** that records: *Who is working? What are they doing? Are they communicating with each other? Have they been modified (phosphorylated, glycosylated)?*

## 1.3 Why Both? — The Central Dogma Bridge

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Central Dogma of Molecular Biology** (Crick, 1958) describes the flow of genetic information:
>
> **DNA → (transcription) → mRNA → (translation) → Protein**
>
> Genomics studies the **source code** (DNA/RNA); Proteomics studies the **executables** (proteins). Computational methods must bridge both — because mRNA abundance correlates **only partially (~40–70%)** with protein abundance (this gap is called the **post-transcriptional regulation gap**).

## 1.4 Key Quantitative Metrics in Genomics and Proteomics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Human) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Genome size (haploid) | $G$ | **3.2 × 10⁹** | base pairs (bp) |
| Number of protein-coding genes | $N_g$ | **~20,000–21,000** | genes |
| Number of chromosomes | $k$ | **23** (22 autosomes + XY) | chromosomes |
| Average gene length | $\bar{L}_g$ | **~10,000–15,000** | bp |
| Total proteome size | $P$ | **~20,000–30,000** distinct proteins | proteins |
| Mass spectrometer resolution | $R$ | **> 30,000** | m/Δm |
| GC content (human) | $\rho_{GC}$ | **~41%** | % |

## 1.5 High-Yield Definitions for KTU Board Exams

> [!NOTE]
> - **Gene Expression** — The process by which information encoded in a gene is used to synthesize a functional gene product (usually a protein). Measured in **Transcripts Per Million (TPM)** or **FPKM**.
> - **Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS)** — High-throughput parallel sequencing technology producing millions of short reads (typically **100–300 bp** each) from fragmented DNA/RNA.
> - **Mass Spectrometry (MS)** — An analytical technique measuring mass-to-charge ratio ($m/z$) of ionized molecules; the workhorse of proteomics.
> - **Sequence Alignment** — A way of arranging sequences (DNA, RNA, or protein) to identify regions of similarity that may indicate functional, structural, or evolutionary relationships.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Computational Workflow — Genomics Pipeline

The standard NGS data-analysis pipeline consists of the following six stages:

1. **Quality Control (QC)** — Raw reads in **FASTQ** format are assessed using tools like **FastQC**. Low-quality bases (Phred score $Q < 20$) and adapter sequences are trimmed using **Trimmomatic** or **Cutadapt**.
2. **Alignment / Mapping** — Reads are aligned to a reference genome using algorithms like **BWA (Burrows-Wheeler Aligner)**, **Bowtie2**, or **STAR** (for RNA-Seq). Output: **SAM/BAM** files.
3. **Variant Calling** — Genomic variants (**SNPs**, **indels**) are identified using **GATK** or **SAMtools/BCFtools**. Output: **VCF** files.
4. **Gene-Expression Quantification** — For RNA-Seq, counts of reads per gene are tallied using **HTSeq** or **featureCounts**.
5. **Differential Expression Analysis** — Statistical comparison between conditions (e.g., diseased vs. healthy) using **DESeq2**, **edgeR**, or **limma-voom**.
6. **Functional Annotation & Pathway Analysis** — Differentially expressed genes (DEGs) are mapped to biological pathways using **GO (Gene Ontology)**, **KEGG (Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes)**, and **Reactome**.

## 2.2 The Computational Workflow — Proteomics Pipeline

A standard shotgun (bottom-up) proteomics MS pipeline:

1. **Sample Preparation** — Proteins are extracted, digested with **trypsin** (cleaves at K, R residues), producing peptides.
2. **LC-MS/MS** — Peptides separated by **Liquid Chromatography (LC)** and analyzed by tandem Mass Spectrometry (**MS/MS**).
3. **Peptide Identification** — MS/MS spectra are matched to in-silico-digested protein databases using search engines like **Mascot**, **SEQUEST**, or **MaxQuant/Andromeda**.
4. **Protein Inference & Quantification** — Identified peptides are rolled up to proteins; quantification via **label-free** (intensity) or **isobaric-label** (TMT/iTRAQ) methods.
5. **Post-Translational Modification (PTM) Analysis** — Search for mass shifts corresponding to phosphorylation (+79.97 Da), ubiquitination (+114.04 Da), acetylation (+42.01 Da), etc.
6. **Protein-Protein Interaction (PPI) Network Analysis** — Constructed using **STRING**, **BioGRID**, or **IntAct** databases.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Why QC first?** NGS instruments report per-base quality as a **Phred score** $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P_e)$, where $P_e$ is the base-calling error probability. A $Q = 20$ read has a 1% error rate; $Q = 30$ = 0.1% error. Without trimming, downstream alignments propagate errors.
>
> **Why reference alignment?** Genomes are non-repetitive at the population level; aligning to a reference is computationally tractable ($O(n \log n)$ via BWT) versus de novo assembly ($O(n^2)$ or worse).
>
> **Why differential expression?** Two cells with identical DNA differ only in **which genes are "switched on"** (transcribed more). DEG analysis statistically distinguishes biological signal from technical noise.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are the **most-asked equations** in KTU board examinations for this module. Memorize the form, the variable definitions, and the units.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Phred Quality Score** | $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P_e)$ | $Q$: quality score (unitless); $P_e$: base-calling error probability |
| 2 | **GC Content** | $\rho_{GC} = \dfrac{n_G + n_C}{n_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $n_G, n_C$: count of G and C bases; $n_{total}$: total bases |
| 3 | **RPKM (Reads Per Kilobase per Million)** | $RPKM = \dfrac{r_g \cdot 10^9}{L_g \cdot N_R}$ | $r_g$: reads mapped to gene; $L_g$: gene length (bp); $N_R$: total mapped reads |
| 4 | **FPKM (Fragments Per Kilobase per Million)** | $FPKM = \dfrac{f_g \cdot 10^9}{L_g \cdot N_F}$ | $f_g$: fragments per gene; $L_g$: gene length; $N_F$: total fragments |
| 5 | **TPM (Transcripts Per Million)** | $TPM_i = \dfrac{x_i \,/\, \ell_i}{\sum_j \left(x_j \,/\, \ell_j\right)} \times 10^6$ | $x_i$: read count of gene $i$; $\ell_i$: gene length of gene $i$ (kb) |
| 6 | **Fold Change (Gene Expression)** | $FC = \dfrac{ETPR_{\text{treated}}}{ETPR_{\text{control}}}$ | ETPR: Expression-level Transcripts Per Read (or any normalized count) |
| 7 | **Log₂ Fold Change** | $\log_2 FC = \log_2 \left( \dfrac{T}{C} \right)$ | $T$: treated count; $C$: control count |
| 8 | **P-value (Bonferroni-corrected)** | $p_{\text{adj}} = p \cdot m$ | $m$: number of hypotheses (genes) tested |
| 9 | **FDR (Benjamini-Hochberg)** | $p_{(i)}^{\text{adj}} = \min \left( \dfrac{m \cdot p_{(i)}}{i}, \, 1 \right)$ | $p_{(i)}$: $i$-th smallest $p$-value; $m$: total tests |
| 10 | **Tanimoto Coefficient (Chemical Similarity)** | $T_{AB} = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert}$ | $A, B$: binary fingerprint bit-vectors of two molecules |
| 11 | **Peptide Mass (Monoisotopic, Residue Sum)** | $M_{\text{peptide}} = M_{\text{water}} + \sum_{i} M_{\text{res},i}$ | $M_{\text{water}} = 18.0106$ Da; $M_{\text{res},i}$: residue monoisotopic mass |
| 12 | **Smith-Waterman Recurrence** | $H_{i,j} = \max \begin{cases} H_{i-1,j-1} + s(a_i, b_j) \\ H_{i-1,j} - g \\ H_{i,j-1} - g \\ 0 \end{cases}$ | $s$: substitution score; $g$: gap penalty |
| 13 | **BLOSUM62 Identity Threshold** | $S_{ij} = \log_2 \left( \dfrac{p_{ij}}{q_i \, q_j} \right)$ | $p_{ij}$: observed pair frequency; $q_i, q_j$: background frequencies |
| 14 | **k-mer Frequency (k-tuple)** | $f_k = \dfrac{N_k}{N_{\text{reads}} \cdot (L - k + 1)}$ | $N_k$: occurrences of $k$-mer; $L$: read length |
| 15 | **Percent Identity (Alignment)** | $\%I = \dfrac{N_{\text{match}}}{N_{\text{cols}}} \times 100\%$ | $N_{\text{match}}$: matching columns; $N_{\text{cols}}$: aligned columns |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Biomedical Utility

> [!NOTE]
> These computational tools are **not academic curiosities** — they are frontline technology in:
> - **Personalized Medicine** — Identifying patient-specific cancer mutations to choose targeted therapy (e.g., **EGFR-mutant lung cancer → gefitinib**).
> - **Drug Discovery** — Virtual screening of millions of compounds against a protein structure (**structure-based drug design**).
> - **Vaccine Development** — mRNA vaccines (e.g., COVID-19) were designed by computationally predicting the **S-protein spike structure** and selecting optimal mRNA sequences for stability and translation efficiency.
> - **Forensic Genomics** — DNA fingerprinting for criminal identification and paternity testing.
> - **Agriculture** — Engineering drought/pest-resistant crops via **CRISPR-Cas9** guide-RNA design (computationally optimized).
> - **Synthetic Biology** — Designing minimal genomes (e.g., **JCVI-syn3.0** with only **473 genes**).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between RPKM, FPKM, and TPM

This is a **classic KTU derivation** because it tests whether students can manipulate transcript-level normalization formulas. All three are ways of normalizing RNA-Seq read counts so that expression levels are comparable **across genes of different lengths and across samples of different sequencing depths**.

### 3.1.1 Start: Define Raw Read Count

Let $r_i$ denote the **raw number of reads** mapped to gene $i$. Let $L_i$ denote the **length of gene $i$ in base pairs**. Let $N$ denote the **total number of reads** in the library (sequencing depth).

### 3.1.2 Step 1: Reads Per Kilobase (RPK)

The number of reads per **kilobase** of gene length is:

$$RPK_i = \frac{r_i}{L_i \,/\, 1000}$$

**Reasoning:** A longer gene naturally captures more reads simply because it has more positions for reads to map to. Dividing by $L_i$ (in kb) normalizes for gene length.

### 3.1.3 Step 2: RPKM (Reads Per Kilobase per Million mapped reads)

Now divide by library size to normalize for sequencing depth:

$$RPKM_i = \frac{RPK_i}{N \,/\, 10^6} = \frac{r_i \cdot 10^9}{L_i \cdot N}$$

**Reasoning:** A sample sequenced at higher depth ($N$ larger) will produce more reads for every gene. Dividing by $N$ (in millions) makes expression values comparable across samples.

### 3.1.4 Step 3: FPKM (Fragments Per Kilobase per Million)

For **paired-end sequencing**, two reads come from a single fragment. If $f_i$ is the **fragment count** (i.e., read pairs mapping to gene $i$), then:

$$FPKM_i = \frac{f_i \cdot 10^9}{L_i \cdot N_F}$$

where $N_F$ is the total fragment count. **FPKM = RPKM for single-end data; for paired-end, FPKM avoids double-counting.**

### 3.1.5 Step 4: TPM (Transcripts Per Million) — Derivation

TPM is the **most modern** normalization. It is computed in two stages.

**Stage 1 — Compute "reads per kilobase" (RPK) for each gene:**

$$RPK_i = \frac{r_i}{L_i \,/\, 1000}$$

**Stage 2 — Sum all RPK values and rescale so that all TPM values sum to $10^6$:**

The "scaling factor" is:

$$S = \frac{1}{\sum_{j=1}^{N_g} RPK_j} \cdot 10^6$$

Therefore, the TPM of gene $i$ is:

$$TPM_i = RPK_i \cdot S = \frac{RPK_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N_g} RPK_j} \cdot 10^6$$

Substituting the RPK formula:

$$TPM_i = \frac{\dfrac{r_i}{L_i / 1000}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N_g} \dfrac{r_j}{L_j / 1000}} \cdot 10^6$$

Simplifying (the factor 1000 cancels inside the ratio):

$$TPM_i = \frac{r_i \,/\, L_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N_g} \left( r_j \,/\, L_j \right)} \cdot 10^6$$

**Final boxed form:**

$$\boxed{\,TPM_i = \frac{x_i \,/\, \ell_i}{\sum_{j} \left( x_j \,/\, \ell_j \right)} \times 10^6\,}$$

where $x_i$ is the read count and $\ell_i$ is the gene length (kb). This is **Formula #5** in the KTU sheet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key difference between FPKM and TPM:** Both sum to $10^6$ per sample, but in **TPM the gene-length normalization happens FIRST** and the library-size normalization happens LAST. In FPKM, the order is reversed. TPM is preferred because the sum-per-sample is invariant — making cross-sample comparison more intuitive.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Phred Quality Score

### 3.2.1 Definition

The Phred score $Q$ is defined as the negative base-10 logarithm (in dB-like units) of the error probability $P_e$:

$$Q = -10 \log_{10}(P_e)$$

### 3.2.2 Derivation of the Equivalent Probability Form

Solve for $P_e$:

$$\frac{Q}{-10} = \log_{10}(P_e)$$

Exponentiate both sides with base 10:

$$P_e = 10^{\,Q \,/\, (-10)} = 10^{-Q/10}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_e = 10^{-Q/10}\,}$$

### 3.2.3 Numerical Examples

- $Q = 20 \Rightarrow P_e = 10^{-2} = 0.01$ → **1% error rate**
- $Q = 30 \Rightarrow P_e = 10^{-3} = 0.001$ → **0.1% error rate** (the standard threshold for "high quality")
- $Q = 40 \Rightarrow P_e = 10^{-4} = 0.0001$ → **0.01% error rate** (NGS standard)

### 3.2.4 Average Phred Score of a Read

If a read has $L$ bases with per-base qualities $Q_1, Q_2, \ldots, Q_L$, the **average error probability** is:

$$\bar{P}_e = \frac{1}{L} \sum_{k=1}^{L} 10^{-Q_k/10}$$

And the **average Phred score** of the read is:

$$\bar{Q} = -10 \log_{10}(\bar{P}_e)$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Computing TPM for Two Genes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample KTU Board Problem:** In an RNA-Seq experiment, a sample has the following data:
>
> | Gene | Length $L$ (bp) | Raw read count $r$ |
> |---|---|---|
> | GAPDH | 1,300 | 26,000 |
> | TP53 | 19,200 | 38,400 |
>
> Compute the TPM value for **TP53**. (Total genes considered = 2 for this simple case.)

**Step 1 — Convert gene lengths to kilobases:**

$$\ell_{\text{GAPDH}} = \frac{1300}{1000} = 1.3 \text{ kb}$$

$$\ell_{\text{TP53}} = \frac{19200}{1000} = 19.2 \text{ kb}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the ratio $r_i / \ell_i$ for each gene:**

$$\frac{r_{\text{GAPDH}}}{\ell_{\text{GAPDH}}} = \frac{26000}{1.3} = 20000 \text{ reads per kb}$$

$$\frac{r_{\text{TP53}}}{\ell_{\text{TP53}}} = \frac{38400}{19.2} = 2000 \text{ reads per kb}$$

**Step 3 — Sum all ratios:**

$$\sum_j \frac{r_j}{\ell_j} = 20000 + 2000 = 22000$$

**Step 4 — Compute TPM for TP53:**

$$TPM_{\text{TP53}} = \frac{2000}{22000} \times 10^6 = 0.0909 \times 10^6 \approx 90{,}909$$

**Step 5 — Compute TPM for GAPDH (for verification):**

$$TPM_{\text{GAPDH}} = \frac{20000}{22000} \times 10^6 \approx 909{,}091$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check: Sum of TPMs = $10^6$?**

$$909{,}091 + 90{,}909 = 1{,}000{,}000 \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Although TP53 has **more raw reads** (38,400 vs. 26,000), GAPDH has a **higher TPM** because it is much shorter. TPM corrects for this — a fair comparison of expression levels.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Computational Tool

| Analysis Goal | Genomics Tool | Proteomics Tool | Output Format |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quality control | FastQC, Trimmomatic | Scaffold viewer for raw MS | HTML report / mzML |
| Sequence/peptide matching | BWA, Bowtie2, STAR | SEQUEST, Mascot, Andromeda | SAM/BAM / pep.xml |
| Variant/SNP calling | GATK, SAMtools | — | VCF |
| Quantification | HTSeq, featureCounts | MaxQuant, Proteome Discoverer | Count matrix / protein list |
| Differential analysis | DESeq2, edgeR, limma | Perseus, MSstats | DEG list / volcano plot |
| Pathway enrichment | DAVID, clusterProfiler, GSEA | STRING, Cytoscape | Pathway / network figure |
| Structural modeling | — | AlphaFold2, Rosetta, I-TASSER | PDB file |
| Database search | UCSC, Ensembl, NCBI | UniProt, PRIDE, PeptideAtlas | Annotated entries |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Question Paper]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks): Define Genomics. List any four major applications of computational genomics in biomedical engineering.

**Model Answer:**

> **Genomics** is the comprehensive study of an organism's complete set of DNA (the genome), including the sequencing, mapping, functional analysis, and comparison of all genes and non-coding regions. Computational genomics uses **algorithms, statistical methods, and software pipelines** to analyze genome-scale data.
>
> **Four applications in biomedical engineering:**
> 1. **Disease-gene identification** — Genome-Wide Association Studies (GWAS) to find SNPs linked to diseases like diabetes, cancer, and Alzheimer's.
> 2. **Pharmacogenomics** — Predicting drug response (efficacy/toxicity) based on a patient's genetic profile; e.g., warfarin dosing via *VKORC1* and *CYP2C9* genotyping.
> 3. **Cancer genomics** — Identifying somatic mutations in tumor cells via tools like **GATK MuTect2** for personalized oncology.
> 4. **CRISPR guide-RNA design** — Computational selection of optimal sgRNA target sites for gene editing.

### Question 2 (3 Marks): Differentiate between Genomics and Proteomics.

**Model Answer:**

> | Aspect | Genomics | Proteomics |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Molecule studied** | DNA (and RNA) | Proteins |
> | **Primary data** | Nucleotide sequences (A, T, G, C) | Mass spectra ($m/z$ values) |
> | **Scale** | ~3.2 × 10⁹ bp (human) | ~20,000–30,000 proteins |
> | **Main technique** | Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS) | Mass Spectrometry (MS), LC-MS/MS |
> | **Output** | FASTA, FASTQ, BAM, VCF | mzML, pep.xml, protein list |
> | **Dynamic range** | Static (same DNA in all cells) | Dynamic (varies with time, tissue, condition) |
> | **Includes PTMs** | No | Yes (phosphorylation, glycosylation, etc.) |
>
> Genomics studies the **blueprint**; proteomics studies the **functional output** — including modifications, interactions, and abundance, which cannot be inferred from DNA/RNA alone.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern]**

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — **Option 1**

> Discuss the major computational tools and algorithms used in the analysis of genomic data. Describe the RNA-Seq pipeline in detail with reference to the following sub-parts:

#### Part (a) (7 Marks): NGS Quality Control and Read Alignment

**Model Answer:**

**Quality Control (QC):**
The first step in any NGS analysis is to assess the **per-base quality** of raw reads. Reads are stored in **FASTQ** format, where each base is accompanied by a Phred quality score $Q$:

$$Q = -10 \log_{10}(P_e)$$

The tool **FastQC** generates reports including:
- Per-base sequence quality
- GC content distribution
- Adapter contamination
- Sequence duplication levels
- Overrepresented sequences

Low-quality bases (typically $Q < 20$) and adapter sequences are trimmed using **Trimmomatic**, which performs:
- **Leading/trailing base removal** (sliding window from the 3′ end)
- **Sliding-window trimming** (window size 4 bp, mean $Q < 20$ removed)
- **Adapter clipping** (using known Illumina adapter sequences)

**Read Alignment:**
After QC, reads are aligned to a reference genome. The dominant algorithm is **BWA (Burrows-Wheeler Aligner)**, which uses the **Burrows-Wheeler Transform (BWT)** and **FM-index** to enable $O(n)$ lookup:

- **BWA-MEM** is used for reads of length **70 bp to 1 Mbp** and is the recommended algorithm for most modern applications.
- For RNA-Seq, **STAR (Spliced Transcripts Alignment to a Reference)** is preferred because it handles **splice junctions** (reads that span exon-exon boundaries) via a 2-pass strategy.

The output of alignment is a **SAM (Sequence Alignment/Map)** file, which is compressed to a sorted, indexed **BAM** file using **SAMtools**:

$$\text{SAM} \xrightarrow{\text{samtools sort}} \text{sorted BAM} \xrightarrow{\text{samtools index}} \text{.bai index file}$$

Alignment quality is assessed using metrics like:
- **Mapping rate** (% reads mapped; should be > 80% for DNA-seq, > 70% for RNA-seq)
- **Insert size distribution** (for paired-end)
- **Coverage depth** $C = \dfrac{N \cdot L}{G}$, where $N$ = read count, $L$ = read length, $G$ = genome size.

#### Part (b) (7 Marks): Variant Calling and Differential Expression

**Model Answer:**

**Variant Calling:**
Once reads are aligned, the next step is to identify positions where the sample differs from the reference. The **GATK (Genome Analysis Toolkit) Best Practices** workflow is:

1. **Mark Duplicates** — PCR/optical duplicates are flagged using `MarkDuplicates`.
2. **Base Quality Score Recalibration (BQSR)** — Corrects systematic errors in quality scores.
3. **Variant Calling** — `HaplotypeCaller` (germline) or `Mutect2` (somatic/cancer) generates a **VCF (Variant Call Format)** file listing each variant with quality, depth, and genotype information.

For **RNA-Seq**, GATK's **SplitNCigarReads** tool is used first to handle reads spanning introns.

**Differential Expression Analysis:**
RNA-Seq allows quantification of gene expression. The pipeline is:

1. **Read Counting** — Tools like **featureCounts** or **HTSeq-count** assign aligned reads to genes using a GTF annotation file. Output is a **count matrix** ($N_g \times N_s$, where $N_g$ = genes, $N_s$ = samples).
2. **Normalization** — Raw counts are normalized using **DESeq2's median-of-ratios** approach:

$$\text{Normalized count}_{ij} = \frac{c_{ij}}{\text{size factor}_i}$$

where the size factor for sample $i$ is:

$$s_i = \text{median}_g \left( \frac{c_{gi}}{\left(\prod_{k=1}^{N_s} c_{gk}\right)^{1/N_s}} \right)$$

3. **Statistical Testing** — DESeq2 uses a **negative binomial generalized linear model** to test for differential expression. The output is a list of DEGs with:
   - **log₂ Fold Change** (magnitude of change)
   - **p-value** (from the Wald test)
   - **Adjusted p-value (FDR)** via Benjamini-Hochberg: $p_{(i)}^{\text{adj}} = \dfrac{m \cdot p_{(i)}}{i}$

A gene is considered differentially expressed if $\vert \log_2 FC \vert \geq 1$ and $p_{\text{adj}} < 0.05$.

4. **Visualization** — **Volcano plot** ($\log_2 FC$ vs. $-\log_{10} p_{\text{adj}}$), **MA plot**, and **heatmaps** of top DEGs.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — **Option 2**

> Explain the role of computational tools in proteomic data analysis. With suitable diagrams, describe the mass spectrometry-based proteomics workflow and protein database searching.

#### Part (a) (7 Marks): Mass Spectrometry Workflow

**Model Answer:**

**Shotgun (Bottom-Up) Proteomics** is the standard approach. The steps are:

1. **Sample Preparation:**
   - Proteins are extracted from cells/tissue using lysis buffer.
   - Disulfide bonds are reduced (with **DTT**) and alkylated (with **IAA / iodoacetamide**).
   - Proteins are digested with **trypsin** (cleaves C-terminal to Lys and Arg, except before Pro).
   - The resulting **peptide mixture** is desalted and dried.

2. **Liquid Chromatography (LC):**
   - Peptides are separated on a reverse-phase C18 column with an acetonitrile gradient.
   - This reduces complexity: only a small fraction of peptides enters the MS at any time.

3. **Mass Spectrometry (MS/MS):**
   - **First MS (MS1)**: Peptide ions are measured by their mass-to-charge ratio ($m/z$). Each peak ideally corresponds to one peptide.
   - **Peptide ions** are then selected (one at a time) and fragmented by **Collision-Induced Dissociation (CID)** or **Higher-Energy Collisional Dissociation (HCD)**.
   - **Second MS (MS2)**: Fragment ions (b- and y-ions) are measured. The pattern of b- and y-ions reveals the **peptide's amino-acid sequence**.

4. **Output:** A **mzML** file containing thousands of MS1 and MS2 spectra per run.

**Why two stages?** MS1 gives **peptide mass**; MS2 gives **fragment pattern**. The combination (precursor mass + fragments) is far more specific than either alone — this is what enables confident peptide identification.

#### Part (b) (7 Marks): Protein Database Searching and Functional Analysis

**Model Answer:**

**Database Search Algorithms:**
The most widely used search engines are **SEQUEST**, **Mascot**, **Andromeda (MaxQuant)**, and **MS-GF+**. The procedure is:

1. **In-Silico Digestion:** The protein database (e.g., **UniProtKB**, ~250 million entries) is digested in silico with the same enzyme (trypsin) used in the lab. This generates a list of all possible peptides and their theoretical masses.
2. **Spectrum Matching:** Each experimental MS2 spectrum is compared to the theoretical fragmentation pattern of each candidate peptide whose mass matches the precursor $m/z$ (within a tolerance, e.g., **±10 ppm**).
3. **Scoring:** A score is computed. Common metrics:
   - **Mascot Ion Score** — based on probability: $S = -10 \log_{10}(P)$
   - **SEQUEST XCorr** — cross-correlation between experimental and theoretical spectra
   - **Andromeda Score** — based on a posterior error probability (PEP)
4. **False Discovery Rate (FDR) Control:** Matches are filtered to keep FDR ≤ **1%** (using a **target-decoy** strategy: searching against a decoy database of reversed sequences to estimate false matches).
5. **Protein Inference:** Identified peptides are grouped into **protein groups** (handling shared peptides using parsimony — Occam's razor).

**Functional Analysis Tools:**
- **Gene Ontology (GO) Enrichment** — Categorizes proteins into Biological Process, Molecular Function, Cellular Component.
- **KEGG Pathway Mapping** — Identifies pathways over-represented among identified proteins.
- **PPI Networks (STRING)** — Builds protein-protein interaction networks from identified proteins; cluster analysis reveals functional modules.
- **AlphaFold2** — Predicts 3D protein structure from amino-acid sequence with near-experimental accuracy.

**Cross-Omics Integration:** Modern studies correlate mRNA levels (from RNA-Seq) with protein abundance (from MS) to identify **post-transcriptional regulation** and validate biomarkers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in this module:**
> 1. **Confusing RPKM, FPKM, and TPM** — These are NOT interchangeable. TPM and FPKM both sum to $10^6$ per sample, but the **order of normalization differs**. KTU examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Writing `|x|` in markdown tables** — Always use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX to avoid table-breaking.
> 3. **Forgetting the +1 (water) term in peptide mass** — The formula is $M_{\text{peptide}} = M_{\text{water}} + \sum_i M_{\text{res},i}$, where $M_{\text{water}} = 18.0106$ Da. Students commonly drop this.
> 4. **Confusing Phred $Q$ with the error probability** — Remember $Q$ is **logarithmic**: $Q = 30$ means 0.1% error, NOT 30% error.
> 5. **Mixing up FASTQ → BAM → VCF pipeline** — A question may ask "what is the output of step 3?" If you confuse VCF with BAM, you lose 7 marks instantly.
> 6. **Not annotating figures in essay answers** — Always add a brief caption ("Figure 1: RNA-Seq differential expression pipeline") and a sentence interpreting each block.
> 7. **Citing imaginary tools** — KTU examiners may deduct marks for invented software names. Stick to **BWA, STAR, GATK, DESeq2, MaxQuant, Mascot, BLAST, ClustalW, AlphaFold2**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- ⭐ **Genomics** = study of the **complete DNA/RNA content** of an organism. **Proteomics** = study of the **complete protein complement**. They are linked via the **Central Dogma**: DNA → mRNA → Protein.
- ⭐ The **human genome** has **~3.2 × 10⁹ bp** and **~20,000–21,000 protein-coding genes** on **23 chromosomes**.
- ⭐ **Phred score formula**: $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P_e)$. $Q = 20$ → 1% error. $Q = 30$ → 0.1% error (the standard threshold).
- ⭐ **NGS pipeline stages**: QC (FastQC + Trimmomatic) → Alignment (BWA/STAR) → Variant Calling (GATK) → Quantification (HTSeq/featureCounts) → DEG Analysis (DESeq2) → Functional Enrichment (GO/KEGG).
- ⭐ **MS Proteomics pipeline**: Digestion (trypsin) → LC separation → MS1 → fragmentation → MS2 → Database search (Mascot/MaxQuant) → FDR ≤ 1% → Protein inference.
- ⭐ **Trypsin** cleaves at K and R (except before P). The peptide mass formula includes a +$M_{\text{water}} = 18.0106$ Da term.
- ⭐ **TPM is preferred over FPKM** because length-normalization is applied first, making cross-sample comparison more intuitive. The sum of TPMs in a sample always equals **$10^6$**.
- ⭐ **Smith-Waterman** is a local alignment algorithm with $O(n^2)$ time complexity. The recurrence uses substitution scores $s(a_i, b_j)$ and gap penalty $g$.
- ⭐ **BLOSUM62** is a substitution matrix derived from protein blocks with ≥ 62% sequence identity. The formula is $S_{ij} = \log_2 \left( \dfrac{p_{ij}}{q_i q_j} \right)$.
- ⭐ **FDR control** is achieved via **Benjamini-Hochberg**: $p_{(i)}^{\text{adj}} = \min \left( \dfrac{m \cdot p_{(i)}}{i}, 1 \right)$.
- ⭐ **Modern tools to remember** — BWA, STAR, GATK, DESeq2, edgeR, MaxQuant, Mascot, STRING, KEGG, DAVID, AlphaFold2, BLAST, ClustalW, Bowtie2.
- ⭐ **Output file formats** — FASTQ (raw reads), BAM (aligned reads), VCF (variants), mzML (mass spectra), PDB (protein structures), GTF (gene annotations).
- ⭐ **Real-world applications** — Personalized medicine, cancer genomics, vaccine design, CRISPR guide-RNA design, drug discovery, forensic DNA, agricultural biotechnology.
- ⭐ **Key databases** — NCBI GenBank, Ensembl, UCSC Genome Browser, UniProt, PDB, STRING, KEGG, Reactome, GeneCards.
